slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

# Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) Power BI & Microsoft Fabric Data Analytics Platform Design

# 2.0 Executive Summary

UMS has embarked on a strategic initiative to modernize its data platform, aiming to enhance business intelligence, analytics, and decision-making capabilities. This Solution Design Document outlines the architectural approach for implementing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, ensuring alignment with UMS’s business objectives and digital transformation roadmap.

The primary objective of this project is to establish a robust, scalable, and secure data workflow—from ingestion through transformation to visualization—leveraging Microsoft Fabric’s unified analytics platform. The scope encompasses the creation of a dedicated Fabric workspace, development of end-to-end ETL pipelines, integration of advanced machine learning processes, and seamless incorporation of Power BI for actionable business insights.

## 2.1 Project Objectives

UMS shall implement the following core capabilities:
- **Data Integration and Orchestration:** Utilizing Fabric Data Factory, UMS will automate and streamline the ingestion of disparate data sources, ensuring data consistency and reliability across the enterprise.
- **Advanced Analytics and Big Data Processing:** Fabric Synapse will enable high-performance analytics, supporting complex data transformations and scalable big data solutions tailored to UMS’s operational requirements.
- **Machine Learning Application:** The Data Science module within Microsoft Fabric shall facilitate predictive analytics and AI-driven insights, empowering UMS to leverage data for proactive business strategies.
- **Business-Aligned Analytics:** Power BI will serve as the visualization layer, delivering intuitive dashboards and reports that drive informed decision-making and foster a data-driven culture.

## 2.2 Strategic Alignment

This solution is meticulously aligned with UMS’s cloud adoption strategy and business imperatives, including:
- **Operational Efficiency:** By consolidating data management and analytics within Microsoft Fabric, UMS will reduce manual processes, minimize data silos, and accelerate time-to-insight.
- **Scalability and Flexibility:** The architecture supports future growth, enabling UMS to scale analytics workloads and adapt to evolving business needs without compromising performance or security.
- **Governance and Compliance:** Microsoft Fabric’s governance framework ensures data privacy, regulatory compliance, and consistent policy enforcement, mitigating risks associated with data management.

## 2.3 Rationale for Microsoft Fabric and Power BI Adoption

The selection of Microsoft Fabric and Power BI is grounded in industry best practices and UMS’s requirements for a unified, enterprise-grade analytics platform. Key architectural pillars underpinning this decision include:
- **Integrated Platform:** Fabric provides a seamless environment for data integration, analytics, and machine learning, reducing complexity and operational overhead.
- **Security and Governance:** Built-in security controls, role-based access, and audit capabilities ensure robust protection of sensitive data and adherence to governance standards.
- **Business Value:** Power BI’s intuitive interface and deep integration with Fabric empower business users to derive actionable insights, fostering agility and innovation.

## 2.4 Expected Business Outcomes

UMS anticipates the following outcomes from the proposed solution:
- Enhanced data-driven decision-making across all business units
- Improved agility and responsiveness to market dynamics
- Streamlined data workflows and reduced operational costs
- Increased reliability, scalability, and security of analytics infrastructure

## 2.5 Value Proposition

By adopting Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, UMS will realize significant value, including:
- **Accelerated Analytics:** Rapid deployment and access to insights, supporting timely business decisions.
- **Unified Data Estate:** Centralized management of data assets, simplifying governance and reducing fragmentation.
- **Future-Proof Architecture:** Extensible platform that supports advanced analytics, machine learning, and evolving business requirements.

In summary, this Solution Design Document presents a comprehensive, best-practice-driven approach to modernizing UMS’s data and analytics landscape, delivering measurable business value and strategic advantage through Microsoft Fabric and Power BI.

# 3.0 Solution Overview

The proposed solution architecture for UMS leverages Microsoft Fabric’s unified analytics platform to deliver a robust, scalable, and secure end-to-end data lifecycle management environment. This solution integrates Fabric Data Factory, Synapse, Data Science, and Power BI to orchestrate data ingestion, transformation, analytics, and visualization, ensuring business-aligned insights and operational excellence. The architecture follows Microsoft Best Practices, emphasizing modularity, governance, and extensibility to support evolving business requirements.

## 3.1 Solution Architecture Components

UMS shall implement the following core components within the Microsoft Fabric ecosystem:

- **Fabric Data Factory**: Serves as the primary data integration and orchestration engine. It enables seamless ingestion of data from heterogeneous sources, including the UMS Staging Area (Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse). Data Factory manages ETL workflows, ensuring data is reliably transferred and transformed across processing layers.
  
- **Fabric Synapse**: Provides advanced analytics, big data processing, and scalable compute. Synapse is responsible for transforming and aggregating data within the Bronze, Silver, and Gold processing layers, facilitating efficient data modeling and preparation for downstream analytics.

- **Data Science**: Integrates machine learning capabilities directly into the data pipeline. Data Science tools are applied to cleansed data (Silver Layer), enabling predictive analytics, anomaly detection, and advanced business intelligence scenarios.

- **Power BI**: Delivers interactive data visualization and reporting. Power BI connects to refined data (Gold Layer) via OneLake, empowering business users to explore, analyze, and share insights securely and efficiently.

- **OneLake**: Acts as a unified data storage layer, providing seamless access and data sharing across all components. OneLake ensures data consistency, governance, and performance by centralizing storage and enabling direct connectivity to analytics and visualization tools.

- **Identity Management (Identity_SM)**: Ensures robust access control and governance throughout the data lifecycle. Identity_SM integrates with Azure Active Directory to enforce role-based access, audit trails, and compliance with organizational security policies.

## 3.2 High-Level Data Workflow

The solution architecture is designed to support the complete data lifecycle, from ingestion to actionable insights. The workflow is as follows:

1. **Data Ingestion**  
   Data originates from the UMS Staging Area, specifically the Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse. Fabric Data Factory orchestrates the extraction and loading of data into the Bronze Layer (XX_Bronze_LK Tables), serving as the raw landing zone.

2. **Data Processing and Transformation**  
   Data Factory and Synapse collaboratively process data through the Silver Layer (XX_Silver_LK Tables), where cleansing, normalization, and enrichment occur. Data Science tools are applied at this stage for machine learning and advanced analytics.

3. **Data Refinement**  
   Processed data is promoted to the Gold Layer (XX_Gold_LK Tables), representing business-ready datasets optimized for reporting and analytics.

4. **Unified Storage and Access**  
   All data layers are stored in OneLake, ensuring a single source of truth and enabling direct access by authorized services and users.

5. **Visualization and Analytics**  
   Power BI connects to the Gold Layer via OneLake, providing interactive dashboards, reports, and self-service analytics capabilities to business stakeholders.

6. **Identity and Governance**  
   Identity_SM enforces access control and governance policies, ensuring that only authorized users can access sensitive data and analytics resources.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_009_img_003.png)

## 3.3 End-to-End Data Lifecycle Management

The solution is architected to support comprehensive data lifecycle management, adhering to Microsoft Best Practices:

- **Data Governance**: Centralized identity and access management via Azure Active Directory and Identity_SM ensure compliance, auditability, and data security. Data lineage is maintained throughout the ETL process, enabling traceability and accountability.
- **Scalability and Performance**: Fabric Synapse and Data Factory provide elastic compute and parallel processing capabilities, supporting high-volume data workloads and future growth.
- **Extensibility**: Modular architecture allows for easy integration of new data sources, analytics models, and visualization tools, aligning with UMS’s evolving business needs.
- **Security**: End-to-end encryption, role-based access control, and audit logging are enforced across all components, mitigating risks and supporting regulatory compliance.
- **Operational Efficiency**: Automated ETL pipelines and unified storage reduce manual intervention, minimize errors, and accelerate time-to-insight.

## 3.4 Integration and Interoperability

The seamless integration of Fabric Data Factory, Synapse, Data Science, and Power BI within the Microsoft Fabric workspace ensures interoperability and consistent data flow. OneLake serves as the backbone, enabling direct connectivity and data sharing across all services. This unified approach eliminates data silos, enhances collaboration, and empowers UMS to derive actionable insights from its enterprise data assets.

In summary, the proposed solution architecture delivers a secure, scalable, and governed platform for end-to-end data lifecycle management, enabling UMS to unlock the full value of its data through advanced analytics and business-aligned visualization.

# 4.0 Infrastructure and Networking Architecture

This section delineates the infrastructure and network architecture underpinning the UMS solution, focusing on Azure Resource Groups, the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) deployment, virtual machine sizing, and secure connectivity to Microsoft cloud services. All architectural decisions are guided by Microsoft best practices, with rigorous attention to governance, security, compliance, and operational limitations.

## 4.1 Azure Resource Groups and Governance

UMS shall implement Azure Resource Groups as the fundamental organizational construct for managing and securing cloud resources. Resource Groups act as logical containers, facilitating unified lifecycle management, access control, and policy enforcement. The primary Resource Group, designated as `Fabric-RG`, will encapsulate all infrastructure components related to the UMS Fabric, including VMs, networking objects, and associated services.

### Governance and Compliance

We recommend the following governance strategies in alignment with Microsoft best practices:

- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** RBAC must be enforced at the Resource Group level, ensuring that only authorized personnel can provision, modify, or delete resources. Access assignments shall be audited regularly.
- **Resource Locks:** Critical resources (e.g., OPDG VM, NSGs) shall be protected with resource locks to prevent accidental deletion or modification.
- **Tagging:** All resources must be tagged with metadata (e.g., environment, owner, cost center) to facilitate cost tracking and operational management.
- **Policy Enforcement:** Azure Policy shall be utilized to enforce compliance requirements, such as restricting VM sizes, enforcing encryption, and preventing public IP assignments unless explicitly required.

### Naming Conventions

To ensure clarity, consistency, and ease of management, the following Azure naming conventions shall be adopted:

| Resource Type         | Naming Convention Example           | Description                          |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Resource Group       | ums-fabric-rg                       | Environment prefix + function + rg   |
| Virtual Machine      | ums-opdg-vm-01                      | Environment prefix + service + vm + index |
| Network Security Group| ums-fabric-nsg                      | Environment prefix + function + nsg  |
| Virtual Network      | ums-fabric-vnet                     | Environment prefix + function + vnet |
| Managed Identity     | ums-fabric-mi                       | Environment prefix + function + mi   |

## 4.2 On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) Role and Architecture

The On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) is a pivotal architectural component, acting as a secure bridge between the UMS Staging Area (Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse) and Microsoft Fabric. The OPDG enables seamless, secure data transfer without exposing inbound ports, adhering to Microsoft’s zero-trust networking principles.

### Architectural Overview

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_011_img_004.png)

- **Data Flow:** The OPDG is installed on a dedicated VM within the `Fabric-RG`. It establishes outbound, encrypted connections to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints, ensuring data integrity and confidentiality.
- **Security:** No inbound ports are exposed on the OPDG VM. All communication is initiated outbound, leveraging SSL/TLS encryption.
- **Reliability:** The OPDG supports automatic failover and load balancing when deployed in clusters, though the current design specifies a single VM for simplicity.

## 4.3 Virtual Machine Sizing and Specifications

UMS shall provision the OPDG VM in accordance with Microsoft’s recommended sizing for optimal performance and reliability. The VM must meet or exceed the following specifications:

| Component        | Specification                                   |
|------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| .NET Framework   | Version 4.8                                     |
| vCPU             | 8 cores                                         |
| RAM              | Minimum 8 GB                                    |

### Rationale

- **CPU & Memory:** The specified resources ensure the gateway can handle concurrent data transfers and authentication requests without performance degradation.
- **Operating System:** Only supported OS versions are permitted, ensuring compatibility and security patching.
- **.NET Framework:** Version 4.8 is required for the OPDG agent, guaranteeing stability and support.

## 4.4 Network Connectivity and Security

### Outbound Security Rules

To facilitate secure communication between the OPDG VM and Microsoft cloud services, UMS shall implement the following outbound network security rules via Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs):

| Name                     | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                       | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|--------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services| 130      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication| 140      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443              | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160   | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                    | 80               | TCP      | Allow  |

#### Best Practices

- **Principle of Least Privilege:** Only essential outbound rules are permitted; all other traffic is denied by default.
- **Logging:** NSG flow logs must be enabled and archived for audit and troubleshooting purposes.
- **No Inbound Exposure:** The OPDG VM shall never expose inbound ports, eliminating attack surface.

### Limitations

- **Private Link Constraints:** The OPDG does not currently support Azure Private Link for direct, private connectivity to Microsoft Fabric endpoints. All communication must traverse the public internet, albeit securely via SSL/TLS.
- **Service Endpoint Restrictions:** Only whitelisted Microsoft domains are permitted for outbound connectivity; any deviation requires explicit security review.

## 4.5 Monitoring, Logging, and Compliance

- **Azure Monitor:** All infrastructure components shall be integrated with Azure Monitor for real-time health and performance tracking.
- **Log Analytics:** Security and operational logs must be ingested into Azure Log Analytics for centralized analysis and alerting.
- **Compliance Auditing:** All access and configuration changes are subject to periodic compliance audits, ensuring adherence to organizational and regulatory requirements.

## 4.6 Summary

The UMS infrastructure and networking architecture is meticulously designed to maximize security, performance, and operational governance. By adhering to Microsoft best practices, enforcing rigid outbound-only security rules, and deploying the OPDG with robust VM specifications, UMS ensures secure, compliant, and scalable connectivity between on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric. All architectural choices are governed by strict naming conventions, policy enforcement, and continuous monitoring to mitigate risks and support future expansion.

# 4.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Infrastructure & Networking

UMS shall implement infrastructure and networking components in strict accordance with Microsoft’s published best practices to ensure security, operational integrity, and future scalability. This section details authoritative recommendations for virtual machine (VM) hardening, least privilege network access, secure gateway deployment, and conditional access controls, referencing official Microsoft documentation where applicable.

## 4.1.1 Virtual Machine Hardening

Microsoft recommends a comprehensive approach to VM hardening, which encompasses operating system configuration, patch management, and access control. UMS shall adhere to the following:

- **Operating System Selection and Updates**: Deploy only supported and up-to-date operating systems, such as Windows Server 2019 or Windows 10 (64-bit). Regularly apply security patches and cumulative updates via Azure Update Management or Windows Update for Business. This mitigates vulnerabilities and aligns with [Microsoft Security Baselines](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/).
- **Configuration Management**: Utilize Azure Policy and Desired State Configuration (DSC) to enforce baseline configurations, including disabling unnecessary services, enforcing password complexity, and restricting local administrator accounts.
- **Endpoint Protection**: Install Microsoft Defender for Endpoint and enable real-time threat detection, malware scanning, and automated remediation. Integrate with Azure Security Center for continuous monitoring and security score improvement.
- **Resource Isolation**: Deploy VMs in dedicated subnets and resource groups, ensuring separation of workloads and minimizing blast radius in case of compromise.

### VM Sizing & Specification

UMS shall provision VMs based on Microsoft’s recommended sizing for gateway and data processing workloads:

| Component                 | Specification                              |
|---------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Operating System          | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 |
| .NET Framework            | Version 4.8                                |
| vCPU                      | 8 cores                                    |
| RAM                       | Minimum 8 GB                               |

## 4.1.2 Least Privilege Network Access

Microsoft’s principle of least privilege is foundational to secure network design. UMS shall implement the following:

- **Network Security Groups (NSGs)**: Restrict inbound and outbound traffic to only essential ports and endpoints. Default deny-all policies shall be enforced, with explicit allow rules for required services.
- **Segmentation**: Place VMs, gateways, and sensitive resources in isolated subnets. Use Azure Virtual Network (VNet) peering and service endpoints to limit exposure.
- **Outbound Rules**: Only permit outbound traffic necessary for operation, especially for gateway VMs connecting to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints. All other outbound traffic shall be blocked.

### Outbound Security Rules

| Name                       | Priority | Source                 | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                      | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|----------------------------|----------|------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services  | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication  | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160     | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                   | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

Refer to [Azure Networking Best Practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/networking/network-security-best-practices) for further guidance.

## 4.1.3 Secure Gateway Deployment

UMS shall deploy the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) in a hardened VM, acting as a secure bridge between on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric. Microsoft recommends:

- **Outbound-Only Connectivity**: The OPDG establishes outbound connections to Microsoft cloud endpoints, eliminating the need for inbound ports and reducing attack surface.
- **Authentication Integration**: The gateway authenticates using Azure Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), ensuring secure token-based access and compliance with identity governance.
- **Monitoring and Health Checks**: Enable gateway monitoring via Azure Monitor and configure alerts for connectivity failures, authentication issues, and anomalous activity.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_011_img_004.png)

Refer to [On-Premises Data Gateway Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/data-integration/gateway/service-gateway-install) for installation and operational details.

## 4.1.4 Conditional Access & Identity Governance

Microsoft advocates for robust identity governance and conditional access policies to secure user and system access to cloud resources:

- **Conditional Access Policies**: UMS shall configure Azure Entra conditional access to enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA), location-based restrictions, and device compliance checks. This ensures only authorized users and devices gain access to sensitive resources.
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**: Assign users and service principals the minimum required permissions, following the principle of least privilege. Regularly review and audit role assignments.
- **External User Management**: Secure external user access via guest accounts, conditional access, and monitoring. All access attempts shall be logged and reviewed for compliance.

Refer to [Conditional Access Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional-access/overview) and [RBAC Best Practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/best-practices).

## 4.1.5 Governance, Limitations, and Naming Conventions

### Governance

UMS shall implement governance controls using Azure Policy, Management Groups, and tagging standards. Policies will enforce resource configuration, location restrictions, and cost management.

### Limitations

- **Gateway Throughput**: The OPDG may be subject to throughput limitations based on VM sizing and network bandwidth. Monitor and scale resources as needed.
- **Conditional Access Complexity**: Overly restrictive policies may impede legitimate access; balance security with usability.

### Azure Naming Conventions

UMS shall adopt Microsoft-recommended naming conventions for clarity and manageability:

| Resource Type      | Naming Pattern           | Example                |
|--------------------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| Resource Group     | [Project]-RG-[Env]      | UMS-RG-Prod            |
| Virtual Machine    | [Project]-VM-[Role]-[Env]| UMS-VM-Gateway-Prod    |
| Network Security Group | [Project]-NSG-[Env] | UMS-NSG-Prod           |
| Subnet             | [Project]-Subnet-[Role] | UMS-Subnet-Gateway     |

Refer to [Azure Naming Guidelines](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/ready/azure-best-practices/naming-and-tagging) for further details.

---

By rigorously applying these Microsoft best practices, UMS will achieve a secure, compliant, and resilient infrastructure foundation, enabling safe connectivity and robust data integration with Microsoft Fabric and related services.

# 4.2 Governance & Compliance for Infrastructure

## 4.2.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a comprehensive governance and compliance framework for all infrastructure components, in strict alignment with Microsoft Best Practices. This framework ensures that every resource deployed within Azure adheres to organizational policies, regulatory mandates, and industry standards. Governance controls are essential for maintaining operational integrity, minimizing risk, and ensuring that infrastructure remains auditable and secure throughout its lifecycle.

## 4.2.2 Resource Tagging

Resource tagging is a foundational governance mechanism in Azure. We recommend that UMS enforce mandatory tagging policies for all infrastructure resources, including virtual machines, storage accounts, network interfaces, and resource groups. Tags provide critical metadata that supports cost management, operational ownership, environment classification, and lifecycle tracking.

### Tagging Policy and Enforcement

UMS shall implement Azure Policy to enforce tagging standards, ensuring that every resource is tagged at creation and throughout its lifecycle. Typical tags include:

| Tag Key         | Tag Value Example     | Purpose                                     |
|-----------------|----------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Environment     | Production, Dev, QA   | Identifies operational environment          |
| Owner           | Team Name, User ID    | Assigns responsibility for resource         |
| CostCenter      | FIN-001, IT-002       | Enables chargeback and cost tracking        |
| Application     | UMS-Fabric, OPDG      | Maps resource to business application       |
| Compliance      | GDPR, ISO27001        | Flags resources subject to regulatory review|

Tags facilitate automated reporting, resource grouping, and policy enforcement. Azure Policy shall be configured to deny resource creation if required tags are missing, ensuring compliance from the outset.

## 4.2.3 Audit Logging

Audit logging is critical for traceability, accountability, and forensic analysis. UMS shall configure Azure Activity Logs and Azure Monitor to capture all management operations, resource changes, and access events across the infrastructure. Logs are retained in accordance with organizational and regulatory requirements, typically for a minimum of 90 days, with options for extended retention in Azure Log Analytics or Azure Sentinel.

### Logging Architecture

- **Azure Activity Logs**: Captures all write, delete, and action operations at the subscription and resource group level.
- **Resource Diagnostic Logs**: Enables granular logging for individual resources, including VMs, network security groups, and storage accounts.
- **Centralized Log Storage**: Logs are aggregated in a secure Log Analytics workspace, enabling query, alerting, and dashboarding.
- **Immutable Storage**: For regulatory compliance, logs may be stored in immutable Azure Blob Storage with Write Once Read Many (WORM) policies.

Audit logs are reviewed regularly by security operations personnel, and automated alerts are configured for anomalous activities, such as unauthorized access attempts or policy violations.

## 4.2.4 Compliance with Organizational and Regulatory Requirements

UMS infrastructure must comply with both internal governance standards and external regulatory requirements, such as GDPR, ISO27001, and local data protection laws. Compliance controls are enforced through Azure Policy, Blueprints, and automated resource configuration checks.

### Compliance Controls

- **Azure Policy**: Enforces resource configuration standards, including encryption, network isolation, and allowed locations.
- **Azure Blueprints**: Provides repeatable templates for deploying compliant environments, ensuring that every deployment adheres to baseline controls.
- **Regulatory Mapping**: Resources are tagged and cataloged for regulatory scope, enabling targeted compliance reviews and audits.
- **Periodic Assessments**: Compliance posture is assessed quarterly using Azure Security Center and Microsoft Purview, with remediation plans for any gaps identified.

## 4.2.5 Outbound Connectivity Monitoring and Control

Outbound connectivity from infrastructure components, particularly the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine, is tightly controlled and monitored to prevent unauthorized data exfiltration and maintain compliance with Azure networking best practices.

### Outbound Security Rules

UMS shall implement explicit outbound network rules within Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) to restrict traffic to only approved Microsoft endpoints. The following table details the outbound rules enforced for OPDG VM connectivity:

| Name                       | Priority | Source                | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                 | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|----------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services  | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *          | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication  | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *          | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test | 160    | VM Private IP Addresses | *          | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                            | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

Outbound traffic is monitored using Azure Network Watcher and NSG Flow Logs, which are integrated into Azure Monitor for real-time visibility and alerting. Any deviation from approved endpoints triggers automated incident response workflows.

### Rationale

Restricting outbound connectivity is a Microsoft-recommended security practice that minimizes attack surface and ensures that only sanctioned communications occur. This approach prevents unauthorized access to external networks and supports compliance with data protection and audit requirements.

## 4.2.6 Conditional Access and Identity Governance

Identity governance is enforced through Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policies, which regulate access to resources based on user identity, device compliance, and network location. This ensures that only authenticated and authorized users can interact with critical infrastructure components, including the UMS Fabric and associated Azure resources.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_002.png)

### Policy Enforcement

- **User Authentication**: All access attempts are evaluated against conditional access policies, requiring multi-factor authentication and device compliance.
- **Signal Collection**: Policies consider user roles, group memberships, and network location to dynamically grant or deny access.
- **Audit and Review**: Access decisions are logged for audit purposes, enabling periodic review and compliance verification.

## 4.2.7 Limitations

While Azure provides robust governance and compliance capabilities, certain limitations must be acknowledged:

- **Third-Party Integrations**: Compliance controls may not extend to external systems or legacy applications outside Azure.
- **Policy Granularity**: Some Azure Policy definitions may lack the granularity required for highly specialized regulatory requirements.
- **Log Retention**: Extended log retention may incur additional storage costs and require explicit configuration for immutable storage.

UMS shall periodically review governance controls to address evolving requirements and mitigate any gaps.

## 4.2.8 Naming Conventions

Consistent resource naming is critical for governance, discoverability, and automation. UMS shall adopt Microsoft-recommended naming conventions for all infrastructure resources, as detailed below:

| Resource Type        | Naming Convention Example           | Description                           |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Resource Group       | rg-<env>-<app>-<location>           | e.g., rg-prod-ums-eus                 |
| Virtual Machine      | vm-<env>-<app>-<role>-<seq>         | e.g., vm-prod-ums-gateway-01          |
| Network Security Group | nsg-<env>-<app>-<location>        | e.g., nsg-prod-ums-eus                |
| Storage Account      | st<env><app><location><seq>         | e.g., stprodums01eus01                |

Adhering to standardized naming conventions enables automated policy enforcement, simplifies resource management, and supports auditability.

---

In summary, UMS’s infrastructure governance and compliance framework is architected to deliver operational excellence, regulatory conformity, and security assurance, leveraging Microsoft’s best practices and Azure-native controls. All controls are regularly reviewed and enhanced to address emerging risks and organizational requirements.

# 4.3 Limitations in Infrastructure & Networking

## 4.3.1 Overview

This section documents the current technical and operational limitations affecting the UMS solution’s infrastructure and networking design. These constraints arise from licensing, tenant-level configuration, and network architecture decisions. It is imperative to recognize these limitations to ensure proper governance, risk management, and to inform future enhancement strategies. The following subsections detail the specific limitations, their impact on the solution, and recommendations for potential remediation in alignment with Microsoft best practices.

## 4.3.2 Conditional Access & Multi-Factor Authentication Limitations

### Technical Limitation

The UMS account is not eligible for Conditional Access (CA) policies due to its current licensing tier, which is limited to Microsoft Entra ID Free. Conditional Access, a feature requiring Microsoft Entra ID Premium P1 or P2 licenses, provides granular access control and risk mitigation for cloud resources. As a result, UMS cannot leverage advanced CA policies to restrict access to specific services such as Power BI, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage.

### Operational Impact

- **Security Posture**: The absence of Conditional Access policies reduces the ability to enforce context-aware authentication, device compliance, and risk-based access controls. This limitation increases exposure to potential unauthorized access and diminishes the organization’s ability to meet compliance requirements.
- **User Experience**: Without CA, MFA enforcement is applied at the tenant level. This approach lacks granularity and may lead to unexpected login prompts for users, potentially impacting productivity and user satisfaction.
- **Governance**: Tenant-level MFA enforcement is less flexible, making it challenging to tailor security controls to specific user groups or applications.

### Current Enforcement Table

| No.  | Conditional Access Policy Description                                                                                 |
|------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage (Not implemented) |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy (Not implemented)                                                   |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts (Implemented at tenant level)   |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment (Permitted, but without CA granularity)                          |

### Future Remediation

We recommend that UMS evaluate the procurement of Microsoft Entra ID Premium P1 or P2 licenses. Upgrading to these tiers will enable Conditional Access policy configuration, providing enhanced security controls, compliance alignment, and improved user experience. This remediation should be prioritized in future phases to address evolving security requirements and regulatory mandates.

## 4.3.3 Private Link & Network Segmentation Limitations

### Technical Limitation

Private Link, a Microsoft Azure feature enabling secure, private connectivity to Azure services without traversing the public internet, is currently not implemented. This decision is primarily due to operational complexity and the lack of immediate business requirements. Consequently, all service communications occur over public endpoints, protected only by standard Azure networking and security controls.

### Operational Impact

- **Data Security**: Without Private Link, sensitive data in transit between the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) and Microsoft cloud services is exposed to the public internet, albeit encrypted via HTTPS. This increases the attack surface and may not satisfy stringent regulatory requirements for data isolation.
- **Compliance**: Certain regulatory frameworks mandate private connectivity for cloud resources. The absence of Private Link may hinder UMS’s ability to achieve certifications or meet contractual obligations.
- **Future Scalability**: As the solution grows, lack of network segmentation may complicate future integration with additional services or partners requiring private access.

### Future Remediation

We recommend a phased approach to Private Link adoption. UMS should conduct a risk assessment and evaluate the operational feasibility of implementing Private Link for critical services such as Azure SQL Database, Azure Storage, and Power BI. This would ensure end-to-end private connectivity, reducing exposure to external threats and aligning with Microsoft’s Zero Trust networking principles.

## 4.3.4 Licensing Constraints

### Technical Limitation

UMS currently operates under Microsoft Entra ID Free licensing, which restricts access to advanced features such as Conditional Access, Privileged Identity Management (PIM), and granular role-based access controls. This limitation constrains the organization’s ability to implement best-practice security and governance measures.

### Operational Impact

- **Feature Availability**: The free tier does not support advanced security features, limiting the ability to enforce least privilege, monitor privileged operations, or apply advanced authentication controls.
- **Risk Management**: Inability to leverage PIM and CA increases risk exposure, particularly for administrative accounts and sensitive workloads.
- **Scalability**: As UMS expands, the lack of premium features may impede operational efficiency and compliance.

### Licensing Table

| License Tier             | Conditional Access | Privileged Identity Management | MFA Granularity | Role-Based Access Control |
|-------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Entra ID Free           | Not Supported      | Not Supported                 | Tenant-wide     | Basic                    |
| Entra ID Premium P1     | Supported          | Supported                     | Granular        | Advanced                 |
| Entra ID Premium P2     | Supported          | Supported (with additional features) | Granular        | Advanced                 |

### Future Remediation

We recommend that UMS perform a cost-benefit analysis to determine the value of upgrading to Entra ID Premium P1 or P2. Investment in premium licensing will unlock advanced security, governance, and compliance features, enabling UMS to adopt Microsoft best practices and scale securely.

## 4.3.5 Network Outbound Rule Limitations

### Current Setup

The On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) VM relies on outbound network rules to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related services. These rules are configured to allow only essential traffic, adhering to Azure networking best practices. However, the reliance on public endpoints and specific port allowances introduces limitations in terms of flexibility and security.

- **Exposure to Public Endpoints**: All outbound traffic traverses the public internet, increasing potential exposure to external threats.
- **Limited Segmentation**: The current rules do not support granular segmentation or isolation of critical workloads.
- **Change Management**: Any modification to endpoints or port requirements necessitates careful coordination and testing to avoid service disruption.

### Future Remediation

To mitigate these limitations, we recommend:
- Evaluating the use of Azure Firewall or Network Security Groups (NSGs) for enhanced outbound filtering.
- Planning for Private Link implementation to eliminate reliance on public endpoints.
- Regularly reviewing and updating outbound rules to ensure alignment with evolving Microsoft service endpoints and security requirements.

## 4.3.6 Tenant-Level Configuration Dependencies

### Technical Limitation

Certain security controls, such as MFA enforcement, are applied at the tenant level due to licensing and architectural constraints. This dependency restricts the ability to apply differentiated policies to specific user groups or applications.

### Operational Impact

- **Policy Granularity**: Tenant-level enforcement lacks flexibility, potentially leading to over- or under-protection of certain users.
- **User Experience**: Broad enforcement may result in unnecessary prompts and reduced productivity.

### Future Remediation

Upon upgrading licensing and implementing Conditional Access, UMS will be able to define targeted policies, improving both security and user experience.

## 4.3.7 Summary & Recommendations

The current infrastructure and networking limitations are primarily driven by licensing constraints, absence of advanced networking features, and dependency on tenant-level settings. While these limitations do not prevent solution deployment, they impact security, compliance, and scalability. UMS shall prioritize remediation strategies, including licensing upgrades, Private Link adoption, and enhanced outbound rule management, to align with Microsoft best practices and future-proof the solution.

# 4.4 Naming Conventions for Infrastructure

## 4.4.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a standardized naming convention for all infrastructure resources deployed within the solution environment. This approach is mandated to ensure clarity, traceability, and operational efficiency across the entire lifecycle of the infrastructure. Adhering to Microsoft Best Practices, the naming convention will facilitate resource identification, streamline management, and minimize ambiguity, thereby supporting robust governance and compliance with enterprise standards.

## 4.4.2 Rationale and Microsoft Best Practices

Microsoft recommends adopting a consistent, descriptive, and scalable naming convention for cloud resources to:

- **Enhance Clarity:** Resource names must convey their purpose, environment, and association, reducing confusion for administrators and stakeholders.
- **Enable Traceability:** Clear names allow for rapid identification and auditing, aiding in troubleshooting and compliance.
- **Support Automation:** Predictable naming patterns facilitate scripting, automation, and integration with DevOps pipelines.
- **Promote Governance:** Standardized names support policy enforcement, cost management, and resource lifecycle tracking.

Naming conventions must be documented, communicated, and enforced across all teams to avoid ad-hoc or inconsistent resource naming.

## 4.4.3 Standard Naming Structure

The recommended naming convention for infrastructure resources is as follows:

`<Identity>-<Scenario/Activity>-<ResourceType>-<InstanceNumber>`

Where:
- **Identity:** Refers to the business unit, project, or application (e.g., StudentAffairs).
- **Scenario/Activity:** Indicates the environment or purpose (e.g., dev, prod, Bronze, Silver).
- **ResourceType:** Specifies the resource (e.g., VM, RG, LK for Lakehouse, DF for Dataflow).
- **InstanceNumber:** A numeric or incremental identifier for uniqueness.

This structure ensures each resource is uniquely identifiable and contextually descriptive.

## 4.4.4 Naming Convention Table

The following table illustrates the standardized naming convention for key infrastructure resources:

| No. | Item in Fabric        | Naming Convention Example                          |
|-----|----------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Fabric Capacity      | stagingareadevcapacity01                           |
| 2   | Workspace            | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                              |
| 3   | Lakehouse            | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_LK |
| 4   | Dataflow             | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_to_Gold_DF |
| 5   | Semantic Model       | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM               |
| 6   | Pipeline             | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL                      |
| 7   | Report               | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report           |

> **Note:** All resource names must avoid special characters, use underscores or hyphens for separation, and adhere to Azure resource-specific length and character restrictions.

## 4.4.5 Governance and Enforcement

UMS shall enforce naming conventions via Azure Policy, resource deployment templates, and periodic audits. All resource creation workflows must validate names against the documented standard. Exceptions shall only be granted through formal change management procedures.

## 4.4.6 Limitations

- **Azure Constraints:** Certain Azure resources impose character limits and restrictions on allowed symbols. Naming conventions must be adapted accordingly.
- **Legacy Resources:** Existing resources with non-standard names may require renaming or tagging for compliance, subject to impact analysis.

## 4.4.7 Alignment with Enterprise Standards

The naming convention is aligned with enterprise architecture principles, ensuring seamless integration with monitoring, billing, and governance platforms. All stakeholders and technical teams shall be trained on the convention as part of onboarding and operational procedures.

## 4.4.8 Summary

A robust, standardized naming convention is foundational to effective infrastructure management. UMS shall implement and rigorously enforce the prescribed naming structure to support clarity, traceability, and enterprise governance, in accordance with Microsoft Best Practices.

# 5.0 Security Architecture

The security architecture for the UMS solution is designed in accordance with Microsoft’s best practices, ensuring robust protection of both internal and external resources, while enabling seamless access to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Azure services. This section details the security model, authentication mechanisms, conditional access policies, multi-factor authentication (MFA) enforcement, outbound security rules, and the rationale for access controls, with a focus on governance, limitations, and compliance.

## 5.1 Security Model Overview

UMS shall implement a layered security model leveraging Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory), conditional access, and MFA. This approach is designed to mitigate risks associated with unauthorized access, credential compromise, and lateral movement within the environment. The architecture distinguishes between internal (corporate) and external (guest, partner) users, applying tailored access controls to each group.

### Security Architecture Diagram

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_002.png)

The diagram above illustrates the authentication and conditional access flow for Microsoft Fabric. User authentication attempts are evaluated against Entra conditional access policies, which collect signals (user, group, network location) to determine access permissions. Enforcement ensures only compliant users can access Microsoft Fabric and its components.

## 5.2 User Authentication & Identity Management

UMS shall utilize Microsoft Entra ID as the authoritative identity provider for all user authentication. This ensures a unified, cloud-native identity platform with integrated security controls.

- **Internal Users**: Employees and administrators are provisioned in Entra ID, with access governed by group memberships and role-based access controls (RBAC).
- **External Users**: Guest accounts are managed via Entra ID B2B, with restricted permissions and conditional access policies tailored to their risk profile.

All authentication flows are secured using modern protocols (OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect), and token issuance is tightly scoped to minimize privilege escalation.

## 5.3 Conditional Access & MFA Enforcement

Conditional access policies are central to the security posture, enabling dynamic access decisions based on user, device, location, and risk signals.

### Conditional Access Policy Table

| No.  | Conditional Access Policy Description                                                                                   |
|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure policy to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage                |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                       |
| DD03 | UMS users undergo additional verification (MFA) to reduce unexpected login prompts                                      |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                     |

#### Enforcement Details

- **MFA Enforcement**: MFA is enforced at the tenant level for all users, regardless of conditional access eligibility, in line with Microsoft’s Zero Trust principles. This ensures that even if conditional access is not available (e.g., due to licensing limitations), all authentication attempts require a second factor.
- **Policy Scope**: UMS accounts are currently not eligible for conditional access due to licensing constraints (Entra ID Free). Should requirements change, upgrading to Entra ID P1 or P2 will enable more granular policies, including device compliance and risk-based access.

#### Rationale

Conditional access and MFA provide defense-in-depth, reducing the risk of credential theft, phishing, and unauthorized access. The enforcement of MFA at the tenant level ensures that all users, including those not covered by conditional access, are protected.

## 5.4 Access Controls & Governance

UMS shall implement role-based access control (RBAC) and least privilege principles, ensuring users only have access to resources necessary for their role. Access reviews and audit logs are enabled to maintain compliance and detect anomalous activity.

### Roles & Responsibilities Table

| Internal User   | Limited access to assigned datasets/services   | Consume reports, submit requests                  |
| External User   | Restricted access (guest)                     | View shared reports, limited collaboration        |

Governance is enforced via periodic access reviews, automated alerts for policy violations, and integration with Microsoft Defender for Cloud for threat detection.

Limitations are acknowledged, with a roadmap for future enhancements as licensing and technical requirements evolve.

# 5.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Security

## 5.1.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a comprehensive security framework for Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environments, strictly adhering to Microsoft’s authoritative guidance and industry-leading security principles. The recommended approach is rooted in Zero Trust, rigorous identity management, robust credential protection, and layered access controls. This section details the best practices, rationale, and implementation specifics, referencing Microsoft’s official documentation and aligning with the latest enterprise security standards.

## 5.1.2 Zero Trust Security Principles

We recommend that UMS adopt the Zero Trust model, which asserts that no user, device, or application—whether inside or outside the corporate perimeter—should be implicitly trusted. Access to resources must be explicitly verified, continuously monitored, and tightly controlled. Microsoft’s Zero Trust framework encompasses the following pillars:

- **Verify Explicitly:** Always authenticate and authorize based on all available data points, including user identity, location, device health, and application context.
- **Least Privilege Access:** Limit user and application permissions to only those required for their role or task. Regularly review and adjust permissions to minimize risk.
- **Assume Breach:** Architect systems under the assumption that a breach may occur. Segment networks, monitor activity, and employ rapid detection and response mechanisms.

Microsoft’s official Zero Trust guidance is available at [Microsoft Zero Trust](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/zero-trust/).

## 5.1.3 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

UMS shall enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level for all users accessing Fabric and Power BI environments. MFA is a critical defense against credential compromise and unauthorized access. Microsoft recommends:

- **Mandatory MFA for All Users:** All users—including administrators, external collaborators, and service accounts—must authenticate using at least two factors (e.g., password and mobile app verification).
- **MFA for High-Privilege Roles:** Administrative accounts and users with access to sensitive data must always be subject to MFA.
- **Tenant-Wide Enforcement:** MFA enforcement at the tenant level ensures consistency and prevents gaps in protection, especially where Conditional Access may not be eligible for certain accounts.

This approach is aligned with Microsoft’s security baseline for Entra ID and Power BI ([Microsoft MFA Guidance](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/authentication/howto-mfa-getstarted)).

## 5.1.4 Conditional Access Policy Design

Conditional Access is a cornerstone of modern identity security, enabling granular control over who can access what, under which conditions. UMS shall design and implement Conditional Access policies in accordance with Microsoft best practices:

- **Policy Scope:** Policies must cover access to Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage, ensuring only authorized users can interact with these resources.
- **Signal-Based Decisions:** Access decisions should leverage signals such as user/group membership, device compliance, network location, and risk level.
- **External User Controls:** External users must be subject to Conditional Access, with policies tailored to limit their access and enforce additional verification.
- **Fallback for Unsupported Accounts:** Where Conditional Access cannot be applied (e.g., UMS accounts without requisite licenses), MFA must be enforced as a compensating control.

### Conditional Access Policy Table

| No.  | Conditional Access Policy Description                                                                                      |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure policies to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage                |
| DD02 | Include UMS users inside Conditional Access policy                                                                         |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification (MFA) to reduce unexpected login prompts                                      |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                        |

For detailed policy design, refer to [Microsoft Conditional Access Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional-access/).

## 5.1.5 Secure Credential Management

UMS shall implement secure credential management practices to prevent unauthorized access and credential leakage:

- **Password Policy Enforcement:** Enforce strong password requirements, including complexity, expiration, and history controls, as recommended by Microsoft.
- **Privileged Access Management:** Use Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) to control, monitor, and audit privileged account access.
- **Secrets Storage:** Store credentials, secrets, and certificates in Azure Key Vault, leveraging role-based access control (RBAC) and audit logging.
- **Credential Rotation:** Regularly rotate credentials and secrets, especially for service accounts and integration endpoints.

Reference: [Microsoft Secure Credential Management](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security/fundamentals/identity-management-best-practices).

## 5.1.6 Security Governance and Monitoring

UMS shall establish robust governance and monitoring mechanisms to ensure ongoing compliance and rapid response to threats:

- **Audit Logging:** Enable and review audit logs for all access attempts, policy changes, and administrative actions in Fabric and Power BI.
- **Alerting and Incident Response:** Configure security alerts for anomalous activity, failed logins, and privilege escalation. Integrate with Microsoft Defender for Cloud for advanced threat detection.
- **Periodic Reviews:** Conduct regular reviews of security policies, access permissions, and audit logs to ensure continued alignment with Microsoft best practices.

## 5.1.7 Limitations and Recommendations

While UMS strives for comprehensive security, certain limitations exist:

- **Conditional Access Licensing:** Advanced Conditional Access features require Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses. UMS currently operates without these; future upgrades are recommended for enhanced policy granularity.
- **Private Link Implementation:** Due to technical and licensing constraints, Private Link integration is deferred. UMS should revisit this option as requirements evolve.
- **External User Management:** Conditional Access for external users is subject to licensing and technical eligibility; fallback to MFA is enforced where necessary.

## 5.1.8 Security Policy Enforcement Table

| Policy Name                   | Enforcement Level | Scope                      | Rationale                                           |
|-------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| Multi-Factor Authentication   | Tenant-wide       | All users                  | Prevents credential compromise                      |
| Conditional Access            | Service-specific  | Power BI, Fabric, Azure    | Granular access control, signal-based decisions     |
| Privileged Access Management  | Role-based        | Administrators, Service Accts | Limits privilege abuse, enables auditing         |
| Audit Logging                 | Platform-wide     | All access events          | Ensures traceability, supports incident response    |
| Secure Credential Storage     | Service-wide      | All secrets & credentials  | Prevents leakage, enables rotation and monitoring   |

# 5.2 Governance & Compliance for Security

## 5.2.1 Governance Processes for Security Policy Management

UMS shall implement a robust governance framework for security policy management, guided by Microsoft’s Cloud Adoption Framework and Security Baseline recommendations. The governance structure will ensure that all security policies are centrally defined, consistently enforced, and periodically reviewed to address evolving threats and regulatory requirements.

We recommend the establishment of a Security Steering Committee comprising representatives from IT, compliance, and business units. This committee shall be responsible for:

- Overseeing the development and maintenance of security policies.
- Ensuring alignment with organizational goals and Microsoft best practices.
- Approving periodic updates based on risk assessments and changes in the threat landscape.

Security policies—including access controls, authentication requirements, and data protection mandates—shall be managed through Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Conditional Access policies are configured to protect key workloads such as Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage, as outlined below:

| No.  | Conditional Access Policy Description                                                                 |
|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure policy to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, Azure Storage |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                    |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA                                                    |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                  |

These policies are centrally managed and enforced at the tenant level to ensure uniformity and reduce the risk of unauthorized access. UMS shall review Conditional Access policies quarterly, or upon significant changes in business requirements or regulatory mandates.

### 5.2.1.1 Security Controls Review and Update

Security controls are subject to a formal review process. UMS shall:

- Conduct biannual security assessments to validate the effectiveness of existing controls.
- Leverage Microsoft Defender for Cloud and Azure Security Center for continuous monitoring and automated recommendations.
- Update controls in response to audit findings, threat intelligence, and Microsoft security advisories.

All changes to security controls must be documented, approved by the Security Steering Committee, and communicated to affected stakeholders. The change management process shall include impact analysis, testing in a non-production environment, and post-implementation review.

## 5.2.2 Audit Logging and Monitoring

UMS shall implement comprehensive audit logging across all Azure resources and user access points, in accordance with Microsoft’s security best practices.

Audit logs will be ingested into Azure Monitor and Azure Sentinel for real-time monitoring, anomaly detection, and automated alerting. UMS shall periodically review audit logs to ensure completeness and integrity, and conduct monthly log analysis to identify suspicious activity.

## 5.2.3 Compliance with Standards (ISO 27001, GDPR)

UMS shall ensure compliance with internationally recognized standards, including ISO 27001 and GDPR, by embedding security and privacy controls throughout the solution lifecycle.

### 5.2.3.1 ISO 27001 Alignment

- UMS shall maintain an Information Security Management System (ISMS) aligned with ISO 27001, covering asset management, access control, incident response, and continuous improvement.
- All Azure resources shall be configured in accordance with Microsoft’s ISO 27001-certified cloud infrastructure.
- Regular internal audits and external assessments shall be conducted to validate compliance.

- UMS shall implement data protection controls to ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of personal data.
- Data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure) shall be supported through Azure’s built-in privacy tools.
- Data processing activities shall be documented, and Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) shall be performed for high-risk scenarios.

## 5.2.4 Limitations and Future Considerations

While the current governance framework leverages Conditional Access and MFA, there are limitations:

- UMS accounts are not eligible for Conditional Access due to licensing constraints (Entra ID Free, not P1/P2).
- Private Link implementation is deferred due to technical and operational limitations; this may be revisited as requirements evolve.
- Additional licensing may be required for advanced Conditional Access features.

UMS shall periodically reassess these limitations and update the governance framework as new capabilities and licenses become available.

## 5.2.5 Security Governance Naming Conventions

To ensure clarity and consistency, UMS shall adopt Microsoft-recommended naming conventions for security policies and audit logs.

| Resource Type         | Naming Convention Example              | Description                         |
|----------------------|----------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Conditional Access    | CA-<Workload>-<PolicyPurpose>          | E.g., CA-PowerBI-MFAEnforcement     |
| Audit Log             | AuditLog-<Resource>-<Date>             | E.g., AuditLog-SQLDB-20240601       |
| Security Policy       | SecPol-<Area>-<Version>                | E.g., SecPol-AccessControl-v1.2     |

These conventions facilitate efficient policy management, audit tracking, and compliance reporting.
