|------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| Limited algorithm coverage         | Reduced flexibility, suboptimal models                        | Integrate Azure ML AutoML; enable model import/export      |
| Compute constraints                | Performance bottlenecks, limited scalability                  | Provision dedicated clusters; enable GPU support           |
| Integration challenges             | Restricted deployment, latency in analytics                   | Expose REST endpoints; optimize data pipelines             |
| Limited auditability & governance  | Compliance risks, lack of traceability                        | Integrate Purview; adopt enterprise governance tools       |
| Restricted collaboration           | Inefficient teamwork, slower innovation                       | Enable collaborative ML workspaces                         |

## 7.3.6 Conclusion

While the current ML workflow in UMS provides a solid foundation for data-driven experimentation and analytics, several architectural and operational limitations must be addressed to achieve enterprise-grade scalability, flexibility, and governance. By adopting the recommended mitigations and future enhancements, UMS will align with Microsoft Best Practices, ensuring robust, secure, and extensible machine learning capabilities that support evolving business requirements.

# 7.4 Naming Conventions for Machine Learning Artifacts

## 7.4.1 Overview

Establishing robust naming conventions for machine learning (ML) artifacts is paramount in ensuring clarity, traceability, and operational efficiency within the Unified Management System (UMS) environment. Microsoft recommends the adoption of systematic, descriptive, and consistent naming standards across all ML resources—including experiments, models, notebooks, datasets, and pipelines—to facilitate governance, lifecycle management, and collaboration. Proper naming conventions also mitigate risks associated with resource ambiguity, accidental overwrites, and compliance breaches, while supporting automated monitoring and auditing processes.

## 7.4.2 Microsoft Best Practices

Microsoft advocates for naming conventions that are:

- **Descriptive:** Clearly convey the artifact’s purpose, scenario, and ownership.
- **Consistent:** Adhere to a standardized structure across all environments and teams.
- **Unique:** Prevent duplication and confusion, particularly in shared or multi-tenant workspaces.
- **Traceable:** Enable straightforward mapping between artifacts, their lineage, and associated business processes.

Artifacts should incorporate elements such as environment, project identity, scenario/activity, and artifact type. For example, `<Identity>-<Scenario/Activity>-<ArtifactType>-<UniqueSuffix>`.

## 7.4.3 UMS Naming Standards for ML Artifacts

UMS shall implement the following naming standards for ML artifacts, aligned with Microsoft’s best practices and tailored for operational clarity and traceability:

| Artifact Type    | Naming Convention Example                                 | Description                                                                                      |
|------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Experiment       | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_EXP_202406              | `<Identity>_<Scenario>_EXP_<YYYYMM>`: Identifies experiment scope and creation date.             |
| Model            | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_MDL_v1                  | `<Identity>_<Scenario>_MDL_v<Version>`: Specifies model lineage and versioning.                  |
| Notebook         | StudentAffairs_DataPrep_NB                               | `<Identity>_<Activity>_NB`: Denotes notebook function and ownership.                             |
| Dataset          | StudentAffairs_Bronze_DS                                 | `<Identity>_<Stage>_DS`: Indicates dataset stage (Bronze/Silver/Gold) and purpose.               |
| Pipeline         | StudentAffairs_IngestData_PL                             | `<Identity>_<Activity>_PL`: Clarifies pipeline function and business context.                    |
| Workspace        | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                                    | `<Identity>_dev_WS`: Identifies workspace environment and project.                               |
| Lakehouse        | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK                                 | `<Identity>_<Stage>_LK`: Specifies data lakehouse stage and business area.                       |
| Dataflow         | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF                       | `<Identity>_<Source>_to_<Target>_DF`: Maps dataflow lineage and transformation direction.        |

> The above conventions ensure that every artifact is uniquely and meaningfully identified, supporting both operational and audit requirements.

## 7.4.4 Governance and Traceability

UMS shall enforce these naming conventions through automated resource creation scripts, workspace policies, and periodic audits. This approach ensures:

- **Governance:** Centralized oversight and compliance with organizational standards.
- **Traceability:** Rapid identification of artifact lineage, ownership, and business relevance.
- **Lifecycle Management:** Simplified versioning, deprecation, and archival processes.

Naming conventions will be documented and disseminated to all project stakeholders. Any deviation shall require formal approval and justification.

## 7.4.5 Limitations and Recommendations

While naming conventions significantly enhance clarity and traceability, they are not a substitute for metadata tagging and documentation. UMS is recommended to supplement naming standards with:

- **Metadata Tags:** For finer-grained classification and searchability.
- **Artifact Descriptions:** To provide context beyond naming.
- **Automated Validation:** To prevent non-compliant naming during artifact creation.

It is also important to note that Azure and Microsoft Fabric impose character limits and restrictions on certain resource names. UMS shall validate all naming conventions against these technical constraints to avoid deployment errors.

## 7.4.6 Summary Table: ML Artifact Naming Conventions

| No. | ML Artifact      | Naming Convention Example                                 |
|-----|------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Experiment       | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_EXP_202406              |
| 2   | Model            | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_MDL_v1                  |
| 3   | Notebook         | StudentAffairs_DataPrep_NB                               |
| 4   | Dataset          | StudentAffairs_Bronze_DS                                 |
| 5   | Pipeline         | StudentAffairs_IngestData_PL                             |
| 6   | Workspace        | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                                    |
| 7   | Lakehouse        | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK                                 |
| 8   | Dataflow         | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF                       |

## 7.4.7 Security and Compliance Considerations

UMS shall ensure that naming conventions do not inadvertently expose sensitive information (e.g., user names, confidential project codes) within artifact names. All naming standards must be reviewed periodically for compliance with data privacy and security policies.

| Policy Area                 | Enforcement Mechanism              | Compliance Requirement                    |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Sensitive Data Exposure     | Automated Name Validation          | No PII or confidential data in names      |
| Auditability                | Naming Convention Audit            | Quarterly review of artifact names        |
| Change Management           | Exception Approval Workflow        | Formal process for naming deviations      |

## 7.4.8 Conclusion

Adherence to Microsoft-endorsed naming conventions for machine learning artifacts is a foundational element of UMS’s operational excellence. By implementing these standards, UMS ensures resource clarity, traceability, and compliance, thereby supporting scalable, secure, and collaborative ML operations.

# 8.0 Power BI Analytics and Visualization Architecture

## 8.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a robust, scalable analytics and visualization architecture leveraging Microsoft Power BI, tightly integrated with Microsoft Fabric and OneLake. This approach is aligned with Microsoft’s best practices for modern enterprise analytics, ensuring seamless data flow from ingestion, processing, modeling, and visualization. The architecture is designed to deliver actionable insights to business stakeholders, while enforcing stringent data governance, security, and performance standards.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_009_img_003.png)

## 8.2 Integration with Fabric and OneLake

UMS’s data workflow is orchestrated within Fabric, utilizing Data Factory for ingestion and transformation, and OneLake for unified data storage. Data flows through a structured multi-layered Lakehouse architecture:

- **Bronze Lakehouse**: Stores raw, unprocessed data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse.
- **Silver Lakehouse**: Contains cleansed and transformed data, prepared using Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint.
- **Gold Lakehouse**: Houses highly refined, modeled, and scored datasets, including outputs from both AutoML and manual machine learning processes.

Direct Lake connectivity via OneLake enables Power BI to access Gold Lakehouse data with minimal latency, eliminating the need for data duplication and ensuring real-time analytics capabilities.

## 8.3 Semantic Model Design

UMS shall implement Semantic Models within Fabric as the foundational layer for Power BI analytics. Semantic Models are constructed using data from the Gold Lakehouse, providing a business-centric abstraction over complex data structures. This approach enables:

- **Consistent Business Logic**: Centralized definitions for measures, calculations, and hierarchies, ensuring uniformity across reports and dashboards.
- **Performance Optimization**: Direct Lake mode leverages in-memory caching and columnar storage, delivering high query performance for large datasets.
- **Self-Service Analytics**: Business users can interact with curated models, reducing dependency on IT for ad-hoc reporting.

### 8.3.1 Data Modeling Process

Data modeling follows a structured process:

1. **Source Data Selection**: Only validated and scored data from the Gold Lakehouse is used, ensuring data quality.
2. **Model Construction**: Semantic Models are built using Power BI Desktop, defining relationships, measures, and business logic.
3. **Model Publishing**: Models are published to Fabric Workspace, enabling centralized access and governance.

## 8.4 Power BI Report Development

UMS shall develop Power BI reports using best practices for enterprise analytics:

- **Direct Lake Connectivity**: Reports connect directly to Semantic Models via OneLake, ensuring up-to-date data without import delays.
- **Reusable Datasets**: Semantic Models serve as reusable datasets for multiple reports, promoting consistency and efficiency.
- **Interactive Visualizations**: Reports are designed with interactive filters, drill-downs, and custom visuals to empower business stakeholders.
- **Lifecycle Management**: Reports are versioned and managed within Fabric Workspace, supporting collaborative development and controlled deployment.

## 8.5 Data Security and Governance

UMS enforces rigorous data security and governance measures across the analytics architecture, adhering to Microsoft’s best practices:

### 8.5.1 Role-Based Access Control

Access to data, models, and reports is governed by Azure Active Directory (AAD) and Fabric Workspace roles.

| Role               | Permission Level         | Responsibilities                                 |
|--------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Data Engineer      | Read/Write Lakehouses   | ETL, data cleansing, transformation              |
| Data Scientist     | Read/Write Silver/Gold  | ML experimentation, scoring, model registration  |
| BI Developer       | Read Gold, Write Models | Semantic modeling, report development            |
| Business Analyst   | Read Models/Reports     | Data exploration, self-service analytics         |
| Data Steward       | Admin Models/Reports    | Data quality, governance, auditing               |

### 8.5.2 Data Security Policies

UMS shall implement the following security controls:

| Policy                        | Enforcement Mechanism          | Description                                      |
|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Data Encryption               | OneLake, Fabric                | All data at rest and in transit is encrypted     |
| Identity Management           | Azure AD, Identity_SM          | User authentication and access control           |
| Row-Level Security (RLS)      | Power BI Semantic Model        | Restricts data visibility based on user roles    |
| Auditing & Logging            | Fabric Workspace, Power BI     | All access and changes are logged for compliance |

## 8.6 Limitations and Considerations

While the architecture leverages Microsoft’s latest capabilities, UMS acknowledges the following limitations:

- **Direct Lake Mode**: Currently supports only certain data types and structures; complex transformations may require intermediate processing.
- **Semantic Model Complexity**: Overly complex models can impact performance; best practice is to maintain simplicity and optimize DAX calculations.
- **Data Refresh Latency**: Real-time analytics is dependent on the frequency of ETL and ML scoring processes; careful scheduling is required.
- **Security Inheritance**: Row-level security must be explicitly defined in Semantic Models; it does not automatically inherit from source tables.

## 8.7 Naming Conventions

UMS shall adopt standardized naming conventions for all Power BI and Fabric artifacts to ensure clarity, maintainability, and compliance with Microsoft recommendations.

| Artifact Type         | Naming Convention Example           | Description                                      |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Lakehouse Tables     | XX_Bronze_LK, XX_Silver_LK, XX_Gold_LK | Prefix with business domain, layer, and type     |
| Semantic Model       | SM_<BusinessArea>_<Subject>          | SM for Semantic Model, followed by area/subject  |
| Power BI Report      | PBI_<BusinessArea>_<ReportName>      | PBI for Power BI, followed by area/report name   |
| Notebook             | NB_<BusinessArea>_<Purpose>          | NB for Notebook, followed by area/purpose        |

## 8.8 Microsoft Best Practices Alignment

UMS’s Power BI analytics architecture is fully aligned with Microsoft’s best practices:

- **Unified Data Lake**: OneLake centralizes storage, reducing silos and simplifying governance.
- **Semantic Modeling**: Promotes reusable, governed datasets for consistent analytics.
- **Direct Lake Connectivity**: Enables high-performance, real-time analytics without data duplication.
- **Role-Based Security**: Ensures data protection and compliance.
- **Lifecycle Management**: Supports collaborative development and controlled deployment.

## 8.9 Summary

UMS’s Power BI Analytics and Visualization Architecture delivers a modern, governed, and scalable platform for business insights. By integrating Power BI with Fabric and OneLake, and implementing semantic models, direct lake connectivity, and enterprise-grade security, UMS empowers stakeholders with timely, reliable, and actionable analytics. This architecture positions UMS for future growth, adaptability, and compliance with evolving data governance standards.

# 8.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Power BI

UMS shall adhere to Microsoft’s established best practices for Power BI implementation, ensuring robust, scalable, and secure analytics capabilities. These practices are critical for enterprise deployments, where governance, performance, and data protection are paramount. The following subsections detail the recommended approaches for workspace management, dataset design, report optimization, and security, referencing authoritative Microsoft documentation such as the [Power BI Enterprise Deployment Guide](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/powerbi-deployment-guide) and the [Power BI Security Whitepaper](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/security-whitepaper).

## 8.1.1 Workspace Management

We recommend structuring Power BI workspaces to align with organizational boundaries, data sensitivity, and lifecycle management. Workspaces act as security and collaboration boundaries; their design directly impacts data governance and operational efficiency.

- **Workspace Segmentation:** UMS shall implement a tiered workspace structure, separating development, testing, and production environments. This separation enforces change control, minimizes risk of inadvertent data exposure, and supports agile development practices.
- **Access Control:** Workspace access shall be governed by Azure Active Directory (AAD) security groups, ensuring only authorized users can publish, modify, or consume content. Roles must be assigned according to the principle of least privilege.
- **Workspace Naming Conventions:** Consistent naming conventions facilitate discoverability and lifecycle management. For example, workspaces may be named using the format: `PBI-{Environment}-{Department}-{Project}` (e.g., `PBI-PRD-Finance-Reporting`).
- **Content Lifecycle:** UMS shall leverage deployment pipelines to promote content from development to production, ensuring traceability and rollback capabilities.

### Workspace Roles and Responsibilities

| Role                | Permissions                                                                 | Typical Assignment                  |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Admin               | Full control: add/remove users, modify settings, publish/delete content      | BI Administrators                   |
| Member              | Publish and update content, schedule refreshes                              | Power Users, Data Analysts          |
| Contributor         | Publish content, cannot modify workspace settings                           | Report Developers                   |
| Viewer              | View content only, no editing or publishing rights                          | Business Users, Executives          |

## 8.1.2 Dataset Design

Adhering to best practices in dataset design is critical for performance, maintainability, and scalability.

- **Data Modeling:** UMS shall implement a star schema wherever possible, as recommended by Microsoft, to optimize query performance and simplify report development.
- **Semantic Model:** All data models shall be developed using Power BI Desktop, with measures and calculations defined in DAX. The semantic model should be published to the Power BI Service and reused across multiple reports to ensure consistency.
- **Data Source Connectivity:** Direct Lake connectivity via OneLake shall be used for high-performance, near real-time analytics, minimizing data movement and leveraging Fabric’s unified data storage.
- **Incremental Refresh:** For large datasets, incremental refresh policies must be configured to reduce refresh times and resource consumption, as documented in [Configure incremental refresh](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/connect-data/incremental-refresh-overview).
- **Data Lineage and Impact Analysis:** UMS shall utilize Power BI’s built-in lineage view to track data sources, transformations, and dependencies, supporting governance and troubleshooting.

## 8.1.3 Report Optimization

Optimized reports ensure a responsive user experience and efficient resource utilization.

- **Visual Design:** Limit the number of visuals per page to reduce rendering time. Use bookmarks and drill-through features to enhance interactivity without overloading report pages.
- **Query Reduction:** Avoid complex DAX calculations in visuals; instead, pre-calculate measures in the semantic model. Utilize aggregation tables for high-volume datasets.
- **Data Volume Management:** Filter data at the source to minimize dataset size. Leverage row-level security (RLS) to restrict data access, improving performance and compliance.
- **Performance Monitoring:** UMS shall utilize Power BI Performance Analyzer and Service Metrics to monitor and optimize report performance, as outlined in [Optimize report performance](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/optimize-report-performance).

## 8.1.4 Security

Security is paramount in enterprise BI deployments. UMS shall implement a multi-layered security approach, combining Power BI’s built-in features with Azure security controls.

### Power BI Security Policies

| Security Measure                | Implementation Details                                                                                 | Reference Documentation                                                |
|---------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)| Assign workspace roles via AAD groups; enforce least privilege principle                              | [Workspace roles](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/collaborate-share/service-new-workspaces) |
| Row-Level Security (RLS)        | Define DAX filters in the semantic model to restrict data access by user attributes                   | [Row-level security](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/admin/service-security-row-level-security) |
| Data Encryption                 | Ensure data is encrypted at rest and in transit (TLS 1.2 enforced)                                    | [Security whitepaper](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/guidance/security-whitepaper)           |
| Audit Logging                   | Enable Power BI audit logs in Microsoft Purview for monitoring and compliance                        | [Audit logs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/admin/service-security-auditing)                |
| Data Loss Prevention (DLP)      | Apply DLP policies to prevent sharing of sensitive data outside the organization                      | [DLP policies](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/admin/service-security-data-protection)        |

## 8.1.5 Governance and Limitations

Effective governance ensures compliance, quality, and operational continuity.

- **Data Governance:** UMS shall integrate Power BI with Microsoft Purview for data cataloging, classification, and lineage tracking, supporting regulatory compliance and data stewardship.
- **Capacity Planning:** Monitor Power BI Premium capacity utilization and scale resources proactively to avoid throttling or degraded performance.
- **Known Limitations:** UMS must be aware of Power BI service limitations, such as dataset size limits (1GB for Pro, 400GB for Premium per dataset), refresh frequency, and concurrent query restrictions. These constraints must be factored into solution design and user expectations.
- **Change Management:** All changes to datasets, reports, and workspaces shall follow a controlled release process, with versioning and rollback procedures in place.

# 8.2 Governance & Compliance for Power BI

UMS shall implement robust governance and compliance measures for Power BI to ensure that data visualization, reporting, and analytics activities adhere to Microsoft best practices and organizational standards. This section outlines the architectural controls, policies, and processes that will be enforced to maintain security, data integrity, and regulatory compliance across all Power BI workspaces and artifacts.

## 8.2.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Power BI Governance

We recommend adopting Microsoft’s Power BI governance framework, which emphasizes centralized oversight, least privilege access, data sensitivity labeling, and continuous monitoring. The following principles shall be embedded within the UMS Power BI environment:

- **Workspace Lifecycle Management**: All Power BI workspaces must be created, maintained, and retired according to a documented lifecycle policy. Workspace owners are required to periodically review membership and usage to prevent orphaned or obsolete workspaces.

- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**: Access to Power BI assets shall be governed by Azure Active Directory (AAD) groups and roles. Permissions will be granted strictly on a need-to-know basis, minimizing risk of unauthorized data exposure.

- **Data Sensitivity Labeling**: Power BI datasets, reports, and dashboards must be classified using Microsoft Information Protection (MIP) sensitivity labels. These labels enforce encryption, access restrictions, and auditing in accordance with data classification policies.

- **Audit Logging and Monitoring**: All user activities within Power BI, including report access, data export, and sharing, shall be logged and monitored using Microsoft Purview and Power BI Activity Logs. Security teams will review logs regularly for anomalies and policy violations.

- **Compliance with Reporting Standards**: Reports and dashboards must adhere to UMS’s established reporting standards, including data accuracy, visualization guidelines, and metadata documentation. All published artifacts will undergo periodic compliance reviews.

## 8.2.2 Workspace Access Controls

UMS shall enforce granular access controls within Power BI workspaces to ensure that only authorized personnel can view, edit, or publish reports. The following table defines the roles, permissions, and responsibilities for Power BI workspace governance:

| Role                | Permission Level              | Responsibilities                                              |
|---------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
| Workspace Admin     | Full control                 | Manage workspace settings, membership, and artifact lifecycle|
| Member              | Edit, publish                | Create and modify reports, datasets, and dashboards          |
| Contributor         | Edit (no publish)            | Prepare datasets and visuals, but cannot publish artifacts   |
| Viewer              | Read-only                    | View reports and dashboards; cannot modify or export data    |
| External User       | Restricted (guest access)    | Access specific reports as permitted; subject to stricter controls |

Access assignments shall be reviewed quarterly. Workspace Admins must validate that membership aligns with current organizational roles and project requirements.

## 8.2.3 Data Sensitivity Labeling

To protect sensitive information and comply with regulatory requirements, UMS shall mandate the use of Microsoft Information Protection sensitivity labels on all Power BI artifacts. The labeling process is as follows:

- **Classification**: Data owners must classify datasets and reports at creation, selecting from predefined labels (e.g., Confidential, Internal, Public).
- **Enforcement**: Sensitivity labels automatically apply encryption, restrict sharing, and enable auditing features.
- **Propagation**: Labels applied in Power BI propagate to downstream Office 365 applications, ensuring consistent data protection.

| Sensitivity Label   | Description                  | Enforcement Actions                        |
|--------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Confidential       | Highly sensitive data         | Encryption, restricted sharing, audit logs  |
| Internal           | Organization-only data        | Limited sharing, watermarking              |
| Public             | Non-sensitive data            | No restrictions; open access               |

UMS shall periodically review label assignments to ensure compliance and update classification schemes as regulatory requirements evolve.

## 8.2.4 Compliance with Organizational Reporting Standards

All Power BI reports and dashboards must conform to UMS’s organizational reporting standards, which are designed to ensure consistency, data integrity, and regulatory compliance. Key aspects include:

- **Data Accuracy**: All reports must source data from validated datasets, preferably from the Gold Lakehouse, which serves as the standardized repository for modeled and scored results.
- **Visualization Guidelines**: Reports must adhere to UMS’s visualization guidelines, including approved color palettes, chart types, and layout conventions to promote clarity and accessibility.
- **Metadata Documentation**: Each report and dataset must include comprehensive metadata, detailing data sources, refresh schedules, and business definitions.
- **Approval Workflow**: Reports intended for external or executive consumption must undergo a formal approval process, including review by data stewards and compliance officers.
- **Periodic Review**: Published reports will be subject to quarterly compliance audits to verify adherence to standards and identify opportunities for improvement.

## 8.2.5 Governance Limitations

While UMS’s governance framework for Power BI is designed to be comprehensive, certain limitations exist:

- **Third-Party Integrations**: Reports that leverage external data sources may be subject to additional compliance risks. UMS must evaluate such integrations on a case-by-case basis.
- **Guest Access**: External user access is restricted but not entirely prevented. UMS must monitor guest activities closely and enforce stricter controls where necessary.
- **Label Propagation**: Sensitivity label propagation relies on user compliance and technical enforcement; gaps may exist if users bypass labeling at artifact creation.

UMS shall continuously assess these limitations and update governance policies to mitigate associated risks.

## 8.2.6 Naming Conventions for Power BI Artifacts

Adhering to standardized naming conventions is critical for discoverability, lifecycle management, and compliance auditing. UMS shall implement the following naming convention for Power BI workspaces, datasets, reports, and dashboards:

| Artifact Type       | Naming Convention Example           | Description                                  |
|---------------------|------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Workspace           | PBI_<Dept>_<Project>_<Env>         | E.g., PBI_Finance_UMSReporting_Prod          |
| Dataset             | DS_<Source>_<Subject>_<Version>    | E.g., DS_GoldLake_Sales_202406               |
| Report              | RPT_<Subject>_<Audience>_<Date>    | E.g., RPT_SalesExec_Q2_202406                |
| Dashboard           | DB_<Subject>_<Purpose>_<Env>       | E.g., DB_SalesPerformance_Mgmt_Prod          |

These conventions facilitate efficient artifact management, enable automated compliance checks, and support auditability.

## 8.2.7 Security Policies for Power BI

UMS shall enforce rigid security policies to safeguard data within Power BI. The following table summarizes key security controls:

| Policy                        | Enforcement Mechanism                  | Review Frequency |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------|------------------|
| MFA for Power BI Access       | Azure AD Conditional Access            | Quarterly        |
| Data Export Restrictions      | Power BI Admin Portal                  | Monthly          |
| Sharing Controls              | Sensitivity Labels & Sharing Policies  | Quarterly        |
| Audit Log Review              | Power BI Activity Logs & Purview       | Weekly           |
| Guest Access Monitoring       | Azure AD & Power BI Audit Logs         | Monthly          |

Security policies will be reviewed and updated in response to evolving threats and regulatory requirements.

# 8.3 Limitations in Power BI Integration

The integration of Power BI within the proposed data workflow, leveraging Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse architecture and OneLake connectivity, introduces several inherent limitations and constraints. These limitations are primarily dictated by the architectural design of Power BI, the underlying data platform, and Microsoft’s published best practices. It is imperative that UMS stakeholders and solution owners are fully aware of these constraints to ensure realistic expectations, effective governance, and sustainable solution evolution.

## 8.3.1 Dataset Size and Storage Limitations

Power BI, when connected to large-scale data repositories such as the Gold Lakehouse via Direct Lake (OneLake), is subject to both technical and licensing-imposed dataset size restrictions.

- **Dataset Size**: In Power BI Premium, the maximum dataset size is currently 400 GB per dataset. For Pro workspaces, the limit is 1 GB per dataset. These constraints may impact the ability to model or visualize extremely large, complex datasets without partitioning or aggregation.
- **Semantic Model Memory**: The in-memory storage engine (VertiPaq) imposes memory limits per dataset, which can affect performance and the ability to load large models.
- **Mitigation**: We recommend implementing data partitioning strategies, aggregations, and incremental data refresh policies to reduce memory footprint and improve query performance. Where possible, leverage Direct Lake mode to bypass import limitations, while monitoring for query latency.

## 8.3.2 Data Refresh Rates and Latency

Power BI’s data refresh capabilities are governed by the workspace licensing tier and the chosen connectivity mode.

- **Scheduled Refresh**: Power BI Pro allows up to 8 refreshes per day, while Premium supports up to 48. However, Direct Lake and DirectQuery modes provide near real-time access but may introduce query latency depending on the underlying data platform’s performance.
- **Latency**: Real-time analytics is limited by the performance of the Gold Lakehouse and network throughput. Large or complex queries may experience delays, especially during peak usage.
- **Mitigation**: For near real-time requirements, we recommend using Direct Lake connectivity. For imported models, schedule refreshes during off-peak hours and optimize dataflows for minimal processing time. Monitor refresh success and failure rates using Power BI Service monitoring tools.

## 8.3.3 Feature and Connectivity Limitations

Certain Power BI features are unavailable or limited when using Direct Lake or DirectQuery connectivity, as opposed to Import mode.

| Feature/Capability            | Import Mode | Direct Lake | DirectQuery |
|------------------------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Advanced DAX Calculations    | Full        | Partial     | Partial     |
| AI Visuals                   | Full        | Partial     | Partial     |
| Aggregations                 | Full        | Supported   | Supported   |
| Q&A Natural Language Query   | Full        | Not Supported | Not Supported |
| Incremental Refresh          | Supported   | Not Required | Supported   |
| Composite Models             | Supported   | Limited     | Supported   |

- **Unsupported Features**: Q&A, certain AI visuals, and advanced DAX functions may not be available or may perform sub-optimally in Direct Lake mode.
- **Mitigation**: Where advanced features are required, consider hybrid models that combine Import and Direct Lake datasets, or pre-aggregate data in the Gold Lakehouse. Regularly review Microsoft’s release notes for feature enhancements.

## 8.3.4 Security and Governance Limitations

Power BI’s security model is robust but has limitations when integrating with external Lakehouse repositories.

- **Row-Level Security (RLS)**: RLS is supported in semantic models but may not propagate to underlying Lakehouse tables in Direct Lake mode, potentially exposing broader data than intended.
- **Data Lineage and Auditing**: End-to-end lineage from Power BI visuals back to Gold Lakehouse tables may be incomplete, impacting governance and compliance.
- **Mitigation**: We recommend enforcing RLS at both the semantic model and Lakehouse access layer. Utilize Microsoft Purview for cross-platform data lineage and auditing.

## 8.3.5 Current Integration Architecture

The current design leverages the following integration points, each with associated limitations:

| Integration Point                  | Description                                                      | Limitation/Constraint         |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Dataflow Gen2                      | Data movement between Lakehouses                                 | Limited transformation logic |
| Gold Lakehouse (OneLake)           | Central repository for modeled/scored data                       | Storage/throughput limits    |
| Power BI Semantic Model            | Data modeling layer connected to Gold Lakehouse                  | Dataset size/memory limits   |
| Power BI Desktop (OneLake)         | Visualization and analytics via Semantic Model                   | Feature parity gaps          |

## 8.3.6 Naming Conventions

To ensure maintainability and clarity, all Power BI datasets, dataflows, and workspace artifacts shall adhere to the following naming convention, as per Microsoft best practices:

| Artifact Type      | Naming Pattern                     | Example                         |
|--------------------|------------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Dataset            | [Domain]_[Lakehouse]_[Purpose]     | FIN_GoldLakehouse_SalesModel    |
| Dataflow           | [Source]_[Destination]_[Operation] | Bronze_Silver_Cleansing         |
| Workspace          | [BusinessUnit]_[Project]_[Env]     | UMS_Analytics_PRD               |

Adhering to these conventions facilitates automated governance, auditing, and supportability.

## 8.3.7 Recommendations for Future Enhancements

To address the above limitations and future-proof the Power BI integration, we recommend:

- **Continuous Monitoring**: Implement proactive monitoring of dataset size, refresh failures, and query performance using Power BI Service and Azure Monitor.
- **Feature Roadmap Alignment**: Regularly evaluate Microsoft’s Power BI and Fabric feature roadmap to adopt new capabilities as they become available.
- **Data Model Optimization**: Invest in ongoing semantic model optimization, including the use of aggregations, perspectives, and incremental refresh.
- **Advanced Security Integration**: Integrate with Microsoft Purview for enhanced data governance and end-to-end lineage tracking.
- **User Training**: Provide targeted training for report developers on the limitations and best practices of Direct Lake and hybrid connectivity modes.

By understanding and proactively managing these limitations, UMS shall ensure a resilient, scalable, and governed Power BI analytics platform that aligns with Microsoft’s recommended architectural patterns.

# 8.4 Naming Conventions for Power BI Artifacts

## 8.4.1 Introduction

UMS shall implement a standardized naming convention for all Power BI artifacts—including workspaces, datasets, reports, and dashboards—in accordance with Microsoft Best Practices. A consistent naming strategy is essential to support operational governance, streamline artifact discoverability, and facilitate effective collaboration across teams. Proper naming conventions also mitigate risks associated with misidentification, orphaned resources, and unauthorized access, while ensuring seamless integration with enterprise data cataloging and lifecycle management processes.

## 8.4.2 Microsoft Best Practices

Microsoft recommends adopting clear, descriptive, and hierarchical naming conventions for Power BI artifacts. Names should reflect the artifact’s purpose, scope, environment, and ownership, enabling users to quickly identify and locate relevant resources. Prefixes and suffixes should be used to denote environments (e.g., dev, test, prod), business domains, and artifact types.

### Key Principles:
- **Descriptive**: Names must clearly indicate the artifact’s function and business context.
- **Consistent**: Uniform structure across all artifacts to minimize confusion and support automation.
- **Environment-Specific**: Environment identifiers (dev, test, prod) must be included to distinguish between deployment stages.
- **Uniqueness**: Names must be unique within their scope to prevent ambiguity and accidental overwrites.
- **Scalable**: Naming patterns should support future expansion as new business scenarios and artifact types emerge.

## 8.4.3 Governance

UMS shall enforce naming conventions through automated validation rules and periodic audits. Owners of Power BI artifacts are responsible for compliance, and deviation from the prescribed patterns must be formally justified and documented. The naming convention shall be reviewed annually to ensure continued alignment with organizational requirements and Microsoft’s evolving best practices.

## 8.4.4 Naming Convention Structure

The recommended naming convention for Power BI artifacts is as follows:

`<Identity>-<Scenario/Activity>-<ArtifactType>-<ArtifactName>`

Where:
- **Identity**: Business domain or owning department (e.g., StudentAffairs)
- **Scenario/Activity**: Business process or functional area (e.g., StudentPerformance)
- **ArtifactType**: Type of Power BI artifact (e.g., WS for Workspace, SM for Semantic Model, Report for Report)
- **ArtifactName**: Specific artifact name or unique identifier

## 8.4.5 Artifact Naming Examples

The following table illustrates the naming convention applied to various Power BI artifacts within the UMS environment:

| No. | Item in Fabric        | Example Naming Convention                                 |
|-----|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Fabric Capacity      | stagingareadevcapacity01                                  |
| 2   | Workspace            | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                                     |
| 3   | Lakehouse            | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_LK      |
| 4   | Dataflow             | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_to_Gold_DF |
| 5   | Semantic Model       | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM                      |
| 6   | Pipeline             | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL                             |
| 7   | Report               | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report                  |

## 8.4.6 Limitations

While naming conventions greatly improve clarity and manageability, they are not a substitute for robust metadata management or access controls. The convention must be adhered to in all artifact creation processes; however, legacy artifacts may require renaming or migration to align with the new standard. Additionally, Power BI imposes character limits and restrictions on certain artifact names—UMS must ensure compliance with these technical constraints.

## 8.4.7 Naming Convention Policy Table

UMS shall enforce the following policy for Power BI artifact naming:

| Artifact Type   | Naming Pattern Example                        | Environment Identifier | Allowed Characters | Max Length |
|-----------------|----------------------------------------------|-----------------------|-------------------|------------|
| Workspace       | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                         | dev, test, prod       | Alphanumeric, _   | 50         |
| Dataset         | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM          | dev, test, prod       | Alphanumeric, _   | 50         |
| Report          | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report      | dev, test, prod       | Alphanumeric, _   | 50         |
| Dashboard       | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Dashboard   | dev, test, prod       | Alphanumeric, _   | 50         |
| Dataflow        | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF            | dev, test, prod       | Alphanumeric, _   | 50         |

## 8.4.8 Conclusion

UMS’s adoption of a rigorous Power BI artifact naming convention will ensure artifacts are easily discoverable, properly governed, and aligned with Microsoft’s best practices. This approach supports operational efficiency, enhances security, and facilitates future scalability as business requirements evolve. All stakeholders are required to comply with the prescribed patterns, and the governance team shall monitor adherence as part of ongoing operational oversight.

# 9.0 Roles, Responsibilities, and Access Management

## 9.1 Overview

Effective roles, responsibilities, and access management are foundational to the security, governance, and operational integrity of the UMS Power BI & Fabric environment. UMS shall implement a robust Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model, aligning with Microsoft’s best practices, to ensure that users are granted the minimum necessary privileges to perform their functions. This approach mitigates the risk of unauthorized access, supports compliance with regulatory requirements, and enables clear accountability for actions within the environment.

## 9.2 Role Definitions and Mappings

UMS shall define and assign roles based on the principle of least privilege, ensuring that each user’s access is strictly limited to their operational requirements. The following table details the mapping of Power BI/Fabric workspace roles to UMS personnel, along with their associated responsibilities:

| No. | Workspace Role       | Suggested Role                          | UMS Personnel (PIC)                                                                                                                                         |
|-----|---------------------|-----------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Workspace Admin     | Fabric Admin / Data Engineer            | Dr Suraya Binti Alias (Sektor Analitik Data DARTIC, PPDM Official), Mohd. Zamrie Bin Musa, Noor Afiza Binti Momen, Ts AG Azihar @ AG KU Azihar Bin AG Tangah |
| 2   | Workspace Contributor | Data Engineer / Business User Admin     | Mohd Khairul Nizam Bin Senu (DB), Assie Zaee Petrus (DB), Muhaimin Bin Mad Noor (Infra), Nuraliah Binti Alias (Infra), Chong Tze Chien (Server Security), Abdul Latif Matali (Server Security) |
| 3   | Workspace Viewer    | Data Analyst / Stakeholder / Project Director | Director: Dr Farashazillah Binti Yahya; Stakeholders: Roslee Bin Talip, Dayangku Rozlina BTE PG. Aliuddin, Norhanieyati Binti Norhaizat, Zaidie @ Mohd Zaidie Bin Adilai, Professor Madya TS Dr Mohd Hanafi Bin Ahmad Hijazi |

### 9.2.1 Role Responsibilities

- **Workspace Admin (Fabric Admin / Data Engineer):**  
  Responsible for overall workspace governance, including user and permission management, workspace configuration, and ensuring compliance with organizational policies. Workspace Admins are empowered to add or remove users, configure datasets, and oversee data security.
- **Workspace Contributor (Data Engineer / Business User Admin):**  
  Tasked with creating, editing, and publishing content within the workspace. Contributors can upload datasets, develop reports, and collaborate on analytics solutions but cannot modify workspace-level permissions.
- **Workspace Viewer (Data Analyst / Stakeholder / Project Director):**  
  Granted read-only access to published reports and datasets. Viewers are responsible for data consumption, insights extraction, and decision-making based on the analytics provided, without the ability to alter content or configurations.

## 9.3 Access Management Processes

UMS shall implement the following access management processes to ensure secure and auditable assignment of roles:

1. **Onboarding and Offboarding:**  
   All requests for access must be formally submitted and approved by the designated Workspace Admin. Upon personnel changes, access rights are reviewed and updated promptly to prevent orphaned accounts or excessive privileges.
2. **Role Assignment and Review:**  
   Role assignments are managed centrally via Azure Active Directory (AAD) groups mapped to Power BI/Fabric roles. Quarterly access reviews are mandated to validate appropriateness of permissions and to detect privilege creep.
3. **Segregation of Duties:**  
   UMS enforces clear separation between administrative and operational roles to prevent conflicts of interest and enhance auditability.
4. **Just-in-Time Access (JIT):**  
   For sensitive administrative actions, JIT access may be provisioned using Privileged Identity Management (PIM), ensuring temporary elevation of privileges with full audit trails.
5. **Change Management:**  
   Any changes to role assignments or access policies must follow the formal change management process, including risk assessment and approval by governance authorities.

## 9.4 RBAC Assignment Model

UMS shall leverage Azure RBAC for granular access control, ensuring that permissions are assigned at the lowest possible scope to minimize risk exposure. The following table summarizes the RBAC assignment model:

| Role                  | Scope                  | Assignment Mechanism              | Review Frequency | Escalation Path        |
|-----------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Workspace Admin       | Workspace/Resource Group | Direct or via AAD Security Group | Quarterly        | ICT Security Manager   |
| Workspace Contributor | Workspace               | AAD Security Group                | Quarterly        | Workspace Admin        |
| Workspace Viewer      | Workspace               | AAD Security Group                | Quarterly        | Workspace Admin        |

## 9.5 Escalation Procedures

In the event of access disputes, incidents, or urgent access requirements, UMS shall adhere to the following escalation procedures:

1. **First Level:**  
   Issues are raised to the Workspace Admin for initial investigation and resolution.
2. **Second Level:**  
   If unresolved, the issue is escalated to the ICT Security Manager, who will review the request and, if necessary, consult with the Project Director or Data Governance Committee.
3. **Emergency Access:**  
   For critical incidents requiring immediate intervention, temporary access may be granted by the ICT Security Manager, with all actions fully logged and reviewed post-incident.

## 9.6 Microsoft Best Practices Alignment

UMS’s access management framework adheres to the following Microsoft best practices:

- **Least Privilege Principle:**  
  Users are granted only the permissions necessary for their roles, reducing the attack surface and risk of data leakage.
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):**  
  Centralized, policy-driven access assignment ensures consistency and simplifies auditing.
- **Privileged Access Management:**  
  Use of Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for sensitive roles provides time-bound, just-in-time access with comprehensive logging.
- **Regular Access Reviews:**  
  Scheduled reviews of permissions prevent privilege creep and ensure ongoing alignment with organizational needs.
- **Separation of Duties:**  
  Administrative and operational responsibilities are segregated to enhance security and accountability.

## 9.7 Governance and Limitations

- **Governance:**  
  All access management activities are subject to oversight by the Data Governance Committee, which is responsible for policy enforcement, audit, and compliance monitoring.
- **Limitations:**  
  While RBAC provides robust access control, it is limited by the granularity of roles available within Power BI/Fabric. Custom roles may require additional configuration or third-party solutions. UMS must ensure all exceptions are documented and approved by governance authorities.

## 9.8 Naming Conventions

To facilitate clarity and auditability, UMS shall adopt the following naming conventions for Azure Active Directory groups and roles:

| Entity Type        | Naming Convention Example                | Description                                      |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Admin Group        | ums-pbi-admin-[workspace]                | Power BI/Fabric Workspace Admins                  |
| Contributor Group  | ums-pbi-contrib-[workspace]              | Power BI/Fabric Workspace Contributors            |
| Viewer Group       | ums-pbi-viewer-[workspace]               | Power BI/Fabric Workspace Viewers                 |

All group names must be unique, descriptive, and consistent with UMS’s organizational taxonomy.

---

This structured and governed approach to roles, responsibilities, and access management ensures that UMS’s Power BI & Fabric environment remains secure, compliant, and operationally efficient, fully aligned with Microsoft’s recommended best practices.

# 10.0 Risks and Mitigations

This section systematically identifies and assesses potential risks inherent in the proposed solution’s infrastructure, security, data workflow, and machine learning (ML) components. For each risk, we provide a comprehensive mitigation strategy, a contingency plan to address risk realization, and a clear residual risk assessment. This approach is aligned with Microsoft’s best practices for risk management in cloud-based analytics and ML solutions, ensuring both operational resilience and regulatory compliance.

## 10.1 Infrastructure Risks

### 10.1.1 Risk: Network Connectivity Disruption

**Description:**  
The On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine is critical for secure data transfer between on-premises sources and Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related cloud services. Disruption in outbound network connectivity can halt data ingestion and analytics workflows.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Strictly enforce Azure outbound security rules, as detailed in section 6.0, to explicitly allow only required endpoints and ports (primarily TCP 443 for HTTPS and TCP 80 for connectivity tests).
- Implement Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) with least-privilege principles to prevent unauthorized outbound access.
- Monitor network health using Azure Monitor and configure alerts for abnormal latency or packet loss.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- Maintain documented fallback procedures for manual data transfer or temporary use of alternative secure VPN tunnels.
- Prepare a secondary gateway VM in a different availability zone for rapid failover.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Low.** With proper NSG configuration and monitoring, the likelihood and impact of prolonged connectivity loss are minimized.

### 10.1.2 Risk: Inadequate VM Sizing or Resource Exhaustion

**Description:**  
Improperly sized VMs for the OPDG or ML workloads can result in performance bottlenecks, failed jobs, or service outages.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Follow Microsoft’s recommended VM sizing guidelines for data gateway and ML workloads, considering expected data volume and concurrency.
- Enable autoscaling where supported and monitor resource utilization with Azure Monitor.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- Maintain a process for rapid VM resizing or horizontal scaling.
- Schedule regular performance reviews and stress tests.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Medium.** While monitoring and scaling mitigate most issues, sudden unexpected spikes may still cause transient slowdowns.

## 10.2 Security Risks

### 10.2.1 Risk: Absence of Conditional Access Policies

**Description:**  
UMS accounts are currently ineligible for Microsoft Entra Conditional Access (CA) due to licensing constraints. This limits the ability to enforce granular access controls on cloud resources.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level for all users, in accordance with Microsoft security best practices.
- Regularly review user access logs and implement Just-In-Time (JIT) access where possible.
- Plan for future CA adoption if licensing is upgraded.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- If suspicious access is detected, immediately disable affected user accounts and conduct a root-cause investigation.
- Prepare incident response playbooks for account compromise scenarios.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Medium.** Tenant-wide MFA reduces most risks, but lack of CA means some advanced threat vectors (e.g., location-based restrictions) are not covered.

### 10.2.2 Risk: Unauthorized Data Exfiltration

**Description:**  
Improperly configured outbound rules or insufficient monitoring may allow data exfiltration from the OPDG VM or other Azure resources.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Apply strict NSG rules, only permitting outbound traffic to whitelisted Microsoft endpoints.
- Enable Azure Defender for Cloud to detect anomalous outbound traffic patterns.
- Audit logs regularly for unauthorized access attempts.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- In the event of suspected exfiltration, immediately isolate affected resources and rotate credentials.
- Notify relevant stakeholders and initiate the established data breach response protocol.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Low.** With robust NSG, monitoring, and logging, the risk of undetected exfiltration is minimal.

#### Security Policy Table

| Policy Area                | Enforcement Mechanism                | Frequency/Scope           | Owner            |
|----------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| Multi-Factor Authentication| Tenant-wide enforcement              | All users                 | Security Admin   |
| Outbound NSG Rules         | Azure NSG, whitelisted endpoints     | All gateway VMs           | Network Engineer |
| Access Review              | Azure AD Sign-In Logs                | Monthly                   | IT Auditor       |
| Incident Response          | Playbook, account disablement        | As needed                 | SOC Team         |

## 10.3 Data Workflow Risks

### 10.3.1 Risk: Data Quality and Integrity Issues

**Description:**  
Data ingested into the Silver Lakehouse may contain inconsistencies, missing values, or errors, adversely impacting downstream ML and analytics outcomes.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Implement automated data validation and cleansing pipelines using Azure Data Factory or Fabric Notebooks.
- Enforce schema validation and data profiling at ingestion points.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- Quarantine suspect data and notify data stewards for manual review and correction.
- Maintain versioned backups of ingested datasets for rollback.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Medium.** Automated checks reduce frequency, but some data issues may require manual intervention.

### 10.3.2 Risk: Data Workflow Failure or Job Orchestration Errors

**Description:**  
Failures in data pipeline execution or orchestration (e.g., due to dependency failures or resource contention) can disrupt the end-to-end workflow.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Adopt robust error handling and retry policies in all orchestration logic.
- Use Azure Monitor and Log Analytics to track job status and trigger alerts on failure.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- Manual rerun procedures for failed jobs.
- Escalation matrix for critical workflow disruptions.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Low.** Well-designed orchestration and monitoring minimize the risk of prolonged workflow disruption.

## 10.4 Machine Learning Component Risks

### 10.4.1 Risk: Model Drift and Performance Degradation

**Description:**  
Deployed ML models may become less accurate over time as data patterns change, leading to unreliable predictions.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Schedule periodic model retraining and validation using fresh data from the Silver Lakehouse.
- Monitor model performance metrics and set thresholds for automatic retraining triggers.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- Roll back to previous model versions if performance drops below acceptable thresholds.
- Notify data science team for root-cause analysis and remediation.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Medium.** Automated monitoring and retraining reduce risk, but some drift may go undetected between cycles.

### 10.4.2 Risk: Inadequate Model Governance and Versioning

**Description:**  
Without systematic model registration and lifecycle management, there is a risk of deploying outdated or unapproved models.

**Mitigation Strategy:**  
- Register all trained models in the Fabric Model Registry, enforcing version control and metadata tagging.
- Require peer review and approval before production deployment.

**Contingency Plan:**  
- Maintain audit trails for all model deployments.
- Immediate rollback procedures for problematic models.

**Residual Risk Assessment:**  
- **Low.** Adhering to model registry and approval workflows ensures governance.

## 10.5 Summary Table: Key Risks and Mitigation Overview

| Risk Area             | Risk Description                              | Mitigation Strategy                       | Contingency Plan                      | Residual Risk |
|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------|
| Infrastructure        | Network connectivity disruption               | NSG rules, monitoring, failover gateway   | Manual transfer, secondary VM         | Low           |
| Infrastructure        | VM sizing/resource exhaustion                 | Sizing best practices, autoscaling        | Rapid resize, performance reviews      | Medium        |
| Security              | No Conditional Access                        | Tenant-wide MFA, access reviews           | Account disablement, incident response| Medium        |
| Security              | Data exfiltration                            | Strict NSG, Defender for Cloud            | Resource isolation, breach protocol   | Low           |
| Data Workflow         | Data quality/integrity issues                 | Automated validation, schema checks       | Quarantine, dataset rollback          | Medium        |
| Data Workflow         | Workflow/job orchestration errors             | Error handling, monitoring                | Manual rerun, escalation               | Low           |
| ML Component          | Model drift/performance degradation           | Scheduled retraining, monitoring          | Rollback, data science review         | Medium        |
| ML Component          | Model governance/versioning gaps              | Model registry, peer review               | Audit trails, rollback                | Low           |

## 10.6 Limitations and Residual Risks

While the above measures align with Microsoft’s recommended practices and provide robust mitigation, certain limitations persist:

- Full Conditional Access enforcement is currently not feasible due to licensing constraints. This limits the granularity of access controls.
- Manual intervention remains necessary for some data quality and model drift scenarios.
- Autoscaling and monitoring reduce but do not eliminate the risk of resource exhaustion during unforeseen spikes.

We recommend periodic review of risk posture, especially following major architectural or workload changes, and advocate for continuous improvement in line with evolving Microsoft best practices and organizational requirements.

The diagram illustrates a technical architecture involving Azure components and user access management. 

1. **Fabric-RG**: This represents a Resource Group in Azure, which is a container that holds related resources for an Azure solution. It is labeled as "Fabric-RG," indicating it is part of a fabric infrastructure.

2. **UMS Fabric**: This component likely refers to a User Management System within the fabric infrastructure. It is responsible for managing user identities, roles, and permissions within the system.

3. **Conditional Access**: This is a security feature in Azure Active Directory that controls access to resources based on specific conditions. It ensures that only authenticated and authorized users can access the system.

4. **External User**: Represents users outside the organization who need access to the system. These users are subject to conditional access policies to ensure secure and controlled access.

**Data Flow and Connections**:
- The diagram shows a direct connection from the "UMS Fabric" to the "External User" through "Conditional Access." This indicates that external users are authenticated and authorized via conditional access policies before they can interact with the resources managed by the UMS Fabric.
- The flow suggests a security layer that evaluates user credentials and access conditions, ensuring that only legitimate external users can access the fabric infrastructure.

Overall, the diagram highlights the integration of user management and security protocols to facilitate secure external access to Azure resources.

The image is a technical architecture diagram.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**: The process begins with a user attempting authentication to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**: The authentication request is evaluated by Entra conditional access policies. This involves three key steps:
   - **Signal**: Various signals are gathered, such as user and group information, and network location.
   - **Decision**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access.
   - **Enforcement**: The decision is enforced, determining whether access is granted or denied.

3. **Inbound to Microsoft Fabric**: If access is granted, the request proceeds inbound to Microsoft Fabric.

4. **Microsoft Fabric Components**: Within Microsoft Fabric, several components are available, including:
   - Data integration and analytics tools.
   - OneLake, which serves as a unified data lake for storage and management.

This diagram illustrates the flow of authentication and access control, highlighting the integration of security policies with Microsoft Fabric's data and analytics capabilities.

The diagram represents a data processing and analytics architecture within the UMS Fabric environment. It illustrates the flow of data from ingestion to visualization using various Azure components.

1. **Data Ingestion**:
   - **Source**: Data originates from the UMS Staging Area, specifically a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse.
   - **Data Factory**: Azure Data Factory is used to ingest data from the SQL Server into the UMS Fabric.

2. **Data Transformation and Storage**:
   - **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: Initial storage and transformation of raw data into Bronze tables.
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Further processing and refinement of data into Silver tables, indicating a higher level of data quality and readiness for analysis.
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: Final transformation into Gold tables, representing the most refined and business-ready data.

3. **Data Processing**:
   - **Data Factory**: Utilized again for orchestrating data movement and transformation between the different layers (Bronze, Silver, Gold).
   - **Data Science**: Involves advanced analytics and machine learning processes applied to the data, likely using Azure Machine Learning or similar services.

4. **Identity Management**:
   - **Identity_SM**: Represents identity and security management, ensuring data access and governance within the architecture.

5. **Data Storage and Access**:
   - **OneLake**: Acts as a unified data lake storage solution, facilitating seamless data access and integration across the architecture.

6. **Data Visualization**:
   - **Power BI**: The final component where data is visualized and analyzed, connected via OneLake for real-time insights and reporting.

This architecture supports efficient data processing, transformation, and visualization, leveraging Azure services to ensure scalability, security, and performance.

The diagram illustrates a data flow architecture involving three main components:

1. **UMS Staging Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse**: This component represents the on-premises data storage system where data is initially staged. It is likely used for storing and processing large volumes of data before further operations.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**: This gateway facilitates secure communication between the on-premises data warehouse and external services. It enables data to be securely transferred without requiring inbound ports, ensuring that the internal network remains protected from external threats. The gateway handles outbound secure connections, allowing data to flow from the SQL Server to external services.

3. **UMS Fabric**: This component represents an external service or platform that interacts with the on-premises data warehouse through the OPDG. The UMS Fabric likely provides additional processing, analytics, or integration capabilities, leveraging the data from the SQL Server.

The data flow begins at the SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse, where data is staged and stored. It then moves through the On-Premise Data Gateway, which securely transmits the data to the UMS Fabric. The gateway ensures that the connection is outbound and secure, eliminating the need for inbound ports and enhancing security. The UMS Fabric processes or utilizes the data for further operations, analytics, or integration tasks.

The diagram represents a data processing and machine learning workflow within an Azure environment, utilizing UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and integration.

1. **Data Source**: 
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: These tables serve as the initial data source, containing pre-processed data ready for machine learning experiments.

2. **Machine Learning Processes**:
   - **AutoML**: 
     - **AutoML Experiment**: Automatically conducts machine learning experiments on the data from XX_Silver_LK Tables.
     - **Result Scoring (Using Notebook)**: The results from the AutoML experiments are scored using a notebook, which likely involves evaluating model performance metrics.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**:
     - **Modelling (Using Notebook)**: Allows for manual creation and training of machine learning models using notebooks, providing flexibility for custom model development.

3. **Data Transformation and Storage**:
   - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: The output from both AutoML and Manual Machine Learning processes is stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables, representing refined and scored data ready for further analysis or reporting.
   - **Identity_SM**: This component likely handles identity management or security measures for accessing the XX_Gold_LK Tables.

4. **Data Visualization and Reporting**:
   - **Power BI**: The XX_Gold_LK Tables are connected to Power BI via OneLake, enabling visualization and reporting of the processed data for business intelligence purposes.

5. **Integration**:
   - **UMS Fabric**: Provides the overarching framework for managing and integrating the components within the Azure environment.
   - **OneLake**: Facilitates seamless data connectivity and integration between the storage tables and Power BI.

The diagram illustrates a comprehensive workflow for data processing, machine learning, and business intelligence within an Azure ecosystem, leveraging automated and manual machine learning techniques, secure data storage, and powerful visualization tools.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.
