# 4.0 Infrastructure and Platform Architecture

This section delineates the comprehensive infrastructure and platform architecture for the UMS solution, encompassing Microsoft Fabric, OneLake, Data Factory, and the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). The architecture is designed for optimal scalability, high availability, data governance, and compliance, in accordance with Microsoft best practices. The following subsections provide detailed explanations of each architectural component, the data flow across Lakehouse layers, VM sizing and specifications, network connectivity, and naming conventions.

## 4.1 Architectural Overview

The UMS platform leverages a hybrid architecture, integrating on-premises data sources with cloud-native Microsoft Fabric services. The solution is structured to facilitate secure, scalable, and efficient data ingestion, transformation, and analytics, with a clear demarcation of data processing stages (Bronze, Silver, Gold Lakehouse layers). The architecture is depicted in the following diagram, which illustrates the end-to-end data workflow from ingestion to visualization.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_009_img_003.png)

### 4.1.1 Key Components

- **UMS Staging Area (SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse):** Serves as the primary data repository for raw operational data. Data is prepared for extraction and ingestion into the cloud environment.
- **On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG):** Acts as a secure bridge between the on-premises SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric, enabling outbound-only connectivity to prevent exposure of inbound ports, thereby adhering to Microsoft’s security best practices.
- **Microsoft Data Factory:** Orchestrates data pipelines for scheduled and automated data ingestion, transformation, and movement between Lakehouse layers.
- **Microsoft Fabric Lakehouse (Bronze, Silver, Gold):** Implements a multi-layered data processing architecture, ensuring data quality, traceability, and readiness for analytics.
- **OneLake:** Provides unified, scalable, and secure data storage, facilitating seamless access and integration across analytics workloads.
- **Power BI & Semantic Model:** Enables advanced data modeling and visualization, sourcing refined data from the Gold Lakehouse via OneLake.

## 4.2 Data Flow and Lakehouse Layering

UMS shall implement a three-tier Lakehouse architecture, in line with Microsoft’s recommended data engineering best practices. This approach ensures data integrity, auditability, and optimized processing for analytics and machine learning.

### 4.2.1 Data Flow Process

| ID    | Description                                                                                          | Workload Type     |
|-------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|
| DC01  | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                 | Data Factory      |
| DC02  | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                | Data Engineering  |
| DC03  | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the Bronze Lakehouse.                     | Data Engineering  |
| DC04  | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the Silver Lakehouse.                    | Data Engineering  |
| DC05  | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook, and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering  |
| DC06  | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from Bronze to Silver or Silver to Gold). | Data Factory      |
| DC07  | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse. | Data Science      |
| DC08  | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.         | Power BI          |
| DC09  | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.              | Power BI          |

### 4.2.2 Lakehouse Layer Definitions

- **Bronze Layer (XX_Bronze_LK):** Stores raw, untransformed data as ingested. This layer provides full traceability and serves as the immutable source for downstream processing.
- **Silver Layer (XX_Silver_LK):** Contains cleansed and standardized data. Dataflows, notebooks, and visual queries are applied to ensure quality and consistency, preparing the data for advanced analytics.
- **Gold Layer (XX_Gold_LK):** Holds highly refined, aggregated, and business-ready data. This layer is optimized for reporting, machine learning, and semantic modeling.

UMS shall enforce strict data lineage and audit controls across these layers, ensuring every transformation is documented and reversible.

## 4.3 Staging Area and On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG)

### 4.3.1 Staging Area

The UMS Staging Area is implemented as a Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse. This environment is responsible for the initial collection, cleansing, and preparation of operational data. Data is extracted periodically and securely transmitted to the cloud using the OPDG.

### 4.3.2 On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) Design

The OPDG is deployed within a dedicated virtual machine, configured to facilitate secure, outbound-only connectivity between the on-premises SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric. This design eliminates the need for inbound port exposure, significantly reducing the attack surface and aligning with Microsoft’s security guidance.

#### VM Sizing and Specifications

| Component        | Specification                         |
|------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Server 2019    |
| .NET Framework   | Version 4.8                           |
| vCPU             | 8 cores                               |
| RAM              | Minimum 8 GB                          |

UMS shall provision the VM with sufficient resources to ensure reliable performance and accommodate future scaling requirements.

## 4.4 Network Connectivity

UMS shall implement a secure network architecture, ensuring all data transfers between on-premises and cloud environments are encrypted and authenticated. The OPDG utilizes outbound connections, eliminating the need for inbound firewall rules and adhering to Microsoft’s Zero Trust principles.

### 4.4.1 Network Ports

| Component          | Port/Protocol        | Direction   | Purpose                         |
|--------------------|---------------------|-------------|----------------------------------|
| OPDG Agent         | TCP 443 (HTTPS)     | Outbound    | Secure data transfer to Fabric   |
| SQL Server         | TCP 1433            | Local       | Data extraction (internal only)  |

UMS shall regularly review and update firewall rules to ensure only essential ports are open, minimizing exposure and maintaining compliance.

## 4.5 Platform Deployment, Scalability, and High Availability

UMS shall adopt Microsoft’s recommended deployment patterns to ensure the platform is resilient, scalable, and highly available:

- **Fabric and OneLake:** Deployed as managed services, automatically scaling to accommodate workload demands. UMS shall leverage autoscaling features and monitor utilization via Azure Monitor.
- **Data Factory Pipelines:** Designed for parallel execution and robust error handling. Pipelines are scheduled and monitored to ensure timely data processing.
- **OPDG:** VM is provisioned with high availability in mind, utilizing Azure Availability Sets or on-premises clustering as appropriate.
- **Disaster Recovery:** UMS shall implement backup and restore procedures for critical data, leveraging Azure Backup and SQL Server native tools.

## 4.6 Governance, Compliance, and Limitations

### 4.6.1 Governance

UMS shall establish clear governance policies for data access, retention, and lifecycle management. Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across all platform components, ensuring only authorized personnel can access sensitive data and perform administrative actions.

#### Roles and Permissions

| Role              | Responsibility                                   | Access Level                  |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Data Engineer     | Pipeline development, data transformation        | Bronze, Silver Lakehouse      |
| Data Scientist    | Advanced analytics, ML modeling                  | Silver, Gold Lakehouse        |
| BI Analyst        | Reporting, dashboard creation                    | Gold Lakehouse, Power BI      |
| Administrator     | Platform configuration, security management      | All components                |

### 4.6.2 Compliance

UMS shall adhere to organizational and regulatory compliance requirements, including data residency, privacy, and auditability. All data transfers are encrypted, and audit logs are maintained for critical operations.

### 4.6.3 Limitations

- **OPDG Throughput:** The OPDG may become a bottleneck if data volumes exceed VM capacity. UMS shall monitor performance and scale resources as needed.
- **Latency:** Outbound-only connectivity may introduce latency during peak data transfer periods.
- **Fabric Integration:** Some advanced analytics features may require additional licensing or configuration within Microsoft Fabric.

## 4.7 Naming Conventions

UMS shall implement standardized naming conventions for all infrastructure components, facilitating clarity, maintainability, and compliance with Microsoft’s recommended practices.

| Component           | Naming Convention Example              | Description                                 |
|---------------------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Lakehouse Layer     | XX_Bronze_LK, XX_Silver_LK, XX_Gold_LK| Prefix (XX) + Layer + Suffix (LK)           |
| Data Gateway VM     | ums-opdg-prod-01                      | Project + Component + Environment + Index   |
| Data Factory        | ums-df-prod                           | Project + Component + Environment           |
| OneLake Workspace   | ums-onelake-prod                      | Project + Component + Environment           |
| Power BI Dataset    | ums-gold-semantic                     | Project + Layer + Purpose                   |

UMS shall maintain a naming registry and enforce naming standards across all deployments.

---

In summary, the UMS infrastructure and platform architecture is meticulously designed to align with Microsoft best practices, ensuring security, scalability, and operational excellence. Each component is governed by strict policies and naming conventions, with robust mechanisms for data processing, access control, and compliance. The architecture is engineered to support current and future analytics workloads, delivering reliable insights for business decision-making.

# 4.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Infrastructure

## 4.1.1 Overview

Microsoft’s recommended practices for deploying critical Azure services—namely Azure Fabric, Azure Data Factory, and Azure Data Gateway—are founded upon principles of security, scalability, operational efficiency, and compliance. UMS shall implement these practices to ensure a robust, future-proof infrastructure that aligns with both Microsoft’s authoritative guidance and the enterprise’s governance standards. This section delineates the architectural approach, referencing official Microsoft documentation and elaborating on the rationale for each recommendation.

## 4.1.2 Resource Group Organization

Resource groups are the fundamental organizational units within Azure, providing logical boundaries for resource management, access control, and lifecycle operations. Microsoft advises that resource groups be structured to reflect application boundaries, environment separation (production, staging, development), and operational ownership.

We recommend the following organizational schema:

| Resource Group Name         | Purpose                                  | Environment | Ownership    | Naming Convention Example      |
|----------------------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------------------|
| rg-fabric-prod             | Azure Fabric resources for production     | Production  | Infra Team   | rg-[service]-[env]            |
| rg-datafactory-dev         | Data Factory resources for development    | Development | Data Team    | rg-[service]-[env]            |
| rg-datagateway-shared      | Shared Data Gateway resources             | Shared      | Integration  | rg-[service]-[scope]          |

**Rationale:**  
This structure facilitates granular access control, streamlined resource lifecycle management, and clear cost allocation. By aligning resource groups with environments and ownership, UMS ensures that resources are isolated, reducing blast radius and simplifying compliance reporting.

## 4.1.3 Region Selection

Microsoft recommends deploying resources in regions that are closest to the primary user base, while also considering compliance requirements, disaster recovery, and latency minimization. UMS shall select Azure regions based on the following criteria:

- **Data Residency:** Ensure compliance with local data sovereignty laws.
- **Availability Zones:** Leverage regions that support Availability Zones for enhanced resiliency.
- **Latency:** Optimize for minimal network latency to users and dependent services.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Pair regions for geo-redundancy as per Microsoft’s cross-region DR guidance.

| Service            | Primary Region      | Secondary Region (DR) | Availability Zone Support | Compliance Considerations    |
|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Azure Fabric       | Southeast Asia      | East Asia             | Yes                      | GDPR, PDPA                 |
| Data Factory       | Southeast Asia      | Australia East        | Yes                      | GDPR, PDPA                 |
| Data Gateway       | Southeast Asia      | N/A                   | N/A                      | GDPR, PDPA                 |

**Rationale:**  
Selecting regions based on these criteria ensures high availability, regulatory compliance, and optimal performance. Microsoft’s official documentation underscores the importance of region selection in achieving enterprise-grade reliability and compliance.

## 4.1.4 Use of Managed Identities

Microsoft strongly advocates the use of Azure Managed Identities for secure, passwordless authentication between Azure services. Managed identities eliminate the need for hard-coded credentials, thereby reducing security risks and operational overhead.

| Service            | Managed Identity Type   | Purpose                                      | Microsoft Reference          |
|--------------------|------------------------|----------------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Azure Fabric       | System-assigned        | Secure access to Key Vault, Storage, etc.    | [MS Docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/managed-identities-azure-resources/) |
| Data Factory       | User-assigned          | Flexible identity reuse across pipelines      | [MS Docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/data-factory/data-factory-managed-identity)           |
| Data Gateway       | System-assigned        | Authentication for data movement             | [MS Docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-bi/service-gateway-mfa)                              |

**Rationale:**  
Managed identities are a cornerstone of Microsoft’s security model for cloud-native applications. They provide seamless integration with Azure Active Directory, support least-privilege access, and simplify credential management. UMS shall implement managed identities for all eligible resources, in accordance with Microsoft’s security best practices.

## 4.1.5 Alignment with Enterprise Standards

UMS’s enterprise architecture mandates adherence to Microsoft’s recommended practices, supplemented by internal governance policies. All deployments shall:

- Utilize standardized naming conventions for resources, as detailed in section 4.4.
- Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) at resource group and subscription levels.
- Implement tagging for cost management, ownership, and compliance tracking.
- Integrate resources with centralized monitoring and logging solutions.

| Monitoring            | Yes                              | EA-MON-2023                |

**Rationale:**  
By harmonizing Microsoft’s best practices with UMS’s enterprise standards, the solution achieves both technical excellence and organizational compliance.

# 4.2 Governance & Compliance for Infrastructure

## 4.2.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a robust governance and compliance framework for all infrastructure resources provisioned within the Microsoft Azure environment. This framework is designed to ensure operational consistency, enforce regulatory mandates, and optimize resource utilization. Governance controls are established to provide visibility, accountability, and traceability across the infrastructure lifecycle, aligned with Microsoft’s Cloud Adoption Framework and best practices.

## 4.2.2 Resource Tagging Strategy

Resource tagging is a foundational governance mechanism, enabling granular classification, tracking, and management of Azure resources. UMS shall enforce a standardized tagging schema to facilitate cost allocation, ownership identification, and lifecycle management. Tags are mandatory for all deployed resources, including compute, storage, networking, and platform services.

### Tagging Schema Table

| Tag Key         | Description                          | Example Value       | Purpose                        |
|-----------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------|---------------------------------|
| Environment     | Deployment environment               | Production, Dev     | Segregate workloads            |
| Owner           | Responsible team or individual       | FinanceDept         | Accountability and support      |
| CostCenter      | Financial tracking                   | CC12345             | Cost management and reporting   |
| Application     | Application or service name          | UMS-Portal          | Resource grouping               |
| DataClassification | Sensitivity level                 | Confidential        | Compliance and audit            |
| RetentionPolicy | Data retention requirements          | 7Years              | Regulatory adherence            |

We recommend enforcing tagging policies using Azure Policy to ensure compliance and prevent resource creation without the required tags.

## 4.2.3 Cost Management Controls

UMS shall utilize Azure Cost Management and Billing to monitor, analyze, and optimize infrastructure expenditure. Cost management controls include:

- **Budget Enforcement:** Define and monitor budgets for each subscription and resource group, triggering alerts when thresholds are approached or exceeded.
- **Resource Optimization:** Regularly review underutilized resources and recommend rightsizing or decommissioning.
- **Cost Allocation:** Use tagging to attribute costs to departments, projects, or applications, enabling chargeback and showback models.
- **Reporting:** Generate scheduled cost reports for stakeholders, supporting financial transparency and accountability.

These controls are critical for preventing cost overruns and ensuring resource efficiency, in accordance with Microsoft’s cost management best practices.

## 4.2.4 Audit Logging & Monitoring

UMS shall implement comprehensive audit logging to ensure traceability and accountability for all infrastructure activities. Azure Activity Logs and Azure Monitor are leveraged to capture and retain records of resource provisioning, configuration changes, and access events.

### Audit Logging Policy Table

| Log Source         | Retention Period | Access Control      | Compliance Requirement        |
|--------------------|-----------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Azure Activity Log | 7 years         | Restricted (SecOps) | Regulatory, UMS internal      |
| Resource Diagnostic Logs | 3 years   | Restricted (IT Ops) | Operational, troubleshooting   |
| Azure Security Center | 7 years      | Restricted (CISO)   | Security, incident response    |

Logs are stored in Azure Log Analytics with immutable retention policies, supporting forensic investigations and regulatory audits. Access to logs is tightly controlled and monitored, ensuring only authorized personnel can retrieve sensitive audit data.

## 4.2.5 Compliance with UMS and Regulatory Requirements

UMS infrastructure must comply with both internal policy and external regulatory mandates, including data residency, retention, and access control.

### Data Residency

UMS shall deploy all infrastructure resources within Azure regions that meet data residency requirements specified by regulatory authorities and UMS policy. Data location is enforced using Azure Policy, preventing resource deployment in non-compliant regions.

### Data Retention

Retention policies are defined for logs, backups, and data repositories, as outlined in the table above. UMS shall ensure that all data is retained for the mandated period and deleted securely upon expiry, using automated lifecycle management.

### Access Control

Access to infrastructure resources is governed by strict role-based access control (RBAC) and privileged identity management (PIM). Only authorized users are granted access based on least privilege principles.

#### Access Control Table

| Role                  | Permissions                   | Assignment Scope        | Approval Required |
|-----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Owner                 | Full control                 | Subscription           | Yes               |
| Contributor           | Create/update resources       | Resource Group         | Yes               |
| Reader                | View resources                | Resource Group         | No                |
| Security Admin        | Manage security settings      | Subscription           | Yes               |
| Audit Reviewer        | Access audit logs             | Log Analytics Workspace| Yes               |

We recommend periodic access reviews and enforcement of Just-In-Time (JIT) access for privileged roles, in accordance with Microsoft’s security best practices.

## 4.2.6 Governance Limitations

While Azure provides extensive governance capabilities, UMS must recognize certain limitations:

- **Tagging Limit:** Azure supports up to 50 tags per resource, but tag values are not enforced for uniqueness.
- **Policy Enforcement:** Azure Policy may not retroactively apply to existing resources; periodic audits are required.
- **Data Residency:** Not all Azure services are available in every region; architectural adjustments may be necessary.
- **Audit Log Retention:** Longer retention periods may incur additional storage costs; UMS should balance compliance with budget.

## 4.2.7 Naming Conventions

UMS shall implement Azure naming conventions to promote clarity, consistency, and discoverability. Naming standards are enforced via Azure Policy and documented for all resource types.

### Naming Convention Table

| Resource Type      | Naming Pattern                  | Example                |
|--------------------|---------------------------------|------------------------|
| Resource Group     | <Env>-<App>-RG                  | prod-umsportal-RG      |
| VM                 | <Env>-<App>-<Location>-VM       | dev-umsportal-eus-VM   |
| Storage Account    | <Env><App>stg                   | produmsportalstg       |
| Log Analytics      | <Env>-<App>-LA                  | prod-umsportal-LA      |

Adhering to these conventions facilitates automated management, reduces operational errors, and supports compliance audits.

## 4.2.8 Conclusion

By rigorously applying governance controls, UMS ensures that infrastructure resources are managed in accordance with Microsoft Best Practices, internal policies, and external regulatory requirements. These measures collectively enhance operational integrity, cost efficiency, and compliance posture, forming the foundation for secure and sustainable cloud operations.

# 4.3 Limitations of Infrastructure Design

The infrastructure design proposed for the Unified Management System (UMS) is architected to align with Microsoft’s best practices for reliability, security, and scalability. Nonetheless, certain technical and operational limitations are inherent in the current approach due to platform constraints, architectural decisions, and organizational requirements. It is imperative to identify these limitations, understand their implications, and define mitigation strategies to ensure the solution remains robust and adaptable.

## 4.3.1 Lack of Private Link Support

The current infrastructure does not leverage Azure Private Link for secure, private connectivity to platform services such as Azure Storage, SQL Database, or Key Vault. As a result, service endpoints are exposed over the public internet, increasing the attack surface and potentially violating corporate security policies.

**Implications:**
- Data in transit may be susceptible to interception or eavesdropping.
- Regulatory compliance requirements (e.g., GDPR, ISO 27001) may not be fully satisfied.
- Increased reliance on Network Security Groups (NSGs) and firewalls to restrict access.

**Mitigation Strategy:**
We recommend a phased adoption of Azure Private Link for all critical platform services. This would ensure traffic between UMS and Azure resources remains within the Microsoft backbone network, significantly reducing exposure to external threats. Where Private Link cannot be immediately implemented, strict NSG rules and Azure Firewall policies must be enforced, and all public endpoints should be monitored using Azure Security Center.

## 4.3.2 Dependency on VM-Based Gateways

The design currently utilizes VM-based network gateways for site-to-site connectivity and traffic routing. While this approach offers flexibility and control, it introduces operational overhead and scalability constraints.

**Implications:**
- Manual patching and maintenance of gateway VMs are required, increasing operational burden.
- Scaling up to accommodate increased traffic necessitates manual intervention and potential downtime.
- VM-based gateways may not support advanced features such as auto-scaling or seamless integration with Azure-native networking.

**Mitigation Strategy:**
We recommend transitioning to Azure-native solutions such as Azure VPN Gateway or Azure Application Gateway, which offer managed services with built-in scalability, high availability, and automated patching. For environments where VM-based gateways are unavoidable, implement automation scripts for patching and scaling, and ensure robust monitoring is in place to detect performance bottlenecks.

## 4.3.3 Scalability Constraints

The current infrastructure is provisioned based on projected workloads and does not fully leverage Azure’s autoscaling capabilities. This may lead to resource exhaustion during peak demand or inefficient utilization during off-peak periods.

**Implications:**
- Potential for service degradation or outages during unexpected traffic spikes.
- Increased operational costs due to over-provisioning of resources.
- Manual scaling increases risk of configuration errors.

**Mitigation Strategy:**
UMS shall implement Azure Autoscale for all compute and networking resources where supported, including Virtual Machine Scale Sets and Azure App Service Plans. Autoscale policies must be defined based on historical usage patterns and business requirements, ensuring optimal performance and cost efficiency. Additionally, Azure Monitor and Application Insights should be used to proactively track resource utilization and trigger scaling actions.

## 4.3.4 Operational Limitations

Certain operational limitations arise from the current design, particularly around backup, disaster recovery, and monitoring.

**Implications:**
- Backup and restore operations may require manual intervention, increasing recovery times.
- Disaster recovery procedures are not fully automated, risking prolonged outages.
- Monitoring and alerting are not uniformly implemented across all infrastructure components.

**Mitigation Strategy:**
We recommend adopting Azure Backup and Azure Site Recovery for automated backup and disaster recovery. All infrastructure components must be integrated with Azure Monitor and Log Analytics, with standardized alerting policies to ensure rapid response to incidents.

## 4.3.5 Summary Table of Limitations and Mitigations

| Limitation                     | Impact                                      | Mitigation Strategy                                      |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| Lack of Private Link Support   | Increased attack surface, compliance risk   | Adopt Azure Private Link; enforce NSG and firewall rules |
| VM-Based Gateway Dependency    | Operational overhead, scalability limits    | Transition to Azure-native gateways; automate maintenance|
| Scalability Constraints        | Service degradation, cost inefficiency      | Implement Azure Autoscale; use Azure Monitor             |
| Operational Limitations        | Slow backup/recovery, inconsistent monitoring| Use Azure Backup/Site Recovery; integrate with Monitor   |

## 4.3.6 Governance and Best Practices

UMS shall implement governance policies to periodically review infrastructure limitations and mitigation measures. Adherence to Microsoft’s Well-Architected Framework is mandatory, ensuring ongoing alignment with best practices for security, reliability, performance efficiency, and cost optimization. Regular architectural reviews and audits shall be conducted to identify emerging risks and opportunities for improvement.

## 4.3.7 Limitations on Naming Conventions

While Azure naming conventions are enforced for resource consistency and discoverability, certain legacy systems or third-party integrations may not support strict compliance. This may lead to inconsistencies in resource naming, impacting automation and management.

**Mitigation Strategy:**
We recommend maintaining a centralized naming convention registry and enforcing naming standards through Azure Policy wherever possible. Exceptions must be documented and reviewed quarterly.

In summary, while the infrastructure design is robust and aligned with Microsoft best practices, the identified limitations must be addressed through strategic mitigation measures to ensure long-term reliability, security, and scalability.

# 4.4 Naming Conventions for Infrastructure

## 4.4.1 Overview

UMS shall implement standardized naming conventions across all Microsoft Fabric infrastructure resources to promote consistency, clarity, and operational efficiency. Adhering to Microsoft’s recommended naming patterns, as well as UMS-specific policies, ensures that resources are easily identifiable, scalable, and manageable. Proper naming conventions are foundational for governance, automation, and compliance, particularly in environments with multiple teams and complex workloads.

## 4.4.2 Microsoft Best Practices

Microsoft advocates for resource naming conventions that are:
- Predictable and consistent across environments and resource types.
- Readable, using clear delimiters and avoiding ambiguous abbreviations.
- Aligned with organizational context, such as business unit, environment, and purpose.
- Unique within the scope of the resource type to prevent conflicts and facilitate automation.
- Short enough to comply with Azure and Fabric resource character limits, yet descriptive.

UMS shall adopt these principles, tailoring them to the specific requirements of its Student Affairs and related workloads.

## 4.4.3 Governance and Policy Alignment

Naming conventions are a critical element of resource governance. They enable:
- Automated resource discovery and inventory management.
- Enforcement of security and compliance policies.
- Efficient troubleshooting and support operations.
- Seamless integration with monitoring, auditing, and tagging frameworks.

UMS shall enforce naming conventions through Azure Policy and Fabric governance controls, ensuring all resources are provisioned in accordance with the prescribed patterns.

## 4.4.4 Standard Naming Structure

The standardized naming structure for UMS Fabric resources is as follows:

`<Identity>-<Scenario/Activity>-<FabricItemName>`

Where:
- **Identity** refers to the business unit, project, or function (e.g., StudentAffairs).
- **Scenario/Activity** denotes the environment or operational context (e.g., dev, Bronze, Silver, Gold).
- **FabricItemName** identifies the specific resource type (e.g., Capacity, Workspace, Lakehouse).

This structure supports both human readability and programmatic parsing, facilitating automation and governance.

## 4.4.5 Naming Convention Table

The following table details the naming conventions for each Fabric resource type:

| No. | Item in Fabric      | Naming Convention Example                       |
|-----|---------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| 1   | Fabric Capacity     | stagingareadevcapacity01                        |
| 2   | Workspace           | StudentAffairs_dev_WS                           |
| 3   | Lakehouse           | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_LK<br>Etc… |
| 4   | Dataflow            | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF<br>StudentAffairs_Silver_to_Gold_DF<br>Etc.. |
| 5   | Semantic Model      | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM            |
| 6   | Pipeline            | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL<br>Etc..          |
| 7   | Report              | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report        |

### Key Guidelines:
- **Delimiter Usage:** Underscores (“_”) separate logical components; hyphens (“-”) are used for environment or activity markers.
- **Environment Indicator:** Include ‘dev’, ‘test’, ‘prod’ as appropriate for non-production and production resources.
- **Resource Type Suffix:** Use standardized suffixes (e.g., WS for Workspace, LK for Lakehouse, DF for Dataflow, SM for Semantic Model, PL for Pipeline, Report for Reports) to clarify resource type.
- **Scalability:** Numbering (e.g., 01, 02) is appended where multiple instances are expected.
- **Avoid Reserved Words:** Ensure names do not conflict with Azure or Fabric reserved keywords.

## 4.4.6 Limitations

While the naming convention is designed for clarity and scalability, certain limitations must be acknowledged:
- **Character Limits:** Azure and Fabric resources impose maximum character limits (typically 64 characters). Names must be concise yet descriptive.
- **Global Uniqueness:** Some resource types (e.g., capacities) require globally unique names; collision checks must be performed before provisioning.
- **Automation Constraints:** Automated deployment scripts must validate naming compliance to prevent errors and ensure seamless integration.

## 4.4.7 Enforcement and Compliance

UMS shall enforce naming conventions through:
- Azure Policy for resource group and resource-level naming.
- Fabric governance controls for workspace and pipeline naming.
- Periodic audits to ensure compliance and remediate deviations.

Failure to adhere to naming conventions may result in operational inefficiencies, security risks, and governance gaps.

## 4.4.8 Conclusion

A robust, standardized naming convention is essential for effective management of Microsoft Fabric resources. By aligning with Microsoft best practices and UMS governance requirements, we ensure that all infrastructure components are easily identifiable, scalable, and compliant. This approach supports operational excellence, security, and long-term sustainability of the UMS data platform.

# 5.0 Networking and Security Architecture

## 5.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a robust networking and security architecture to ensure secure, compliant, and reliable access to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI environments. This section details the design principles, access control mechanisms, authentication flows, and outbound security rules for the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) Virtual Machine (VM). The solution adheres to Microsoft best practices, emphasizing governance, compliance, and operational integrity. Special attention is given to Conditional Access, MFA enforcement, and the limitations inherent in the current infrastructure, such as the absence of Private Link.

## 5.2 Access Control and Authentication

UMS’s access control strategy leverages Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) to enforce authentication and authorization policies. While Conditional Access is recommended for granular policy enforcement, current licensing constraints restrict its application to tenant-wide Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA).

### 5.2.1 User Authentication Flow

The authentication process for accessing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI is governed by Entra ID Conditional Access policies, where applicable, and MFA enforcement at the tenant level. The flow ensures that only authorized users can access sensitive resources, mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_002.png)

**Explanation:**  
The diagram above illustrates the authentication flow:
- Users initiate authentication requests to Microsoft Fabric.
- Entra Conditional Access evaluates signals (user identity, group membership, network location).
- Decisions are made based on risk, compliance, and policy requirements.
- Enforcement ensures access is granted only if all conditions are satisfied, otherwise additional authentication (MFA) is required.

### 5.2.2 Conditional Access and MFA Enforcement

Given the current licensing (Entra ID Free), Conditional Access is not fully enabled for UMS accounts. Therefore, MFA is enforced at the tenant level to provide baseline security. This approach ensures that all users undergo additional verification, reducing the risk of credential compromise.

**Conditional Access Policy Table**

| No.  | Conditional Access Policy Description                                                                                                                                      |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| DD01 | Configure policy to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage                                                                  |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                                                                         |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification (MFA) to reduce unexpected login prompts                                                                                     |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                                                                       |

**Best Practice:**  
Microsoft recommends enforcing Conditional Access policies for all privileged and external users. Where licensing is limited, enforcing MFA at the tenant level is a minimum requirement for compliance and security.

**Governance & Compliance:**  
UMS shall maintain audit logs of authentication attempts and policy enforcement actions, ensuring traceability and compliance with regulatory requirements.

**Limitation:**  
Conditional Access is not available for all users due to Entra ID Free licensing. Private Link is not implemented, exposing resources to public endpoints. These limitations are acknowledged and shall be addressed in future phases.

## 5.3 Network Security: OPDG VM Outbound Rules

The OPDG VM acts as a secure bridge between the on-premises SQL Server data warehouse and Microsoft Fabric. Network security is enforced through Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs), restricting outbound traffic to only essential Microsoft endpoints.

### 5.3.1 Outbound Security Rule Configuration

UMS shall configure outbound rules to allow only necessary traffic for data transfer, authentication, and connectivity verification. This minimizes the attack surface and ensures compliance with Microsoft’s zero-trust networking principles.

| Allow_OPDG_Authentication | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses| *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |

## 5.4 Security Governance and Compliance

UMS shall implement a governance framework aligned with Microsoft’s Cloud Adoption Framework. Key elements include:

# 5.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Security & Networking

## 5.1.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a comprehensive security and networking architecture grounded in Microsoft’s authoritative best practices. These recommendations are designed to ensure robust protection of the Fabric, Power BI, and Data Gateway components, while enabling secure, scalable, and compliant operations. Our approach addresses identity management, network segmentation, access control, and secure deployment, referencing official Microsoft documentation and guidelines.

## 5.1.2 Identity and Access Management

Microsoft advocates for a Zero Trust security model, emphasizing strict identity verification, least privilege access, and continuous assessment. UMS shall enforce the following:

- **Conditional Access:** All access to Fabric, Power BI, and Data Gateway must be governed by Azure Active Directory (AAD) Conditional Access policies. These policies dynamically evaluate user context (location, device compliance, risk signals) and enforce controls such as requiring MFA or restricting access from untrusted networks.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** MFA shall be mandatory for all administrative and privileged users, and strongly recommended for all end-users accessing sensitive datasets or management portals. This mitigates credential theft and unauthorized access.
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** Access to resources will be strictly managed using RBAC, ensuring users are granted only the permissions necessary for their job functions. Administrative roles must be segregated, and privileged access regularly reviewed.

### Roles & Responsibilities Table

| Role                    | Permission Scope                  | MFA Required | Conditional Access | Description                                           |
|-------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| Global Administrator    | Full tenant control               | Yes          | Yes               | Manages Azure AD, Fabric, Power BI, Data Gateway      |
| Power BI Admin          | Power BI service administration   | Yes          | Yes               | Manages workspaces, datasets, gateways                |
| Data Gateway Manager    | Gateway configuration & monitoring| Yes          | Yes               | Installs, configures, and monitors Data Gateway       |
| Fabric User             | Workspace-level access            | Recommended  | Yes               | Consumes published reports and datasets               |
| External User           | Limited guest access              | Yes          | Yes               | Restricted access for partners or vendors             |

## 5.1.3 Network Segmentation & Secure Connectivity

Microsoft recommends segmenting networks to minimize attack surfaces and contain potential breaches. UMS shall implement:

- **Private Endpoints:** Where feasible, Fabric, Power BI, and Data Gateway services will be connected via Azure Private Endpoints, ensuring traffic remains within the trusted Azure backbone and is not exposed to the public internet.
- **Network Security Groups (NSGs):** NSGs will be used to tightly control inbound and outbound traffic to VMs and subnets hosting Data Gateway or related services. Only required ports and protocols will be permitted.
- **Virtual Network Integration:** Data Gateway deployments must be integrated into dedicated Azure Virtual Networks (VNets), enabling granular control and monitoring of data flows.
- **Firewall & Threat Protection:** Azure Firewall and Microsoft Defender for Cloud will be leveraged to monitor, log, and block suspicious activity.

### Network Ports & Outbound Rules Table

| Service         | Port(s)         | Protocol | Source/Destination | Allowed Purpose                      |
|-----------------|-----------------|----------|--------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Data Gateway    | 443             | TCP      | Gateway ↔ Power BI | Secure data transfer (HTTPS)         |
| Fabric Services | 443, 80         | TCP      | User ↔ Service     | User access, API calls               |
| Admin Portal    | 443             | TCP      | Admin ↔ Portal     | Management & configuration           |
| Monitoring      | 443             | TCP      | Service ↔ Azure Log| Telemetry, logging                   |

## 5.1.4 Least Privilege Access

UMS shall strictly adhere to the principle of least privilege, as recommended by Microsoft. This mandates:

- **Minimal Permissions:** Users and service accounts are granted only the permissions essential for their roles. Elevated privileges are temporary and auditable.
- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Access:** Administrative access is provisioned on-demand using Azure Privileged Identity Management (PIM), reducing persistent exposure.
- **Audit & Monitoring:** All access and configuration changes are logged and monitored. Azure Monitor and Sentinel will alert on anomalous activities.

## 5.1.5 Secure Deployment Guidelines

UMS shall reference Microsoft’s official documentation for secure deployment:

- **Fabric:** Deploy using ARM templates or Azure DevOps pipelines, ensuring repeatable, auditable provisioning. Apply security baselines and monitor compliance.
- **Power BI:** Enable tenant-level security settings, restrict sharing to trusted domains, and enforce data loss prevention (DLP) policies.
- **Data Gateway:** Install gateways on hardened, patched hosts, preferably within Azure or on-premises DMZs. Restrict gateway admin access and enable automatic updates.

## 5.1.6 Governance & Compliance

UMS shall establish governance controls aligned with Microsoft’s Cloud Adoption Framework:

- **Policy Enforcement:** Azure Policy will enforce tagging, resource location, and security configurations.
- **Compliance Monitoring:** Regular reviews against regulatory standards (e.g., GDPR, ISO 27001) using Microsoft Compliance Manager.
- **Documentation:** All security and networking configurations shall be documented, versioned, and reviewed quarterly.

## 5.1.7 Limitations

While Microsoft best practices provide a robust framework, UMS must recognize certain limitations:

- **Third-Party Integrations:** Some legacy or third-party applications may not support Conditional Access or Private Endpoints, requiring compensating controls.
- **User Experience:** Strict MFA and Conditional Access policies may impact user productivity; UMS shall balance security with operational needs.
- **Network Complexity:** Extensive segmentation and firewall rules may increase operational complexity and troubleshooting overhead.

## 5.1.8 Naming Conventions

UMS shall adopt Microsoft-recommended naming conventions for security and networking resources to ensure clarity and maintainability.

|-----------------------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------|

| Network Security Group| `<env>-nsg-<purpose>`               | prod-nsg-gateway          |
| Data Gateway          | `<env>-dg-<location>`               | prod-dg-singapore         |
| Power BI Workspace    | `<env>-pbw-<dept>`                  | prod-pbw-finance          |

# 5.2 Governance & Compliance for Security

## 5.2.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a comprehensive governance framework to ensure robust security, continuous compliance, and effective risk management across all solution components. This framework is grounded in Microsoft’s best practices, leveraging Azure-native tools and processes to enforce security policies, maintain audit trails, and uphold adherence to international standards such as ISO 27001, as well as relevant local regulatory requirements. The governance model is designed to provide clear accountability, transparent operations, and demonstrable compliance, thereby reducing risk and strengthening organizational trust.

## 5.2.2 Policy Enforcement

UMS will utilize Azure Policy and Azure Security Center to systematically enforce security controls across resources. These tools enable automated validation of resource configurations, ensuring that only compliant deployments are permitted. Policies shall be defined to cover data residency, encryption, network segmentation, and identity management.

- **Azure Policy**: Enables definition and assignment of policies at the subscription, resource group, or resource level. Policies are used to restrict non-compliant resource creation, enforce tagging, and require encryption-at-rest.
- **Security Center**: Provides continuous assessment of security posture, recommends remediation actions, and automates enforcement of baseline configurations.

The rationale for policy enforcement is to prevent misconfiguration, reduce the attack surface, and maintain a consistent security posture across all environments.

## 5.2.3 Audit Trails & Monitoring

To ensure accountability and traceability, UMS shall implement extensive logging and audit mechanisms using Azure Monitor, Azure Activity Logs, and Microsoft Sentinel.

- **Azure Monitor**: Aggregates logs and metrics from all resources, enabling real-time monitoring and alerting.
- **Activity Logs**: Capture all management operations, including resource creation, modification, and deletion.
- **Microsoft Sentinel**: Provides advanced security analytics, threat detection, and incident response capabilities.

Audit logs are retained according to regulatory requirements, with regular reviews conducted by designated security personnel. This ensures that all access and configuration changes are recorded and can be traced back to individual users or service principals.

UMS’s governance model is aligned with ISO 27001, ensuring that information security management systems are established, maintained, and continually improved. Additionally, compliance with local regulations (e.g., PDPA, GDPR) is achieved through:

Azure’s built-in compliance offerings and certifications provide assurance that underlying infrastructure meets stringent global standards.

# 5.3 Limitations of Security & Networking Design

## 5.3.1 Conditional Access Constraints

Based on our comprehensive assessment of the current UMS tenant, we have identified a significant limitation regarding the implementation of Conditional Access policies. The UMS account is provisioned under Microsoft Entra ID Free licensing, which does not support advanced Conditional Access features. Conditional Access is a cornerstone of modern identity security, enabling organizations to enforce granular access controls based on user context, device health, location, and risk signals. However, the absence of Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses precludes the configuration of targeted Conditional Access policies.

### Impact

- **Tenant-Wide MFA Enforcement:** In lieu of Conditional Access, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) has been enforced at the tenant level. While this provides a baseline of security, it lacks the flexibility to tailor authentication requirements to specific applications, user groups, or risk scenarios. All users are subject to uniform MFA prompts, which may lead to usability challenges and reduced user satisfaction.
- **Lack of Application-Specific Controls:** Without Conditional Access, UMS cannot restrict access to sensitive services (e.g., Power BI, Azure SQL Database, Azure Storage) based on device compliance or geographic location. This increases the risk of unauthorized access from unmanaged devices or untrusted locations.
- **Limited Risk Mitigation:** Conditional Access provides adaptive risk-based controls, such as blocking access for high-risk users or requiring additional verification. The current design cannot leverage these capabilities, potentially exposing the environment to credential compromise and lateral movement.

### Potential Future Improvements

We recommend that UMS evaluate the acquisition of Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 licenses to unlock Conditional Access functionality. This will enable the implementation of best-practice security controls, including:

- Application-specific MFA requirements
- Device compliance enforcement
- Location-based access restrictions
- Risk-based conditional policies

The following table summarizes the intended Conditional Access configurations and their current status:

| No.  | Conditional Access Policy Description                                                                                      | Current Status        |
|------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, Azure Storage | Not Implemented      |
| DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy                                                                          | Not Implemented      |
| DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts                                      | Tenant-wide MFA Only |
| DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment                                                                        | Allowed, No CA       |

## 5.3.2 Absence of Private Link Integration

A further limitation pertains to the networking architecture. Private Link, a critical Azure feature for securing service endpoints by enabling private connectivity from virtual networks, has not been implemented. Private Link mitigates exposure to public internet and enhances data exfiltration prevention by ensuring traffic remains within the Azure backbone.

### Impact

- **Exposure to Public Endpoints:** Without Private Link, services such as Azure Storage, SQL Database, and Power BI remain accessible via public endpoints. This increases the attack surface and may violate compliance requirements for regulated workloads.
- **Limited Network Segmentation:** The absence of Private Link restricts the ability to enforce network-level isolation and granular access controls, potentially leading to data leakage or unauthorized access.

### Potential Future Improvements

We recommend that UMS plan for phased adoption of Private Link, subject to infrastructure readiness and resource allocation. This will require careful planning to address DNS resolution, endpoint management, and integration with existing virtual networks.

## 5.3.3 Reliance on Tenant-Wide MFA

As previously noted, the enforced MFA is applied at the tenant level due to licensing constraints. While this approach provides a foundational security posture, it lacks the nuance required for differentiated access policies.

### Impact

- **User Experience:** Uniform MFA prompts may result in unnecessary friction for users accessing low-risk applications, potentially impacting productivity.
- **Security Gaps:** High-risk applications and privileged accounts are not afforded additional protections beyond tenant-wide MFA, leaving critical assets vulnerable to sophisticated threats.

### Potential Future Improvements

Upon upgrading to Entra ID P1/P2, UMS shall implement granular MFA policies aligned with Microsoft’s Zero Trust principles, ensuring that authentication requirements are proportional to risk and business context.

## 5.3.4 Governance and Compliance Considerations

The current security and networking design, constrained by licensing and architectural limitations, does not fully align with Microsoft’s best practices for governance and compliance. Specifically, the inability to implement Conditional Access and Private Link may impede UMS’s ability to meet regulatory standards for data protection and access control.

### Recommendations

- **License Upgrade:** Secure Entra ID P1/P2 licenses to enable Conditional Access.
- **Private Link Adoption:** Integrate Private Link for all supported Azure services.
- **Continuous Review:** Establish periodic reviews of security and networking controls to ensure alignment with evolving Microsoft best practices and regulatory requirements.

## 5.3.5 Summary Table: Security & Networking Limitations

| Limitation                  | Root Cause                  | Impact                                           | Recommended Improvement                  |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| No Conditional Access       | Entra ID Free License       | Cannot enforce granular access policies          | Upgrade to Entra ID P1/P2                |
| No Private Link             | Architectural Constraints   | Services exposed to public endpoints             | Plan phased Private Link implementation   |
| Tenant-wide MFA only        | Licensing limitation        | Uniform MFA prompts, no risk-based controls      | Implement granular MFA via Conditional Access |
| Limited Governance          | Policy/Technical limitation | May not meet regulatory requirements             | Periodic review and policy enhancement    |

By proactively addressing these limitations, UMS will be positioned to enhance its security posture, reduce risk exposure, and align with Microsoft’s recommended enterprise architecture standards.

# 5.4 Naming Conventions for Security & Networking

## 5.4.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a rigorous and standardized naming convention for all security and networking resources within the Azure environment. Adherence to well-defined naming standards is critical for ensuring operational consistency, traceability, and compliance with Microsoft’s best practices. Proper naming conventions facilitate efficient management, auditing, automation, and troubleshooting, especially as the environment scales and multiple teams interact with shared resources.

## 5.4.2 Microsoft Best Practices

Microsoft recommends that naming conventions be:

- **Consistent**: All resources should follow a predictable, standardized pattern to reduce ambiguity and prevent naming collisions.
- **Descriptive**: Names should clearly indicate the resource’s purpose, environment, and scope, enabling rapid identification.
- **Traceable**: Naming must support traceability for security reviews, incident response, and compliance audits.
- **Scalable**: Names should accommodate future expansion and new scenarios without requiring disruptive renaming.

UMS shall align its naming conventions with these principles to maximize manageability and governance.

## 5.4.3 Naming Standards for Security & Networking Resources

The following table defines the naming conventions for key security and networking resources. Each resource name shall incorporate relevant identifiers such as environment, scenario/activity, and resource type, ensuring clarity and traceability.

| Resource Type           | Naming Format Example                          | Description & Rationale                                                                                   |
|------------------------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Network Security Group  | `<Identity>-<Env>-NSG-<Location>-<Purpose>`    | E.g., `StudentAffairs-dev-NSG-eastus-web`.<br>Identity and environment ensure traceability; purpose clarifies scope. |
| Firewall Policy         | `<Identity>-<Env>-FWP-<Scenario>`              | E.g., `StudentAffairs-prod-FWP-dataprotect`.<br>Scenario denotes the policy’s specific function.          |
| Route Table             | `<Identity>-<Env>-RT-<VNetName>`               | E.g., `StudentAffairs-dev-RT-corevnet`.<br>VNetName links route tables to their associated networks.      |
| Private Endpoint        | `<Identity>-<Env>-PE-<ResourceType>-<Target>`  | E.g., `StudentAffairs-prod-PE-sql-db01`.<br>ResourceType and target clarify endpoint destination.         |
| Security Policy         | `<Identity>-<Env>-SP-<Scenario>`               | E.g., `StudentAffairs-prod-SP-accesscontrol`.<br>Scenario describes the policy’s enforcement area.        |
| Network Rule            | `<Identity>-<Env>-NR-<Service>-<Direction>`    | E.g., `StudentAffairs-dev-NR-web-inbound`.<br>Service and direction define rule specifics.                |

### Key Naming Elements

- **Identity**: Business unit or project (e.g., StudentAffairs)
- **Env**: Environment (dev, test, prod)
- **Resource Type**: Abbreviation (NSG, FWP, RT, PE, SP, NR)
- **Location/Scenario/Purpose**: Specifies region, function, or activity

## 5.4.4 Governance & Traceability

UMS shall enforce naming convention adherence via Azure Policy and periodic governance reviews. Resource creation workflows must validate names against the prescribed patterns. This ensures:

- **Auditability**: All resources can be traced back to their owners and intended use.
- **Compliance**: Supports regulatory requirements by enabling clear resource lineage.
- **Operational Efficiency**: Simplifies resource discovery and reduces misconfiguration risks.

## 5.4.5 Limitations & Considerations

While naming conventions are essential, Azure imposes certain constraints:

- **Character Limits**: Resource names must comply with Azure’s maximum character restrictions (typically 80-90 characters).
- **Allowed Characters**: Only alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores are permitted; spaces and special characters are disallowed.
- **Uniqueness**: Names must be globally unique for certain resources (e.g., storage accounts).

UMS shall periodically review naming patterns to ensure compatibility with evolving Azure requirements and organizational needs.

# 6.0 Data Workflow and Integration

## 6.1 Overview

UMS shall implement a robust, scalable, and governed data workflow leveraging Microsoft Fabric and Azure Data Services. The workflow encompasses end-to-end data movement, transformation, enrichment, and analytics, adhering strictly to Microsoft best practices for data engineering, governance, and security. Data originates from the UMS Staging Area (Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse) and traverses through Data Factory pipelines, Lakehouse layers (Bronze, Silver, Gold), Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and ultimately surfaces in Power BI via the Semantic Model and OneLake integration.

## 6.2 End-to-End Data Workflow

### 6.2.1 Data Ingestion

UMS shall utilize Azure Data Factory to orchestrate the extraction of data from the Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse. Data Factory pipelines are configured for scheduled and event-driven ingestion, supporting both batch and incremental loads. Data is landed in the XX_Bronze_LK Lakehouse tables, preserving raw fidelity and enabling traceability.

**Microsoft Best Practices:**
- Use parameterized pipelines for reusability and maintainability.
- Implement robust error handling and logging for operational transparency.
- Leverage managed identities for secure access to source systems.

### 6.2.2 Lakehouse Layering: Bronze, Silver, Gold

UMS adopts the Lakehouse layering paradigm to optimize data quality, lineage, and analytics readiness:

- **Bronze Layer (XX_Bronze_LK):** Stores raw, unprocessed data. This layer acts as the immutable source of truth, capturing all ingested records for auditability.
- **Silver Layer (XX_Silver_LK):** Contains cleansed, validated, and lightly transformed data. Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and SQL Analytics Endpoints are used for data quality enforcement, normalization, and enrichment.
- **Gold Layer (XX_Gold_LK):** Hosts refined, business-ready datasets. This layer aggregates, models, and prepares data for analytics, reporting, and machine learning outputs.

**Data Movement:**
Dataflow Gen2 orchestrates the movement and transformation between Lakehouse layers, ensuring consistency and lineage tracking.

**Workflow Artifact Naming Conventions:**

| Artifact Type        | Naming Convention Example           | Description                                             |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Lakehouse Tables     | XX_Bronze_LK, XX_Silver_LK, XX_Gold_LK | Prefix for project, layer, and Lakehouse identifier      |
| Dataflow Gen2        | DF2_XX_BronzeToSilver               | DF2 for Dataflow Gen2, source-to-target indication       |
| Notebooks            | NBK_XX_DataCleansing                | NBK for Notebook, descriptive purpose                   |
| Data Factory Pipeline| DFP_XX_IngestStaging                | DFP for Data Factory Pipeline, source description        |
| Semantic Model       | SM_XX_GoldLakehouse                 | SM for Semantic Model, target Lakehouse                 |

### 6.2.3 Data Transformation and Machine Learning

UMS employs Dataflow Gen2, Fabric Notebooks (PySpark/Python), and Visual Query for advanced transformation, feature engineering, and machine learning. Cleaned Silver Lakehouse data serves as input for both manual and automated ML processes.

**Manual ML:** Notebooks enable custom feature engineering, algorithm selection, and model tuning.
**AutoML:** Automated experiments select optimal models and hyperparameters. Both approaches store results in the Gold Lakehouse.

**Model Lifecycle Management:**
Trained models are registered in the Fabric Model Registry, supporting versioning and reuse.

### 6.2.4 Analytics and Visualization

The Semantic Model is constructed atop the Gold Lakehouse, leveraging Direct Lake connectivity via OneLake. Power BI Desktop connects to the Semantic Model for interactive reporting and visualization.

**Integration with OneLake:**
OneLake provides unified, centralized storage, ensuring seamless data access, governance, and performance for analytics workloads.

### 6.5.2 Data Volume

- Lakehouse layers are optimized for large-scale analytics; however, performance may degrade for single-table loads exceeding 1 billion records or 10 TB per transaction.
- Data Factory pipelines are subject to Azure subscription-level throughput limits.

### 6.5.3 Transformation Complexity

- Dataflow Gen2 is recommended for moderate transformations; highly complex logic should leverage Notebooks or Spark jobs.
- Real-time streaming is not supported in the current batch-oriented architecture.

# 6.1 Microsoft Best Practices for Data Workflow

## 6.1.1 Overview

Microsoft Fabric is designed to unify analytics workloads, providing a robust platform for data ingestion, transformation, and modeling. Adhering to Microsoft’s best practices ensures that data workflows are scalable, secure, and maintainable, while maximizing performance and integration capabilities across the enterprise. This section outlines authoritative guidance for implementing Lakehouse architecture, ETL/ELT patterns, and seamless integration with Power BI, as recommended by Microsoft.

## 6.1.2 Lakehouse Architecture

UMS shall implement a Lakehouse architecture as the foundational layer for data workflows within Fabric. The Lakehouse paradigm combines the scalability and flexibility of data lakes with the structured data management capabilities of data warehouses. This approach enables organizations to store raw, semi-structured, and structured data in a single location, supporting diverse analytics workloads.

Key principles of Microsoft’s Lakehouse architecture include:

- **Unified Storage Layer:** Data is stored in open formats (e.g., Delta Lake, Parquet) within OneLake, enabling interoperability and minimizing vendor lock-in.
- **Separation of Compute and Storage:** Compute resources (such as Spark pools) are provisioned independently from storage, allowing for elastic scaling and cost optimization.
- **Schema Enforcement and Governance:** Data is ingested with schema validation, ensuring data quality and consistency. Metadata management and cataloging are enforced through Microsoft Purview integration.
- **Security and Access Controls:** Role-based access controls (RBAC) and data masking are implemented at the storage layer, ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements.

### Lakehouse Architecture Diagram

![](fabric_lakehouse_architecture.png)

## 6.1.3 ETL/ELT Patterns

UMS shall adopt Microsoft-recommended ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) patterns to optimize data processing within Fabric. The choice between ETL and ELT is dictated by data volume, transformation complexity, and latency requirements.

- **ETL Pattern:** Suitable for scenarios where data needs to be transformed before loading into the Lakehouse. Dataflows and Data Factory pipelines are leveraged to extract data from source systems, perform transformations (cleansing, enrichment, aggregation), and load the processed data into OneLake.
- **ELT Pattern:** Preferred for high-volume or real-time workloads. Raw data is loaded directly into OneLake, and transformations are performed using Spark or SQL in-place. This pattern reduces data movement and accelerates processing.

Microsoft recommends the following workflow:

1. **Data Ingestion:** Use Data Factory or Dataflows for batch and streaming ingestion. Sources include on-premises databases, SaaS applications, and external files.
2. **Transformation:** Employ Spark notebooks or Dataflows for scalable data transformation. Leverage reusable transformation logic and parameterization for maintainability.
3. **Modeling:** Structure data into curated tables and views, applying business logic and aggregations. Maintain a clear separation between raw, staged, and curated data zones.

### ETL/ELT Workflow Table

| Pattern | Use Case | Tools | Advantages | Limitations |
|---------|----------|-------|------------|-------------|
| ETL     | Complex transformation before loading | Data Factory, Dataflows | Data quality, early validation | Higher latency, increased data movement |
| ELT     | High-volume, real-time, simple transformation | Spark, SQL | Scalability, reduced movement | Requires robust compute, delayed validation |

## 6.1.4 Integration with Power BI

UMS shall integrate curated Lakehouse datasets directly with Power BI, following Microsoft’s best practices for semantic modeling and visualization.

- **Direct Lake Connectivity:** Power BI connects natively to Lakehouse tables, enabling real-time analytics without data duplication.
- **Semantic Models:** Create reusable semantic models in Power BI, applying business logic, measures, and hierarchies. This promotes consistency across reports and dashboards.
- **Row-Level Security:** Implement row-level security in Power BI datasets, ensuring users access only authorized data.
- **Incremental Refresh:** Configure incremental data refresh for large datasets, optimizing performance and minimizing resource consumption.

### Power BI Integration Table

| Integration Aspect      | Best Practice                  | Benefit                      |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Connectivity           | Direct Lakehouse connection    | Real-time analytics          |
| Semantic Modeling      | Centralized models             | Consistent reporting         |
| Security               | Row-level security             | Data protection              |
| Performance            | Incremental refresh            | Efficient resource usage     |

UMS shall enforce governance policies across all data workflow stages, leveraging Microsoft Purview for data cataloging, lineage, and compliance monitoring. Limitations inherent to Fabric, such as supported data formats, compute quotas, and integration boundaries, must be documented and mitigated through architectural controls.
