Role assignments will be reviewed regularly to ensure continued alignment with Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s organizational structure and project needs. # 5.0 Power BI & Fabric Design and Decision This section details the comprehensive solution architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) covering workflow, access, resource organization, governance, and platform configuration for Power BI and Microsoft Fabric. ## 5.1 Workflow Design Considerations UMS shall implement a layered data pipeline leveraging Azure Data Factory, Lakehouse architecture, Dataflow Gen2, and Power BI. The following table outlines key workflow design considerations to ensure scalable, maintainable, and secure data processing. | ID | Description | Workload Type | |------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------| | DC01 | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server. | Data Factory | | DC02 | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server. | Data Engineering | | DC03 | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse. | Data Engineering | | DC04 | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse. | Data Engineering | | DC05 | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering | | DC06 | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold). | Data Factory | | DC07 | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse | Data Science | | DC08 | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse. | Power BI | | DC09 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization. | Power BI | ## 5.2 Workflow Design Decisions UMS shall adopt a multi-layered data workflow for optimal data quality, traceability, and analytics. The architecture diagram below illustrates the end-to-end data flow, from ingestion to reporting. ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_009_img_003.png) Key design decisions include: - Data ingestion from Microsoft SQL Server 2017 (UMS Staging Area) via Azure Data Factory ensures reliable ETL operations and separation of source and target environments. - Bronze, Silver, and Gold Lakehouse layers provide structured transformation stages, improving data governance and auditability. - Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query are utilized for cleansing, enrichment, and transformation, aligning with modern data engineering best practices. - Integration of Data Science processes (AutoML) between Silver and Gold layers enables advanced analytics and predictive modeling, with results stored in the Gold Lakehouse. - Identity management is embedded at the Gold layer to enforce access controls and security. - All data is consolidated in OneLake, providing a unified storage platform and seamless integration with Power BI. - Power BI connects to the Semantic Model in OneLake, enabling robust visualization and business intelligence capabilities. These decisions ensure UMS's data pipeline is scalable, secure, and capable of supporting advanced analytics and reporting requirements. ## 5.3 Access Design Considerations UMS shall enforce secure access to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI through Conditional Access and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). The following sub-sections detail the access methods and their technical implementation. ### Conditional Access Conditional Access is recommended to protect UMS Fabric resources by evaluating user signals (identity, group, network location) and enforcing access policies. The diagram below demonstrates the secure access workflow, where external users interact with UMS Fabric through Conditional Access. ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_001.png) Conditional Access policies are configured to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage. However, due to licensing constraints (UMS currently does not possess Entra ID P1/P2), Conditional Access is deferred for future implementation. ### Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Tenant-level MFA is enforced to compensate for the absence of Conditional Access. All UMS users must undergo additional verification to reduce unauthorized access and mitigate security risks. The authentication and access control flow is illustrated below. ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_002.png) MFA enforcement ensures that only verified users can access Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, maintaining compliance with security best practices even in the absence of advanced Conditional Access policies. ## 5.4 Access Design Decisions The following table summarizes the access policy decisions made for UMS, based on current licensing and operational requirements. | No. | Conditional Access | |------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage | | DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy | | DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts | | DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment | UMS shall enforce tenant-level MFA for all users. Conditional Access policies are designed but deferred until appropriate licensing (Entra ID P1/P2) is acquired. ## 5.5 Identity and Access Management UMS shall utilize Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) for identity and access management. All UMS personnel are included in the tenant directory, enabling centralized user management and authentication. - User inclusion is managed through Entra ID, ensuring only authorized personnel have access to Fabric and Power BI resources. - MFA is enforced at the tenant level, requiring all users to complete an additional authentication step before accessing sensitive data and analytics environments. - Role-based access controls are implemented within Fabric workspaces, aligning permissions with job functions and operational needs. This approach ensures robust identity management, minimizes risk of unauthorized access, and supports auditability for compliance purposes. ## 5.6 Virtual Machine Size UMS shall provision virtual machines to support Power BI and Fabric workloads, based on the following specifications. These configurations ensure adequate performance for data processing and analytics operations. | Component | Specification | |-------------------|----------------------------------------| | Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) | | .NET Framework | Version 4.8 | | vCPU | 8 cores | | RAM | Minimum 8 GB | These VM specifications are selected to balance performance and cost, supporting both development and production environments for data engineering and visualization. ## 5.7 Resource Organization Design UMS shall implement a structured resource organization strategy to maximize manageability, traceability, and compliance across Azure and Fabric environments. ### Azure Management Groups Management Groups are used to organize Azure subscriptions and resources, enabling hierarchical policy enforcement and centralized governance. UMS's Azure resources are grouped by function and department, facilitating streamlined access control and cost management. - Management Groups provide a scalable structure for applying policies, monitoring usage, and managing budgets at different organizational levels. - Resource Groups (e.g., Fabric-RG) are used to logically contain related assets, simplifying lifecycle management and security configuration. ### Naming and Tagging UMS shall adopt standardized naming conventions and tagging strategies for Azure and Fabric resources, ensuring clarity, consistency, and ease of automation. #### Azure Naming Convention The following table defines abbreviations and examples for Azure resource naming. | Resource | Abbreviation | Example | |------------------|-------------|--------------------------| | Resource Group | RG | Fabric-RG | | Virtual Machine | VM | BIProcessingVM | | Storage Account | SA | UMSDataLakeSA | | Network Security Group | NSG | UMSFabricNSG | #### Fabric Naming Convention UMS shall follow the Fabric naming convention for capacity, workspace, lakehouse, dataflow, semantic model, pipeline, and report items, as detailed below. | No. | Item in Fabric | Naming -- | |-----|--------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------| | 1 | Fabric Capacity | stagingareadevcapacity01 | | 2 | Workspace | StudentAffairs_dev_WS | | 3 | Lakehouse | StudentAffairs_Bronze_LK
StudentAffairs_Silver_LK
Etc… | | 4 | Dataflow | StudentAffairs_Bronze_to_Silver_DF
StudentAffairs_Silver_to_Gold_DF
Etc.. | | 5 | Semantic Model | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_SM | | 6 | Pipeline | StudentAffairs_Ingest_Data_PL
Etc.. | | 7 | Report | StudentAffairs_StudentPerformance_Report | #### Application Acronyms The following table defines acronyms used in the application landscape. | Acronym | Full Name | |---------|------------------------| | LK | Lakehouse | | DF | Dataflow | | SM | Semantic Model | | PL | Pipeline | | WS | Workspace | #### Suggested Tags Tags are recommended to facilitate resource tracking, cost attribution, and compliance reporting. | Tag Key | Description | Example Value | |--------------|-----------------------------|----------------------| | Owner | Resource owner | DataEngineeringTeam | | Department | Associated department | StudentAffairs | | Environment | Deployment environment | Dev | | CostCenter | Cost attribution | CC1001 | ## 5.8 Governance Consideration UMS shall implement Azure governance disciplines to ensure cost control, compliance, and operational efficiency. ### Governance Disciplines Cost management, security, and resource compliance are enforced through Azure-native tools. The table below summarizes recommended tools and their disciplines. | Azure Tool | Description | Cost Management Discipline | |-----------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------| | Azure Cost Management | Monitors and analyzes resource consumption | Cost Control | | Azure Policy | Enforces resource compliance | Compliance | | Azure Monitor | Tracks resource health and performance | Operational Efficiency | | Azure Advisor | Provides optimization recommendations | Cost Optimization | These tools enable UMS to proactively manage resource usage, enforce policies, and optimize spending. ### Violation Triggers and Actions UMS shall define triggers and automated actions for policy violations to maintain operational integrity. | Trigger | Action | |-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------| | Resource exceeds budget | Notify owner, restrict further deployment | | Non-compliant resource detected | Auto-remediate or quarantine resource | | Unauthorized access attempt | Alert admin, enforce MFA | | Tag missing on resource | Flag for review, block provisioning | ## 5.9 Multi-Factor Authentication UMS shall enforce tenant-level MFA for all users accessing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. This is a critical security measure, especially since Conditional Access cannot be fully implemented due to licensing constraints (Entra ID P1/P2 not available). - MFA is configured at the tenant level, requiring all users to verify their identity through an additional authentication factor. - Conditional Access policies are designed for future implementation when licensing permits. Private Link was evaluated but deferred due to technical and licensing limitations. - UMS must plan for potential acquisition of Entra ID P1/P2 licenses to enable Conditional Access in the future, further strengthening security posture. This approach ensures UMS maintains a robust security baseline while accommodating current licensing and operational realities. # 6.0 Security Design and Decision This section details the security architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), focusing on Azure Network Security Group (NSG) configuration, outbound rule enforcement, and encryption design for the Power BI and Microsoft Fabric integration. ## 6.1 Azure NSG Overview Universiti Malaysia Sabah shall implement Azure Network Security Groups (NSGs) as the primary mechanism to control network traffic to and from the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine. NSGs are a critical Azure security feature, enabling granular control of inbound and outbound traffic at both subnet and network interface levels. By leveraging NSGs, UMS ensures that only authorized communications occur between the OPDG VM and Microsoft cloud services, reducing the risk of unauthorized access and data exfiltration. The design decision to use NSGs aligns with Azure networking best practices, which recommend explicit rule-based traffic filtering over default open configurations. NSGs provide robust logging and monitoring capabilities, allowing UMS security administrators to audit and respond to anomalous traffic patterns. The NSG configuration for the OPDG VM will be tightly scoped to permit only essential outbound connections required for Fabric, Power BI, and authentication services. Operationally, NSGs are managed via the Azure portal, PowerShell, or Azure CLI, enabling automated deployment and consistent policy enforcement across environments. NSG rules are prioritized and evaluated in order, ensuring deterministic traffic flow and minimizing configuration errors. UMS shall maintain NSG rule documentation and change control processes to support ongoing security governance. ## 6.2 Inbound Rules No inbound NSG rules are specified for the OPDG VM in the current design. This is a deliberate security decision to minimize the attack surface and prevent unsolicited inbound connections from external sources. By default, Azure NSGs deny all inbound traffic unless explicitly allowed, and UMS shall retain this posture for the OPDG VM. Should future integration requirements necessitate inbound connectivity (e.g., for management or monitoring), UMS must conduct a comprehensive risk assessment and update the NSG configuration accordingly. All inbound rule changes must be reviewed and approved by the UMS security team, with proper audit logging enabled. ## 6.3 Outbound Rules UMS shall enforce the following outbound NSG rules to enable secure communication between the OPDG VM and Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints. These rules are designed to allow only necessary outbound traffic, ensuring compliance with Azure security standards and minimizing exposure to external threats. The table below summarizes the outbound NSG rules, including rule name, priority, source, destination, ports, protocol, and access action. | Name | Priority | Source | Source Ports | Destination | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access | |----------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------| | Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services | 130 | VM Private IP Addresses | * | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443 | TCP | Allow | | Allow_OPDG_Authentication | 140 | VM Private IP Addresses | * | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443 | TCP | Allow | | Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test | 160 | VM Private IP Addresses | * | *.msftncsi.com | 80 | TCP | Allow | These outbound rules are configured to: - Permit HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) from the OPDG VM to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core service domains, enabling secure data transfer and service integration. - Allow authentication traffic to Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) endpoints for user sign-ins and token validation, ensuring secure identity management. - Enable connectivity testing via HTTP (TCP port 80) to Microsoft network status endpoints, supporting operational monitoring and troubleshooting. UMS shall regularly review and update these NSG rules to accommodate evolving service endpoints and security requirements. All rule changes must be documented and subjected to change control procedures. ![](slide_14_outbound_nsg_diagram.png) ## 6.4 Encryption Design UMS shall enforce SSL/TLS encryption for all outbound communications from the OPDG VM to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints. This encryption design ensures that sensitive data, authentication tokens, and service requests are transmitted securely over the internet, mitigating risks associated with interception and man-in-the-middle attacks. The outbound NSG rules are scoped to allow only HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to Microsoft service domains, which inherently requires SSL/TLS encryption. Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints mandate encrypted connections, and the OPDG VM must be configured to validate server certificates during handshake processes. UMS administrators shall verify that the OPDG VM operating system and gateway software are updated to support the latest TLS versions and cipher suites, in accordance with Microsoft security recommendations. Operationally, this encryption design provides end-to-end data protection from the OPDG VM to Microsoft cloud services. UMS shall monitor SSL/TLS handshake logs and certificate expiration events to ensure ongoing compliance and minimize disruption risks. No unencrypted traffic shall be permitted for service integration, and any exceptions must be formally documented and approved by the UMS security team. # 7.0 AutoML Workflow Design in Microsoft Fabric This section details the end-to-end AutoML workflow architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), covering technical overview, workflow paths, process phases, and key design decisions. All recommendations are tailored to UMS’s requirements and Fabric environment. ## 7.1 Technical Overview UMS shall implement a unified machine learning workflow leveraging Microsoft Fabric, OneLake, and Power BI to streamline data science operations and analytics. The architecture is designed to ensure seamless integration between data engineering, machine learning, and business intelligence components, maximizing data value and operational efficiency. The workflow begins with cleaned and curated data stored in Silver Lakehouse tables within OneLake. This data serves as the foundation for both AutoML and manual machine learning experimentation. Results from these processes are stored in Gold Lakehouse tables, which act as the standardized repository for all scored and modeled outputs. Power BI connects to Gold Lakehouse via OneLake, enabling advanced visualization and reporting capabilities. The following diagram illustrates the complete workflow, highlighting the interaction between Silver Lakehouse, machine learning paths, Gold Lakehouse, identity management, and Power BI: ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_021_img_005.png) ## 7.2 Approach / Paths UMS shall utilize both AutoML and Manual Machine Learning paths within Microsoft Fabric, providing flexibility for diverse data science workloads and user skill levels. Each path is optimized for different scenarios and technical requirements. ### AutoML Path The AutoML path is recommended for rapid prototyping and model generation on well-structured datasets. Cleaned data from XX_Silver_LK tables is ingested into AutoML experiments, which automate feature selection, model training, and evaluation. This approach minimizes manual intervention, allowing UMS data scientists to quickly assess model performance and suitability. AutoML experiments are executed within Fabric, leveraging built-in algorithms and scoring notebooks. Resulting models and performance metrics are stored in XX_Gold_LK tables, ensuring consistency and traceability. This path is ideal for scenarios where speed and automation are prioritized over custom feature engineering. ### Manual Machine Learning Path UMS shall primarily adopt the Manual Machine Learning approach for advanced use cases requiring custom feature engineering, algorithm tuning, and explainable results. Data from XX_Silver_LK tables is processed using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python), enabling granular control over preprocessing, modeling, and evaluation. Data scientists perform model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning directly in the notebook environment. Trained models are registered in the Fabric Model Registry, supporting lifecycle management and versioning. Scoring notebooks are used to apply models to new data, with outputs stored in XX_Gold_LK tables. This path is recommended for complex datasets and research-driven projects where accuracy and interpretability are critical. ## 7.3 Process Flow UMS’s machine learning workflow is structured into distinct phases, each designed to optimize data quality, model performance, and business impact. ### Data Preparation Cleaned and curated data from Silver Lakehouse tables is selected for experimentation. Data preparation includes validation, transformation, and feature engineering, ensuring readiness for both AutoML and manual ML processes. The selection process is governed by program requirements, with data sourced from the overall Staging DB. ### Model Training UMS data scientists initiate model training either via AutoML experiments or manual notebook-based workflows. In the manual path, custom algorithms, hyperparameter tuning, and iterative optimization are performed to maximize model accuracy and explainability. AutoML automates these steps, providing rapid baseline models for comparison. ### Scoring Trained models are applied to new or unseen data using scoring notebooks. This phase generates predictions, probabilities, and performance metrics, which are critical for assessing model effectiveness. Scored outputs are systematically stored in Gold Lakehouse tables, ensuring standardized access and downstream integration. ### Registration Models developed through manual ML are registered in the Fabric Model Registry. Registration supports versioning, lifecycle management, and potential reuse across future projects. This ensures that UMS maintains a robust catalog of production-ready models, aligned with best practices for governance and traceability. ### Visualization The Gold Lakehouse acts as the central repository for all modeled and scored results. Power BI connects to Gold Lakehouse via OneLake, utilizing Direct Lake connectivity for real-time analytics. Semantic models are built on top of Gold Lakehouse, enabling advanced data modeling and visualization tailored to UMS’s reporting needs. Power BI Desktop further enhances visualization capabilities, providing interactive dashboards and insights for stakeholders. ## 7.4 Design Decisions The following table summarizes critical design considerations and decisions for UMS’s AutoML workflow in Microsoft Fabric. Each entry is mapped to the relevant workload type for clarity. The purpose of this table is to document the rationale behind each workflow component, ensuring alignment with UMS’s data engineering, data science, and business intelligence objectives. | ID | Description | Workload Type | |------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------| | DE01 | The cleaned data stored in Silver Lakehouse will serve as the input for all machine learning experimentation. The chosen data will be one of the programs selected from the overall Staging DB. | Data Engineering | | DE02 | The Project will primarily adopt the Manual Machine Learning approach using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python) to enable custom feature engineering, algorithm tuning, and model optimization. | Data Science | | DE03 | Users will perform model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning directly in the Notebook environment to achieve more accurate and explainable results tailored to UMS’s datasets. | Data Science | | DE04 | Trained models will be registered in the Fabric Model Registry to support model lifecycle management, versioning, and potential reuse across further adoption. | Data Science | | DE05 | Scoring Notebooks will be used to apply trained models to new or unseen data. The scored outputs, predictions, probabilities, and performance metrics will be stored in the Gold Lakehouse. | Data Engineering | | DE06 | The Gold Lakehouse will serve as the standardized repository for all modeled or scored results, ensuring consistency between AutoML and manual ML outputs. | Data Engineering | | DE07 | The Semantic Model will connect to the Gold Lakehouse via Direct Lake (OneLake) and Data Modelling will be implemented. | Power BI | | DE08 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization. | Power BI | These design decisions ensure that UMS’s machine learning workflow is robust, scalable, and fully integrated with the broader Fabric ecosystem, supporting both operational excellence and advanced analytics. # 8.0 Appendix This appendix provides reference materials, quota boundaries, and supplementary documentation for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s Azure solution. Each domain is organized to support ongoing operations and governance. ## Appendix 1 Computing ### Introduction/Prerequisites - [Azure Virtual Machines Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/) - [Azure Compute Overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/topics/compute) - [Azure VM Deployment Best Practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows/deployment-methods) ### Limits and Boundaries - [Azure Subscription and Service Limits, Quotas, and Constraints](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/azure-subscription-service-limits) - [Azure VM Size Limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/sizes) - [Azure Regional VM Quotas](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/quotas) ### Others - [Azure VM Maintenance and Updates](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/maintenance-and-updates) - [Azure Reserved VM Instances](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/windows/reserved-vm-instance) - [Refer to Section 4.2 for VM specifications table] ## Appendix 2 Network ### Introduction/Prerequisites - [Azure Networking Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/networking/) - [Azure Virtual Network Overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-networks-overview) - [Azure ExpressRoute Documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/expressroute/) ### Limits and Boundaries - [Azure Networking Limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network-limits) - [Azure Public IP Address Limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-network/virtual-network-public-ip-address) - [Azure Load Balancer Limits](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/load-balancer/load-balancer-overview) ### Others - asset_library.description:
This is a technical architecture diagram.

**Description:**

1. **Fabric-RG (Resource Group):**
   - Represents an Azure Resource Group named "Fabric-RG," which is a logical container for managing Azure resources.
   - Contains the "UMS Fabric" component.

2. **UMS Fabric:**
   - Likely a service or application hosted within the Fabric-RG resource group.
   - Acts as the source or provider of data or services in this architecture.

3. **Conditional Access:**
   - A security mechanism applied to control access to resources.
   - Ensures that only authorized users meeting specific conditions can access the system.

4. **External User:**
   - Represents an external user or client attempting to access the UMS Fabric service.
   - Access is mediated through the Conditional Access layer.

5. **Data Flow:**
   - The arrow indicates a unidirectional flow of data or access request from the UMS Fabric to the External User.
   - The Conditional Access layer acts as a gatekeeper, enforcing security policies before granting access to the external user.

This diagram illustrates a secure access workflow where an external user interacts with a service (UMS Fabric) hosted in Azure, with Conditional Access ensuring compliance with security policies.

This image is a technical architecture diagram illustrating the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra Conditional Access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**:  
   - The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**:  
   - The authentication request is evaluated against Entra Conditional Access policies. This involves three key steps:
     - **Signal (Step 3)**: Signals such as user identity, group membership, and network location are collected. Examples include:
       - Users and groups.
       - Network location.
     - **Decision (Step 4)**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access. This decision evaluates whether the user meets the defined policy requirements.
     - **Enforcement (Step 5)**: The decision is enforced, either granting or denying access based on the policy evaluation.

3. **Inbound Access to Microsoft Fabric**:  
   - If access is granted, the user is allowed to interact with Microsoft Fabric.  
   - Microsoft Fabric includes various components and services, such as:
     - Data Factory.
     - Synapse Data Engineering.
     - Synapse Data Science.
     - Synapse Data Warehousing.
     - Synapse Real-Time Analytics.
     - Power BI.
     - OneLake (a unified data lake for storage).

This diagram effectively demonstrates the integration of Entra Conditional Access policies with Microsoft Fabric to ensure secure and conditional access to its services.

This image represents a technical architecture diagram illustrating a data processing and analytics workflow within the Azure ecosystem, leveraging UMS Fabric, Data Factory, OneLake, and Power BI.

1. **Data Source**:
   - The process begins with data ingestion from the **UMS Staging Area**, which is hosted on **Microsoft SQL Server 2017**. This serves as the data warehouse for raw data.

2. **Data Ingestion**:
   - **Azure Data Factory** is used to ingest data from the staging area into the UMS Fabric environment.

3. **Data Transformation Layers**:
   - The ingested data is processed through three transformation layers:
     - **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**: This layer represents the raw data storage, where data is ingested in its original form.
     - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: This layer contains cleaned and enriched data, prepared for further analysis.
     - **XX_Gold_LK Tables**: This layer holds the final, curated data, optimized for reporting and analytics.

4. **Data Science Integration**:
   - Between the **Silver** and **Gold** layers, **Data Science** processes are applied. These processes may include advanced analytics, machine learning, or predictive modeling, facilitated by Azure tools.

5. **Identity Management**:
   - The **Identity_SM** component is integrated at the **Gold** layer, likely for managing user access, security, or identity-related data.

6. **Data Storage**:
   - All data layers are stored in **OneLake**, a unified data lake solution, ensuring seamless data access and integration.

7. **Visualization and Reporting**:
   - The curated data in the **Gold** layer is connected to **Power BI** via **OneLake** for visualization, reporting, and business intelligence.

This architecture demonstrates a robust, scalable data pipeline for transforming raw data into actionable insights, leveraging Azure's data and analytics services.

This is a technical architecture diagram.

The diagram illustrates a data flow between three main components:

1. **UMS - Staging_Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 - Data Warehouse**:
   - Represents an on-premises SQL Server 2017 database used as a staging area or data warehouse.
   - It stores and processes data before it is transferred to other systems.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**:
   - Acts as a bridge between the on-premises SQL Server and the cloud-based UMS Fabric.
   - Facilitates secure data transfer without requiring inbound ports to be opened in the on-premises network.
   - Establishes an outbound secure connection to the UMS Fabric.

3. **UMS Fabric**:
   - Represents a cloud-based service or platform that consumes data from the on-premises SQL Server.
   - Likely used for advanced analytics, reporting, or integration with other cloud services.

**Data Flow**:
- Data flows from the SQL Server database to the On-Premise Data Gateway.
- The On-Premise Data Gateway securely transmits the data to the UMS Fabric using an outbound connection.
- The UMS Fabric processes or utilizes the data for its intended purpose.

This architecture ensures secure and efficient data transfer between on-premises and cloud environments without compromising network security.

This is a technical architecture diagram.

### Description:
The diagram illustrates a data processing and machine learning workflow within a unified data platform, leveraging components such as **OneLake**, **UMS Fabric**, **AutoML**, **Manual Machine Learning**, and **Power BI** for analytics.

1. **Data Source:**
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables**: Represents the intermediate data layer stored in OneLake. This serves as the input for machine learning processes.

2. **Machine Learning Pathways:**
   - **AutoML**:
     - Data from **XX_Silver_LK Tables** is fed into an **AutoML Experiment**.
     - The experiment generates models and performs **Result Scoring** using a notebook.
   - **Manual Machine Learning**:
     - Alternatively, data from **XX_Silver_LK Tables** is processed through **Manual Machine Learning**.
     - This involves custom **Modeling** using a notebook.

3. **Processed Data Output:**
   - The results from both **AutoML** and **Manual Machine Learning** are stored in **XX_Gold_LK Tables**, representing the refined data layer.

4. **Identity Management:**
   - **Identity_SM**: A component integrated with the **XX_Gold_LK Tables** for identity or metadata management.

5. **Analytics and Visualization:**
   - The **XX_Gold_LK Tables** are connected to **Power BI** via **OneLake** for data visualization and reporting.

6. **Integration Framework:**
   - The entire workflow operates within the **UMS Fabric**, ensuring seamless integration and data flow across components.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

# UMS Power BI & Fabric

# 2.0 Executive Summary

This section provides a concise overview of the Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) Power BI & Microsoft Fabric implementation, the formal purpose of this solution design document, and the intended audience.

## 2.1 Project Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) has initiated a strategic project to modernize its data analytics infrastructure by leveraging Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. The business context is driven by the need for robust, scalable, and integrated analytics to support decision-making across academic, administrative, and research domains. The project scope encompasses the full data workflow, beginning with data ingestion, followed by transformation and orchestration, and culminating in advanced visualization and analytics.

Key objectives include the establishment of a dedicated Microsoft Fabric workspace to serve as the central hub for all data activities. This workspace will facilitate the development of an end-to-end ETL pipeline, ensuring seamless data integration and transformation via Fabric Data Factory. Advanced analytics and big data processing will be enabled through Fabric Synapse, while machine learning capabilities will be incorporated using Fabric Data Science components. The final stage involves integrating Power BI to deliver business-aligned analytics dashboards and reports tailored to UMS’s operational and strategic needs.

The design aligns with UMS’s cloud adoption strategy, ensuring that each component—Fabric Data Factory, Fabric Synapse, and Data Science—works cohesively to provide a unified, secure, and scalable analytics platform. This approach positions UMS to harness the full potential of Microsoft Fabric for data-driven insights and operational excellence.

## 2.2 Document Purpose

This Solution Design Document is intended to formalize the technical and architectural blueprint for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s implementation of Power BI and Microsoft Fabric. It details the design decisions, configuration specifics, and integration strategies required to deliver a comprehensive data analytics solution aligned with UMS’s business objectives.

The document provides authoritative guidance on the setup, governance, and operationalization of the Fabric workspace, ETL pipeline, machine learning processes, and Power BI integration. It serves as the definitive reference for all stakeholders involved in the project, ensuring clarity, consistency, and alignment throughout the solution lifecycle.

## 2.3 Document Audience

The primary audience for this document includes all stakeholders responsible for the success of the Power BI & Fabric implementation at UMS. This encompasses:

- UMS business leaders and decision-makers seeking actionable insights from analytics.
- UMS analytics and data science teams responsible for data modeling, transformation, and visualization.
- UMS IT administrators and cloud infrastructure specialists managing platform deployment, security, and governance.
- Enfrasys Solutions enterprise architects and technical consultants overseeing solution design, implementation, and best practice adherence.

This document is designed to provide each stakeholder group with the necessary context, technical depth, and operational guidance to ensure a successful, sustainable, and value-driven analytics platform for Universiti Malaysia Sabah.

# 3.0 Network Design and Decision
This section details the network architecture and connectivity decisions for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), focusing on secure integration between on-premises data sources, the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG), and Microsoft Fabric.

## 3.1 Network Connectivity Overview
UMS shall implement a secure, outbound-only network connectivity model to facilitate seamless integration between its on-premises SQL Server, the OPDG virtual machine, and Microsoft Fabric. This design ensures that data transfer occurs without exposing inbound ports, thereby minimizing attack surfaces and adhering to industry best practices for hybrid cloud environments.

The connectivity architecture is structured as follows:

- The UMS on-premises SQL Server 2017, serving as the staging area and data warehouse, is the primary data source.
- The OPDG VM is deployed within the UMS network perimeter and is configured to establish outbound connections to Microsoft Fabric.
- Data flows securely from the SQL Server to the OPDG, which then transmits data to Microsoft Fabric for analytics, reporting, and advanced processing.

This outbound-only approach is critical for UMS, as it avoids the risks associated with opening inbound firewall ports on the on-premises network. The OPDG agent initiates all communication, ensuring that only authorized and encrypted traffic leaves the UMS environment. This model aligns with Microsoft’s recommended hybrid connectivity patterns and supports compliance requirements for data protection.

Operationally, this design reduces the complexity of firewall management and enhances the security posture of UMS by preventing unsolicited access attempts from external sources. It also simplifies troubleshooting, as all connectivity is initiated from within the trusted UMS network.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_011_img_004.png)

The diagram above illustrates the data flow: the on-premises SQL Server connects to the OPDG, which then securely transmits data to UMS Fabric. No inbound connections are required, and all traffic is encrypted and authenticated.

## 3.2 Fabric Data Gateway
UMS shall utilize the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) as the critical bridge between its internal SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric. The OPDG agent is installed on a dedicated virtual machine within the UMS network, configured to securely access local data sources and relay information to Fabric services in the cloud.

The OPDG is responsible for:

- Establishing secure, outbound connections to Microsoft Fabric, eliminating the need for inbound firewall exceptions.
- Authenticating and encrypting all data transfers, ensuring confidentiality and integrity.
- Supporting scheduled and real-time data refreshes, enabling UMS to maintain up-to-date analytics and reporting within Fabric.

From a technical perspective, the OPDG leverages Microsoft’s gateway architecture, which uses HTTPS for outbound communication. This ensures compatibility with most enterprise firewall configurations and supports granular access controls. The gateway agent is managed centrally, allowing UMS administrators to monitor connectivity, configure data sources, and enforce security policies.

The operational impact for UMS includes improved data governance, reduced risk of unauthorized access, and simplified maintenance. The gateway architecture also supports scalability, enabling UMS to add new data sources or increase throughput as business requirements evolve.

For detailed VM specifications supporting the OPDG deployment, refer to Section 5.x Platform Design. These specifications ensure optimal performance, reliability, and security for the gateway service.

In summary, the OPDG is a foundational element in UMS’s hybrid data architecture, providing a secure, scalable, and manageable bridge between on-premises assets and Microsoft Fabric. This design decision directly supports UMS’s objectives for secure data integration, operational efficiency, and compliance with best practices.

# 4.0 Roles in Power BI & Fabric

This section defines the role structure, assignment processes, and access mapping for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) within Microsoft Power BI and Fabric, ensuring secure and appropriate access for all stakeholders.

## 4.1 Fabric Administrator Role

1. Nomination: Candidates are nominated by department heads based on expertise and responsibility.
2. Validation: The IT governance committee reviews nominations for compliance with internal security policies and Microsoft best practices.
3. Assignment: The selected personnel are granted Fabric Administrator privileges via the Microsoft 365 Admin Center, ensuring traceability and auditability.

# 5.0 Power BI & Fabric Design and Decision

This section details the architectural design and decision-making process for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) Power BI and Microsoft Fabric deployment. The following sub-sections provide comprehensive technical rationale, configuration specifics, and business impact for each major design element.

## 5.1 Workflow Design Considerations

UMS shall implement a multi-layered data workflow leveraging Azure Data Factory, Lakehouse architecture, and integrated machine learning to optimize data processing, analytics, and visualization. The following table summarizes the key workflow design considerations, including AutoML integration for advanced analytics:

The table below outlines the primary workflow components and their workload types:

| ID    | Description                                                                                                  | Workload Type     |
|-------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|
| DC01  | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server.                                         | Data Factory      |
| DC02  | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server.                        | Data Engineering  |
| DC03  | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse.                             | Data Engineering  |
| DC04  | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse.                            | Data Engineering  |
| DC05  | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering  |
| DC06  | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold).  | Data Factory      |
| DC07  | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse. | Data Science      |
| DC08  | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse.                 | Power BI          |
| DC09  | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                      | Power BI          |

UMS’s workflow design ensures that data is ingested, cleansed, transformed, and modeled in a structured manner. The inclusion of AutoML allows for scalable and repeatable machine learning processes, with results stored in the Gold Lakehouse for downstream analytics and visualization.

## 5.2 Workflow Design Decisions

UMS shall adopt a three-tier Lakehouse architecture (Bronze, Silver, Gold) to maximize data quality and analytical value. The following key workflow decisions have been made:

- Bronze Lakehouse will capture raw data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server via Azure Data Factory.
- Silver Lakehouse will host cleansed and enriched data, processed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Queries.
- Gold Lakehouse will store transformed and highly curated data, including results from Data Science AutoML processes.
- Semantic Models will be built on Gold Lakehouse data, enabling robust Power BI visualizations through OneLake integration.
- Data pipelines will be orchestrated using Azure Data Factory, ensuring reliable ETL and ML integration.

This design enables UMS to maintain data lineage, improve data quality, and facilitate advanced analytics, supporting both operational reporting and predictive modeling.

## 5.9 Multi-Factor Authentication

UMS shall enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level for all users accessing Power BI and Fabric. This decision is driven by the current account’s ineligibility for Conditional Access licensing and aligns with security best practices.

- Tenant-level MFA ensures all users are subject to strong authentication, regardless of Conditional Access eligibility.
- MFA reduces risk from credential compromise, phishing, and unauthorized access.

This approach provides robust security for UMS’s analytics environment, balancing usability and risk mitigation. For network security rules, refer to Section 6.0 Security Design.

# 6.0 Security Design and Decision
This section defines the security architecture for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), focusing on Azure Network Security Group (NSG) configuration and encryption design for outbound connectivity from the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine.

## 6.1 Azure NSG Overview
UMS shall implement a tightly scoped Azure Network Security Group (NSG) to enforce outbound connectivity controls for the OPDG VM. This approach aligns with Azure networking best practices, ensuring both operational continuity and minimized attack surface. The NSG is designed to permit only essential outbound traffic required for Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication services, while blocking all unnecessary flows.

### Inbound Rules
No inbound NSG rules are required for the OPDG VM. This decision is based on the architectural principle that the gateway operates solely as an outbound connector to Microsoft cloud services, never exposing services or ports to external sources. By not permitting inbound connections, UMS eliminates a major vector for external threats, ensuring the VM is not accessible from the internet or other untrusted networks.

This approach adheres to the principle of least privilege and supports UMS’s compliance posture by preventing unsolicited inbound access.

### Outbound Rules
UMS shall configure the following outbound NSG rules for the OPDG VM. These rules are specifically designed to allow secure communication, authentication, and connectivity verification with Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Azure services. Each rule is assigned a priority to ensure deterministic processing and prevent conflicts.

The table below summarizes the outbound NSG rules required for OPDG operation:

| Name                       | Priority | Source                  | Source Ports | Destination                                                                                                               | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access |
|----------------------------|----------|-------------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------|
| Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services  | 130      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Authentication  | 140      | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443               | TCP      | Allow  |
| Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test | 160    | VM Private IP Addresses | *            | *.msftncsi.com                                                                                                            | 80                | TCP      | Allow  |

These rules are configured as follows:

- **Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services** (Priority 130): Permits outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) from the OPDG VM to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core endpoints. This is essential for secure data transfer, service communication, and ensures all traffic is encrypted via SSL/TLS.
- **Allow_OPDG_Authentication** (Priority 140): Enables outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) and related authentication endpoints. This rule is critical for user sign-ins, token validation, and secure identity management.
- **Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test** (Priority 160): Allows outbound HTTP traffic (TCP port 80) to Microsoft network connectivity status endpoints. This is used solely for connectivity verification and troubleshooting.

UMS shall ensure that all other outbound traffic is denied by default, further reducing the risk of data exfiltration or unauthorized access. These rules are reviewed periodically as part of the operational security process.

![](slide14_outbound_nsg_diagram.png)

## 6.2 Encryption Design
UMS shall enforce SSL/TLS encryption for all outbound connections from the OPDG VM to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication services. This design decision is driven by the need to protect sensitive data in transit, comply with regulatory requirements, and align with Microsoft’s security recommendations.

All permitted outbound traffic (as defined in the NSG rules above) utilizes HTTPS (TCP port 443), which ensures that data is encrypted end-to-end between the OPDG VM and Microsoft cloud endpoints. This includes:

- Secure data transfer to Fabric and Power BI endpoints, preventing interception or tampering.
- Encrypted authentication flows with Microsoft Entra ID, safeguarding credentials and tokens.
- Connectivity tests, where applicable, are performed over HTTP but do not transmit sensitive information.

SSL/TLS certificates are managed by Microsoft for all cloud endpoints, ensuring that UMS does not need to provision or manage certificates for these connections. The OPDG VM is configured to validate certificate chains and enforce strict protocol negotiation, preventing downgrade attacks or usage of deprecated cipher suites.

Operationally, this encryption strategy ensures that all data leaving the UMS network is protected against eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. It also supports UMS’s compliance with data privacy standards, including those mandated by Malaysian higher education regulations.

For access control and multi-factor authentication (MFA) decisions, refer to Section 5.x of this document, where these mechanisms are described in detail as part of the platform access design.

In summary, UMS’s security design for OPDG leverages Azure NSG outbound rules and SSL/TLS encryption to deliver a robust, compliant, and operationally efficient solution for connecting to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI.

# 7.0 AutoML Workflow Design in Microsoft Fabric

This section details the comprehensive design for integrating AutoML and Manual Machine Learning workflows within Microsoft Fabric for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS).

## 7.1 Technical Overview

Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) shall implement a hybrid machine learning architecture in Microsoft Fabric, leveraging both AutoML and Manual Machine Learning approaches. The design utilizes OneLake as the unified storage layer, ensuring seamless data integration and workflow orchestration across the UMS Fabric environment. The primary objective is to enable scalable, repeatable, and explainable machine learning processes that can be tailored to UMS’s diverse academic and operational datasets.

The architecture is anchored on the principle of data refinement through distinct lakehouse layers: Silver Lakehouse for cleansed data and Gold Lakehouse for enriched outputs. Both AutoML and Manual ML workflows are orchestrated within Fabric, with results converging in the Gold Lakehouse for downstream analytics. Power BI is tightly integrated via OneLake, providing robust visualization and business intelligence capabilities. This dual-path approach empowers UMS to balance rapid experimentation with AutoML and deep customization with manual ML, optimizing both efficiency and model quality.

Integration of identity management, model registry, and scoring notebooks ensures secure, auditable, and lifecycle-aware machine learning operations. The architecture supports advanced feature engineering, algorithm tuning, and versioned model deployment, aligning with UMS’s requirements for explainability, reproducibility, and future scalability.

## 7.2 Approach/Paths

UMS’s machine learning solution in Fabric is designed to support two distinct but complementary paths: AutoML and Manual Machine Learning. The following subsections provide technical detail for each approach, referencing the architectural diagram.

![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_021_img_005.png)

### AutoML

UMS shall utilize Fabric’s AutoML capabilities to streamline the creation of machine learning models. AutoML automates the entire modeling pipeline, from feature selection to hyperparameter optimization, enabling rapid experimentation and deployment. The workflow initiates with cleansed data from the Silver Lakehouse, which serves as input for the AutoML Experiment. The system automatically generates candidate models, evaluates their performance, and selects the optimal configuration.

Scoring is performed using Fabric Notebooks, where the selected model is applied to new or unseen data, producing predictions and performance metrics. The results are stored in the Gold Lakehouse, ensuring standardized output for downstream analytics. This approach is ideal for scenarios where speed and operational efficiency are paramount, and where domain-specific customization is less critical.

AutoML’s integration with the Fabric Model Registry facilitates model lifecycle management, including versioning and reuse. The architecture ensures that all AutoML outputs are securely mapped and auditable via the Identity_SM component. This path is particularly suited for UMS’s operational workloads requiring rapid prototyping and deployment.

### Manual Machine Learning

UMS shall also implement a Manual Machine Learning path using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python), allowing for advanced feature engineering and algorithmic customization. This approach is initiated from the same Silver Lakehouse data but diverges by enabling data scientists to craft bespoke models, tune algorithms, and optimize for UMS-specific requirements.

Modeling is performed directly in the Notebook environment, supporting iterative experimentation and fine-tuning. The manual ML process emphasizes explainability and accuracy, with users able to tailor models to the unique characteristics of UMS’s datasets. Once trained, models are registered in the Fabric Model Registry, supporting version control and lifecycle management.

Scoring notebooks are used to apply these custom models to new data, with outputs—predictions, probabilities, performance metrics—stored in the Gold Lakehouse. Manual ML is recommended for academic and research workloads where deep domain knowledge and interpretability are essential.

## 7.3 Process Flow

UMS’s machine learning workflow in Fabric is structured into five distinct phases, each facilitating robust data processing and model management.

### Data Preparation

Data preparation is performed in the Silver Lakehouse, where raw data is cleansed, transformed, and curated for machine learning experimentation. UMS shall select relevant programs from the Staging DB, ensuring only high-quality, representative datasets are used. This phase is critical for both AutoML and Manual ML paths, as it establishes the foundation for accurate and reliable modeling.

Data engineers are responsible for validating data integrity, handling missing values, and engineering features as needed. The Silver Lakehouse acts as the standardized input repository, supporting reproducibility and auditability across all subsequent phases.

### Model Training

Model training occurs within Fabric Notebooks. For AutoML, the training process is automated, with the system iterating through various algorithms and configurations to identify the best-performing model. For Manual ML, UMS data scientists conduct custom training, leveraging domain expertise to optimize feature selection, algorithm parameters, and model architecture.

Both approaches utilize the Silver Lakehouse as input and produce trained models ready for evaluation. The training phase is tightly integrated with the Fabric Model Registry, ensuring models are versioned and managed throughout their lifecycle.

### Scoring

Scoring is performed using dedicated notebooks, where trained models are applied to new or unseen data. This phase generates predictions, probabilities, and performance metrics, which are essential for assessing model efficacy and operational impact.

UMS shall ensure that scoring outputs are securely mapped via Identity_SM, maintaining data lineage and access control. The results are stored in the Gold Lakehouse, providing a unified repository for all scored data, regardless of modeling approach.

### Registration

Model registration is a mandatory step for lifecycle management. All trained models—whether from AutoML or Manual ML—are registered in the Fabric Model Registry. This process supports versioning, audit trails, and potential reuse across future projects.

UMS shall implement policies for model approval, deprecation, and rollback, ensuring operational stability and compliance. Registration also facilitates integration with downstream analytics and reporting tools.

### Visualization

Visualization is achieved through Power BI, which connects to the Gold Lakehouse via OneLake. UMS users can build semantic models, dashboards, and reports, leveraging the enriched data produced by machine learning workflows.

Power BI Desktop is recommended for advanced analytics, supporting direct connectivity to OneLake and enabling real-time insights. Visualization completes the workflow, translating complex model outputs into actionable intelligence for academic, operational, and strategic decision-making.

## 7.4 Design Decisions

The following table summarizes key design considerations and their associated workload types for UMS’s machine learning workflow in Fabric. Each decision is grounded in technical and operational requirements identified during solution analysis.

The table below provides a concise reference for all major workflow design decisions.

| ID   | Description                                                                                                                | Workloads type   |
|------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------|
| DE01 | The cleaned data stored in Silver Lakehouse will serve as the input for all machine learning experimentation. The chosen data will be one of the programs selected from the overall Staging DB. | Data Engineering |
| DE02 | The Project will primarily adopt the Manual Machine Learning approach using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python) to enable custom feature engineering, algorithm tuning, and model optimization. | Data Science     |
| DE03 | Users will perform model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning directly in the Notebook environment to achieve more accurate and explainable results tailored to UMS’s datasets. | Data Science     |
| DE04 | Trained models will be registered in the Fabric Model Registry to support model lifecycle management, versioning, and potential reuse across further adoption. | Data Science     |
| DE05 | Scoring Notebooks will be used to apply trained models to new or unseen data. The scored outputs, predictions, probabilities, and performance metrics will be stored in the Gold Lakehouse. | Data Engineering |
| DE06 | The Gold Lakehouse will serve as the standardized repository for all modeled or scored results, ensuring consistency between AutoML and manual ML outputs. | Data Engineering |
| DE07 | The Semantic Model will connect to the Gold Lakehouse via Direct Lake (OneLake) and Data Modelling will be implemented | Power BI         |
| DE08 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization.                                     | Power BI         |

These design decisions collectively ensure that UMS’s Fabric-based machine learning workflows are robust, scalable, and aligned with both academic and operational objectives.

This is a technical architecture diagram.

The diagram illustrates a system where an external user interacts with a resource group and fabric service through conditional access. The components and data flow are as follows:

1. **Fabric-RG (Resource Group)**:
   - Represents an Azure Resource Group that contains resources related to the UMS Fabric.
   - Acts as the logical container for managing and organizing resources.

2. **UMS Fabric**:
   - A service or application hosted within the Fabric-RG.
   - Likely provides specific functionalities or services to users.

3. **Conditional Access**:
   - A security mechanism that enforces access policies based on conditions such as user identity, device compliance, or location.
   - Ensures that only authorized external users can access the UMS Fabric.

4. **External User**:
   - Represents a user outside the organization attempting to access the UMS Fabric.
   - Access is mediated by the Conditional Access policies.

**Data Flow**:
- The external user initiates a request to access the UMS Fabric.
- The request is evaluated by the Conditional Access system, which applies security policies.
- If the conditions are met, the user is granted access to the UMS Fabric hosted within the Fabric-RG.

This architecture emphasizes secure access control for external users interacting with Azure-hosted resources.

This image is a technical architecture diagram illustrating the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra Conditional Access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**:  
   - The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**:  
   - The authentication request is evaluated against Entra Conditional Access policies.  
   - **Signal (Step 3)**: Signals such as user identity, group membership, and network location are collected.  
   - **Decision (Step 4)**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access.  
   - **Enforcement (Step 5)**: The decision is enforced, allowing or denying access based on the policy configuration.

3. **Inbound Access to Microsoft Fabric**:  
   - If access is granted, the user is directed to Microsoft Fabric.  
   - Microsoft Fabric includes various components such as data engineering, data integration, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence tools.  
   - **OneLake**: A unified data lake is part of the ecosystem, providing centralized data storage and management.

This diagram highlights the integration of conditional access policies with Microsoft Fabric to ensure secure and policy-driven access control.

This image represents a technical architecture diagram illustrating a data processing and analytics workflow within the Microsoft Azure ecosystem, leveraging UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and Power BI for visualization.

1. **Data Source**:  
   - The process begins with data ingestion from the **UMS Staging Area**, which is hosted on **Microsoft SQL Server 2017 – Data Warehouse**. This serves as the initial repository for raw data.

2. **Data Ingestion**:  
   - **Azure Data Factory** is used to ingest data from the staging area into the UMS Fabric environment. Data Factory orchestrates the movement and transformation of data.

3. **Bronze Layer**:  
   - The ingested data is stored in **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**, representing the raw data layer. This layer is used for initial storage without significant transformation.

4. **Silver Layer**:  
   - Data is processed and transformed into **XX_Silver_LK Tables**, which represent the cleansed and enriched data layer. This layer is optimized for further analysis and processing.

5. **Data Science Integration**:  
   - The **Data Science** component is integrated into the pipeline, enabling advanced analytics, machine learning, or predictive modeling on the Silver Layer data. This step is facilitated by Azure Data Factory for orchestration.

6. **Gold Layer**:  
   - The processed and refined data is stored in **XX_Gold_LK Tables**, representing the final, highly curated data layer. This layer is designed for direct consumption by business intelligence tools.

7. **Identity Management**:  
   - The **Identity_SM** component is included, likely for managing data access, security, or user identity within the pipeline.

8. **Data Lake Integration**:  
   - The entire workflow is integrated with **OneLake**, which serves as a unified data lake for centralized storage and management of data across the pipeline.

9. **Visualization**:  
   - The final curated data in the Gold Layer is connected to **Power BI** via OneLake, enabling interactive dashboards and reports for business users.

This architecture demonstrates a modern data pipeline leveraging Azure services for scalable, secure, and efficient data processing and analytics.

This image is a technical architecture diagram.

The diagram illustrates a data flow between three main components:

1. **UMS - Staging_Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 - Data Warehouse**:
   - Represents an on-premises SQL Server 2017 database used as a staging area or data warehouse.
   - It stores and processes data locally before it is transferred to other systems.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**:
   - Acts as a bridge between the on-premises SQL Server and cloud-based services.
   - Facilitates secure data transfer by establishing an outbound secure connection to the cloud without requiring inbound ports to be opened in the on-premises network.
   - Ensures data is transmitted securely and efficiently.

3. **UMS Fabric**:
   - Represents a cloud-based service or platform that consumes the data from the on-premises SQL Server.
   - The data is transferred securely from the On-Premise Data Gateway to the UMS Fabric for further processing, analysis, or storage.

**Data Flow**:
- Data originates in the on-premises SQL Server 2017 database.
- The On-Premise Data Gateway securely connects to the SQL Server and facilitates the transfer of data to the UMS Fabric.
- The connection between the On-Premise Data Gateway and UMS Fabric is outbound and secure, eliminating the need for inbound ports, enhancing security.

This architecture ensures secure and seamless integration between on-premises and cloud environments.

This is a technical architecture diagram.

### Description:
The diagram illustrates a data processing and machine learning workflow within the context of Azure services, leveraging **UMS Fabric** and **OneLake** for data storage and integration. The workflow involves two primary machine learning approaches: **AutoML** and **Manual Machine Learning**, with the results feeding into downstream analytics and reporting tools like **Power BI**.

---

### Components and Data Flow:

1. **Data Source: XX_Silver_LK Tables**
   - Represents the intermediate data layer stored in **OneLake**.
   - Acts as the input data source for both machine learning workflows.

2. **Machine Learning Workflows:**
   - **AutoML:**
     - **AutoML Experiment:** Automatically generates machine learning models based on the input data.
     - **Result Scoring (Using Notebook):** The generated models are evaluated, and scoring results are produced.
   - **Manual Machine Learning:**
     - **Modeling (Using Notebook):** Custom machine learning models are developed manually using notebooks.

3. **Output Data:**
   - Both workflows output their results to **XX_Gold_LK Tables**, representing the refined and enriched data layer in **OneLake**.

4. **Identity_SM:**
   - A component that likely handles identity mapping, security, or metadata management for the processed data in **XX_Gold_LK Tables**.

5. **Integration with Power BI:**
   - The **XX_Gold_LK Tables** are connected to **Power BI** via **OneLake**, enabling visualization and reporting of the processed data.

---

### Key Connections:
- **UMS Fabric** provides the overarching framework for data integration and processing.
- **OneLake** serves as the unified data storage layer, facilitating seamless data flow between components.
- The outputs from both machine learning workflows converge into a single refined data layer (**XX_Gold_LK Tables**) for downstream analytics.
- **Power BI** is the final consumer of the processed data, enabling business intelligence and decision-making.

This architecture demonstrates a robust pipeline for data processing, machine learning, and analytics, leveraging Azure's ecosystem.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.
