# UMS Power BI & Fabric # 2.0 Executive Summary This section provides a high-level summary of the project context, the purpose of this Solution Design Document, and the intended audience for its contents. ## 2.1 Project Overview Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) has initiated a strategic project to modernize its data analytics infrastructure by leveraging Microsoft Fabric. The primary business objective is to design and implement a robust data workflow that spans ingestion, transformation, and visualization, tailored to UMS’s operational and analytical requirements. The scope encompasses the establishment of a dedicated Fabric workspace, the development of an end-to-end ETL pipeline, the execution of machine learning processes, and the seamless integration of Power BI for business-aligned analytics and reporting. To ensure alignment with UMS’s cloud adoption strategy, the solution will utilize Microsoft Fabric’s suite of services, including Fabric Data Factory for data integration and orchestration, Fabric Synapse for advanced analytics and big data processing, and the Data Science module for machine learning applications. This comprehensive approach is intended to empower UMS with scalable, secure, and efficient data-driven insights, supporting both operational excellence and informed decision-making. ## 2.2 Document Purpose The Solution Design Document serves as the authoritative blueprint for the technical architecture and implementation strategy of the UMS Power BI & Fabric project. Its intent is to articulate the design principles, technology selection, and configuration standards that will guide the deployment of Microsoft Fabric components within UMS. The document provides a detailed reference for all stakeholders, ensuring clarity on the solution’s objectives, scope, and expected outcomes, and establishing a foundation for subsequent phases such as build, test, and operationalization. ## 2.3 Document Audience This document is intended for a diverse group of stakeholders involved in the UMS Power BI & Fabric project. The primary audience includes: - UMS IT management and technical teams responsible for implementation, maintenance, and governance of the solution. - UMS business stakeholders and analytics users seeking to understand the impact and capabilities of the new data platform. - Enfrasys Solutions architects and consultants tasked with designing, delivering, and supporting the Microsoft Fabric environment. - Project sponsors and decision-makers requiring assurance of alignment with UMS’s strategic objectives and compliance standards. By addressing the needs of these audiences, the document ensures shared understanding and facilitates effective collaboration throughout the project lifecycle. # 3.0 Network Design and Decision This section details the network architecture and connectivity decisions for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), focusing on secure access to Microsoft Fabric and the integration of the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). ## 3.1 Network Connectivity Overview UMS shall implement a network architecture that prioritizes secure, policy-driven access to Microsoft Fabric resources. The design leverages Entra Conditional Access to enforce robust authentication and authorization controls, ensuring only compliant users and devices can access sensitive workloads. The architecture is illustrated in the following diagrams, which depict both the resource group structure and the conditional access enforcement flow. ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_001.png) ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_006_img_002.png) The first diagram presents the logical organization within Azure, where the Fabric-RG (Resource Group) contains the UMS Fabric services. External users attempting to access UMS Fabric are routed through the Conditional Access layer. This layer evaluates user credentials, device compliance, and contextual signals such as location and risk posture before granting access. This approach aligns with Microsoft’s Zero Trust principles, reducing the attack surface and minimizing unauthorized access. The second diagram expands on the authentication and access control flow. When a user initiates authentication to Microsoft Fabric, Entra Conditional Access policies analyze signals including user identity, group membership, and network location. The policy engine makes a decision—grant or deny—based on these signals, and enforces the outcome. If access is approved, the user is directed to Microsoft Fabric, which encompasses components such as OneLake, data engineering, integration, science, analytics, and business intelligence. UMS’s decision to implement Conditional Access ensures that only authorized personnel, using compliant devices and from trusted locations, can access critical data assets. This not only meets regulatory requirements but also provides operational flexibility for remote and hybrid work scenarios. The architecture supports seamless integration with existing Azure services and enables granular policy enforcement without compromising user productivity. ## 3.2 [Power BI & Fabric] Data Gateway UMS shall deploy the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) within a dedicated virtual machine to bridge on-premises data sources and Microsoft Fabric. The OPDG is essential for secure, reliable data transfer between UMS’s SQL Server 2017 data warehouse and cloud-based analytics platforms, including Power BI and Fabric. The following diagram illustrates the connectivity flow and operational role of the OPDG. ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_011_img_004.png) The diagram shows three primary components: the UMS staging area (Microsoft SQL Server 2017), the OPDG, and UMS Fabric. The OPDG agent is installed on a VM within the UMS network. It establishes a secure outbound connection to Microsoft Fabric, eliminating the need to open inbound ports and thereby enhancing security posture. Data flows from the SQL Server to the OPDG, which then transmits it securely to Fabric for further processing and analytics. Installation of the OPDG requires careful configuration to ensure high availability and optimal performance. UMS must provision a VM with sufficient resources to handle expected data volumes and concurrency. The gateway VM should be placed in a network segment with access to the SQL Server, and firewall rules must permit outbound connectivity to Microsoft Fabric endpoints. Regular updates and monitoring are recommended to maintain operational integrity and compliance. The OPDG’s role as a secure bridge is critical for UMS’s hybrid data strategy. It enables seamless integration of on-premises data with cloud analytics, supports scheduled and real-time refresh scenarios, and ensures data sovereignty by keeping sensitive information within UMS’s controlled environment until transmission is required. ### Gateway VM Specifications The table below outlines the recommended specifications for the OPDG virtual machine, ensuring reliable performance and security for UMS’s data integration workloads. | Component | Specification | |-------------------|----------------------------------------| | VM Size | Standard D2s v3 | | CPU | 2 vCPUs | | Memory | 8 GB RAM | | OS | Windows Server 2019 | | Storage | 128 GB SSD | | Network | 1 Gbps Ethernet, outbound access only | | Availability | Single instance, backup recommended | These specifications are designed to meet the operational demands of the OPDG, supporting concurrent data refreshes and maintaining secure connectivity. UMS should monitor gateway performance and scale resources as needed, based on data volume and user concurrency. Regular backups and system updates are mandatory to ensure resilience and compliance with university IT policies. # 4.0 Roles in Power BI & Fabric This section defines the role hierarchy, access permissions, and specific Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) personnel assignments for Power BI and Microsoft Fabric environments. ## 4.1 Power BI & Fabric Administrator Role The Administrator role is pivotal for managing the overall Power BI and Fabric environment at UMS. This role is responsible for tenant-level configuration, security, monitoring, and lifecycle management of resources. We recommend that Universiti Malaysia Sabah assign this role to personnel with deep technical expertise and authority to enforce governance and operational policies. ### Assigning the Administrator Role UMS shall assign the Power BI & Fabric Administrator role to individuals who possess both technical proficiency and organizational authority. Administrators must be empowered to configure tenant settings, monitor usage, and manage security policies. Assignment should be conducted via Azure Active Directory, ensuring that only authorized personnel have access to the admin portal. The selection process must prioritize continuity, accountability, and the ability to respond to incidents rapidly. Administrators should be granted access to the Power BI Admin Portal and Fabric Admin interfaces, enabling them to oversee workspace creation, user provisioning, and compliance enforcement. It is recommended that UMS maintain a minimum of two administrators for redundancy and operational resilience. All administrator actions should be audited to maintain traceability and meet compliance requirements. The role assignment must be reviewed quarterly, and any changes in personnel must be reflected immediately to prevent unauthorized access. UMS shall establish a documented process for onboarding and offboarding administrators, including mandatory training on Power BI and Fabric governance best practices. ### The Power BI & Fabric Admin Portal Capabilities The following table summarizes the key capabilities available to Power BI & Fabric Administrators at Universiti Malaysia Sabah: | Capability | Description | |---------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Tenant Configuration | Manage global settings, licensing, and integrations for Power BI & Fabric. | | Security Policy Management| Configure authentication, access controls, and data protection policies. | | Workspace Management | Create, delete, and monitor workspaces across the tenant. | | User Provisioning | Add, remove, and assign roles to users and groups. | | Audit & Compliance | Access activity logs, usage metrics, and compliance reports. | | Resource Monitoring | Track resource utilization, performance, and health metrics. | | Lifecycle Management | Oversee resource retention, archival, and deletion policies. | This table ensures UMS administrators have a comprehensive understanding of their operational scope and responsibilities. ## 4.2 Power BI & Fabric-native Roles Power BI and Fabric environments provide granular role-based access control (RBAC) at both the workspace and item levels. UMS shall leverage these native roles to enforce least privilege access and align responsibilities with organizational structure. ### Workspace-level Roles Workspace-level roles define permissions for users within a Power BI or Fabric workspace. UMS shall utilize these roles to segregate duties and ensure secure collaboration. | Role | Permissions | |-------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Admin | Full control: add/remove users, manage workspace settings, publish, delete, and audit content. | | Contributor | Create, edit, and publish content; cannot manage workspace settings or user access. | | Member | View content, interact with datasets, limited editing rights on assigned items. | | Viewer | Read-only access to reports, dashboards, and datasets; no editing or publishing rights. | This table provides clarity on the scope of each workspace role, enabling UMS to map personnel to appropriate access levels. ### Item-level Roles Item-level roles govern permissions on individual assets such as datasets, reports, and dashboards within a workspace. UMS shall utilize item-level roles to restrict access to sensitive data and maintain compliance. | Item | Role | Permissions | |-----------|------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Dataset | Admin | Full control: refresh, update schema, manage permissions, delete. | | Dataset | Contributor | Edit schema, refresh data, but cannot manage permissions or delete. | | Dataset | Viewer | View data, run queries, no edit or refresh rights. | | Report | Admin | Full control: edit, publish, manage permissions, delete. | | Report | Contributor | Edit and publish, but cannot manage permissions or delete. | | Report | Viewer | View only, no editing or publishing rights. | | Dashboard | Admin | Full control: edit, share, manage permissions, delete. | | Dashboard | Contributor | Edit and share, but cannot manage permissions or delete. | | Dashboard | Viewer | View only, no editing or sharing rights. | This table ensures granular control over individual resources, supporting UMS’s data governance and security objectives. ## 4.3 UMS Workspace Role and Access UMS shall implement a structured role mapping for Power BI and Fabric workspaces, assigning specific personnel to roles based on their responsibilities and expertise. UMS shall review these assignments annually and update them as organizational roles evolve. # 5.0 Power BI & Fabric Design and Decision This section details the comprehensive design and decision framework for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) regarding the implementation of Microsoft Power BI and Fabric, including workflow, access, resource organization, governance, and virtual machine sizing. ## 5.1 Workflow Design Considerations UMS’s workflow design is structured to ensure robust data processing, transformation, and analytics, leveraging Azure Data Factory, Lakehouse architecture, and Power BI. The following table summarizes key workflow considerations, mapped to their workload types: | ID | Description | Workload Type | |------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------| | DC01 | Data Pipeline will be used to ingest data from Microsoft SQL Server. | Data Factory | | DC02 | The Bronze Lakehouse will store raw/original data ingested from Microsoft SQL Server. | Data Engineering | | DC03 | The Silver Lakehouse will store cleansed data sourced from the bronze Lakehouse. | Data Engineering | | DC04 | The Gold Lakehouse will store transformed data sourced from the silver Lakehouse. | Data Engineering | | DC05 | Data cleansing and transformation for the Silver Lakehouse will be performed using Dataflow Gen2, Notebook and Visual Query in the SQL Analytics Endpoint. | Data Engineering | | DC06 | Dataflow Gen2 will be used to move data between Lakehouses (e.g., from bronze to silver or silver to gold). | Data Factory | | DC07 | AutoML from Data Science will be used to demonstrate the Machine Learning Process, and the results will be stored in Gold Lakehouse | Data Science | | DC08 | Data modeling will be implemented in the Semantic Model, using data from the Gold Lakehouse. | Power BI | | DC09 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization. | Power BI | These considerations ensure a layered, modular approach to data management, supporting scalability and advanced analytics. ## 5.2 Workflow Design Decisions UMS shall implement a multi-layered data workflow, leveraging Azure Data Factory for ingestion, Lakehouse architecture for data storage, and Power BI for visualization. The technical architecture is illustrated below: ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_009_img_003.png) Key workflow choices include: - Data originates from the UMS Staging Area (Microsoft SQL Server 2017 Data Warehouse) and is ingested via Azure Data Factory. - Bronze, Silver, and Gold Lakehouse layers provide structured data refinement, ensuring data quality and readiness for analytics. - Dataflow Gen2, Notebooks, and Visual Query tools are employed for cleansing and transformation in the Silver Lakehouse. - AutoML integration enables advanced machine learning, with results stored in the Gold Lakehouse for reporting. - OneLake serves as the unified storage backbone, facilitating seamless connectivity to Power BI for visualization. - The final output is delivered through Power BI, ensuring actionable business intelligence for UMS stakeholders. The workflow design is further supported by the following diagram, which illustrates the end-to-end data processing and analytics pipeline: ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_009_img_003.png) This approach ensures data integrity, traceability, and scalability across the UMS analytics ecosystem. ## 5.3 Access Design Considerations UMS’s access design is based on a layered security approach, balancing usability with compliance and future scalability. Each access method is detailed below: ### Conditional Access UMS evaluated Conditional Access as a primary method for securing Power BI and Fabric environments. However, due to licensing constraints (Entra ID Free, lacking P1/P2), Conditional Access policies cannot be fully enforced at this stage. The design incorporates future readiness for Conditional Access, recommending policy configuration to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage. This ensures that, once licensing is upgraded, UMS can seamlessly activate Conditional Access for granular control. ### Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Given the limitations in Conditional Access eligibility, UMS shall enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level. MFA provides an additional verification layer for all users accessing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI, significantly reducing the risk of unauthorized access and unexpected login prompts. Tenant-level enforcement guarantees consistent application across all users and workloads. ### Private Link Consideration Private Link was evaluated as an access control mechanism to further isolate and secure data access within Azure. However, due to current licensing and technical constraints, Private Link implementation is deferred for future phases. UMS is advised to revisit Private Link integration once Conditional Access policies and advanced licensing are in place, enabling enhanced network security and compliance. ## 5.4 Access Design Decisions UMS’s final access policy decisions are summarized in the table below. These decisions reflect current capabilities and future readiness, ensuring robust security and operational efficiency. | No. | Conditional Access | |------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | DD01 | Configure Policy in Conditional Access to include Power BI Service, Azure Data Explorer, Azure SQL Database, and Azure Storage | | DD02 | Include UMS user inside Conditional Access policy | | DD03 | UMS users go through additional verification, MFA, to reduce unexpected login prompts | | DD04 | Users able to access Microsoft Fabric’s environment | These policies prioritize security, user experience, and future scalability. ## 5.5 Identity and Access Management UMS shall utilize Microsoft Entra ID as the central identity provider for Power BI and Fabric. Entra ID enables unified user management, authentication, and access control, supporting integration with Azure and Microsoft 365 services. MFA enforcement at the tenant level ensures all users undergo additional verification, mitigating risks associated with credential compromise. Limitations include the absence of Entra ID P1/P2 licensing, restricting Conditional Access policy deployment and advanced identity governance features. UMS is advised to monitor licensing requirements and upgrade as necessary to unlock full Conditional Access capabilities and advanced security controls. Operational impact includes streamlined user onboarding, centralized access management, and improved auditability across the analytics environment. ## 5.6 Virtual Machine Size UMS’s Power BI and Fabric workloads require robust virtual machine specifications to ensure optimal performance and reliability. The following table details the recommended VM specifications: | Component | Specification | |------------------|---------------------------------------| | Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) | | .NET Framework | Version 4.8 | | vCPU | 8 cores | | RAM | Minimum 8 GB | These specifications support high-volume data processing, transformation, and visualization tasks, minimizing latency and maximizing throughput. ## 5.7 Resource Organization Design UMS shall implement a structured resource organization model to enhance manageability, traceability, and compliance across Azure and Fabric environments. ### Azure Management Groups Management Groups provide hierarchical organization of Azure resources, enabling policy enforcement, cost management, and access control at scale. UMS is advised to create management groups aligned with business units, workloads, and environments (e.g., staging, production). This facilitates granular governance and simplifies resource lifecycle management. ### Naming and Tagging Consistent naming and tagging are critical for resource discoverability, cost tracking, and operational efficiency. 5.9 Multi-Factor Authentication UMS shall enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) at the tenant level for all Power BI and Fabric users. This decision is driven by the current limitation in Conditional Access eligibility and the need to mitigate risks associated with credential compromise. Tenant-level MFA ensures uniform enforcement, reducing the likelihood of unauthorized access and improving overall security posture. UMS is advised to periodically review MFA adoption rates and user experience, adjusting policies as licensing and technical capabilities evolve. --- This section establishes a robust, scalable, and secure foundation for UMS’s Power BI and Fabric implementation, aligning with best practices and organizational objectives. # 6.0 Security Design and Decision This section details the security architecture and decisions for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), focusing on Azure Network Security Groups (NSG) and encryption protocols for the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) and Microsoft Fabric. ## 6.1 Azure NSG Overview Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) shall implement Azure Network Security Groups (NSG) to enforce granular control over outbound and inbound traffic to critical infrastructure components, particularly the OPDG virtual machine. NSGs are a fundamental Azure security mechanism, providing a stateless firewall at the subnet and NIC level, enabling UMS to define, monitor, and audit network flows in alignment with best practices and compliance requirements. The NSG configuration for UMS is designed to restrict outbound connectivity from the OPDG VM to only essential Microsoft services required for operation, authentication, and monitoring. Each rule is prioritized to ensure that only authorized traffic is permitted, minimizing the attack surface and preventing unauthorized access or data exfiltration. The structure of these rules adheres to Azure’s principle of least privilege, ensuring that only necessary ports and protocols are open, and all other traffic is implicitly denied. Operationally, NSGs provide UMS with visibility and control over network activity, supporting auditability and incident response. The rules are maintained and reviewed periodically to adapt to evolving service endpoints and security requirements. The configuration is tightly integrated with Azure monitoring and logging tools, enabling real-time alerts for anomalous traffic patterns and facilitating rapid remediation. ![](slide_13_security_architecture.png) ## 6.2 Outbound Rules Table The table below summarizes the outbound NSG rules required for secure operation of the OPDG VM. These rules are carefully crafted to ensure connectivity to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, authentication endpoints, and network connectivity testing services. Each rule is assigned a unique priority and source/destination specification to guarantee precise enforcement. | Name | Priority | Source | Source Ports | Destination | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access | |----------------------------|----------|------------------------|--------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------| | Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services | 130 | VM Private IP Addresses| * | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443 | TCP | Allow | | Allow_OPDG_Authentication | 140 | VM Private IP Addresses| * | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443 | TCP | Allow | | Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160 | VM Private IP Addresses| * | *.msftncsi.com | 80 | TCP | Allow | These rules are implemented on the OPDG VM’s subnet NSG. The “Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services” rule enables secure data transfer to Microsoft endpoints via HTTPS, using SSL/TLS encryption. “Allow_OPDG_Authentication” ensures the gateway can perform identity verification and token exchanges with Microsoft Entra ID and associated authentication services. “Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test” allows HTTP connectivity checks to Microsoft’s network status endpoints, facilitating troubleshooting and service availability validation. All other outbound traffic is implicitly denied, ensuring a secure and controlled environment. ## 6.3 Encryption Design UMS shall enforce SSL/TLS encryption for all communications between the OPDG VM and Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication services. This decision is grounded in Azure’s security best practices, which mandate the use of strong encryption protocols to protect data in transit and prevent interception or tampering. For the OPDG, outbound connections to Microsoft endpoints are established exclusively over HTTPS (TCP port 443), leveraging TLS 1.2 or higher. This ensures that sensitive data—including authentication tokens, user credentials, and analytical payloads—are encrypted end-to-end, meeting regulatory requirements and safeguarding against man-in-the-middle attacks. The gateway VM is configured to reject insecure protocols and enforce certificate validation for all outbound sessions. Microsoft Fabric and Power BI natively support SSL/TLS for all API and data interactions. UMS’s implementation ensures that both internal and external data flows are encrypted, with certificate pinning and periodic validation of endpoint certificates. This approach not only protects data confidentiality but also maintains the integrity and authenticity of communications. Operationally, UMS shall monitor SSL/TLS handshake logs and certificate expiration events, integrating these with Azure Sentinel and Security Center for proactive alerting. The encryption design is reviewed quarterly to ensure compliance with evolving standards and to address any vulnerabilities identified in the cryptographic stack. This comprehensive encryption strategy forms the backbone of UMS’s data protection posture within the Azure ecosystem. # 7.0 AutoML Workflow in Microsoft Fabric This section details the end-to-end design of the AutoML and manual machine learning workflows within Microsoft Fabric for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). ## 7.1 Technical Overview UMS shall implement a dual-path machine learning workflow in Microsoft Fabric, leveraging both AutoML and manual ML approaches to maximize flexibility and accuracy. The architecture integrates data engineering, model development, registration, scoring, and visualization components, enabling seamless insights delivery. The following diagram illustrates the technical architecture, highlighting the flow from data sources through machine learning processes to visualization: ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\extraction-output\images\slide_021_img_005.png) The workflow begins with cleaned data in XX_Silver_LK Tables, progresses through AutoML or manual ML experiments, stores outputs in XX_Gold_LK Tables, and culminates in Power BI reporting via OneLake. This design ensures robust lifecycle management and consistent data consumption across all analytics workloads. ## 7.2 Approach/Paths UMS shall support two distinct machine learning paths within Fabric: AutoML and Manual ML. Each path is designed to address specific operational and analytical requirements, providing both automation and customizability. ### AutoML Path The AutoML path enables rapid experimentation and model development by automating key machine learning tasks. Data from XX_Silver_LK Tables is ingested into AutoML Experiments, where Fabric's AutoML engine performs automated feature selection, model selection, and hyperparameter tuning. Scoring is conducted via notebooks, and outputs are stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables for downstream consumption. This approach is ideal for scenarios requiring quick prototyping, baseline models, or non-specialist user engagement. AutoML reduces the technical barrier for UMS personnel, accelerates time-to-insight, and ensures standardized processes. ### Manual ML Path The Manual ML path empowers UMS data scientists to perform advanced feature engineering, algorithm customization, and model optimization using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python). Data from XX_Silver_LK Tables is processed in custom notebooks, enabling granular control over modeling steps. Model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning are executed directly within the notebook environment. Outputs, including trained models and performance metrics, are stored in XX_Gold_LK Tables. This path supports explainability, tailored modeling, and higher accuracy for UMS-specific datasets. Manual ML is recommended for complex use cases or when domain expertise is required to maximize model performance. ## 7.3 Process Flow UMS's machine learning workflow is structured into distinct phases, each with defined technical and operational steps to ensure data integrity, model quality, and lifecycle management. ### Data Preparation Data preparation is performed in the Silver Lakehouse (XX_Silver_LK Tables), where cleaned and pre-processed data is curated for machine learning experimentation. Selection criteria are based on programs from the Staging DB, ensuring only relevant datasets are used. This phase guarantees high-quality input for both AutoML and manual ML paths, minimizing downstream errors and maximizing modeling potential. ### Model Training Model training occurs in Fabric Notebooks. For AutoML, the process is largely automated, with the platform selecting optimal algorithms and parameters. For manual ML, UMS data scientists leverage PySpark or Python to engineer features, tune algorithms, and optimize models. Training, evaluation, and fine-tuning are performed iteratively to achieve explainable and accurate results tailored to UMS's requirements. ### Model Registration Trained models are registered in the Fabric Model Registry. This enables lifecycle management, versioning, and potential reuse across future analytics projects. Registration ensures traceability, governance, and operational consistency, supporting UMS's long-term adoption and scaling of machine learning capabilities. ### Scoring Scoring notebooks apply trained models to new or unseen data, generating predictions, probabilities, and performance metrics. Outputs are stored in the Gold Lakehouse (XX_Gold_LK Tables), providing a standardized repository for all modeled results. This phase supports both batch and real-time inference scenarios, ensuring flexibility in operational deployment. ### Visualization The Gold Lakehouse is connected to Power BI via Direct Lake (OneLake). Semantic models are implemented to structure data for reporting, and Power BI Desktop connects for visualization. This integration enables UMS stakeholders to access actionable insights, monitor model performance, and drive data-driven decision-making across the organization. ## 7.4 Design Decisions The following table summarizes key design considerations and their associated workloads for UMS's AutoML workflow in Microsoft Fabric. The purpose of this table is to document the rationale and workload alignment for each major workflow component. | ID | Description | Workloads Type | |------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------| | DE01 | The cleaned data stored in Silver Lakehouse will serve as the input for all machine learning experimentation. The chosen data will be one of the programs selected from the overall Staging DB. | Data Engineering | | DE02 | The Project will primarily adopt the Manual Machine Learning approach using Fabric Notebooks (PySpark or Python) to enable custom feature engineering, algorithm tuning, and model optimization. | Data Science | | DE03 | Users will perform model training, evaluation, and fine-tuning directly in the Notebook environment to achieve more accurate and explainable results tailored to UMS’s datasets. | Data Science | | DE04 | Trained models will be registered in the Fabric Model Registry to support model lifecycle management, versioning, and potential reuse across further adoption. | Data Science | | DE05 | Scoring Notebooks will be used to apply trained models to new or unseen data. The scored outputs, predictions, probabilities, and performance metrics will be stored in the Gold Lakehouse. | Data Engineering | | DE06 | The Gold Lakehouse will serve as the standardized repository for all modeled or scored results, ensuring consistency between AutoML and manual ML outputs. | Data Engineering | | DE07 | The Semantic Model will connect to the Gold Lakehouse via Direct Lake (OneLake) and Data Modelling will be implemented. | Power BI | | DE08 | Power BI Desktop will connect to the Semantic Model via OneLake for data visualization. | Power BI | These decisions ensure that UMS's machine learning workflow is robust, scalable, and aligned with best practices for data engineering, data science, and business intelligence within Microsoft Fabric. # 8.0 Appendix This appendix provides authoritative reference materials, quota boundaries, and supplementary resources for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s Azure solution design. asset_library.description:
This is a technical architecture diagram.

**Description:**

1. **Fabric-RG (Resource Group):**
   - Represents an Azure Resource Group named "Fabric-RG," which is a logical container for managing Azure resources.
   - Contains the "UMS Fabric" component.

2. **UMS Fabric:**
   - Likely a service or application hosted within the Fabric-RG.
   - Could represent a unified management system or a specific Azure service.

3. **Conditional Access:**
   - A security mechanism applied to control access to the UMS Fabric.
   - Enforces policies based on user identity, device compliance, location, or other conditions.

4. **External User:**
   - Represents an external user or entity attempting to access the UMS Fabric.
   - Access is mediated through the Conditional Access layer.

**Data Flow:**
- The external user initiates a request to access the UMS Fabric.
- The request is routed through the Conditional Access layer, which evaluates the user's credentials and compliance with defined access policies.
- Upon successful validation, the user is granted access to the UMS Fabric resources within the Fabric-RG.

This image is a technical architecture diagram illustrating the authentication and access control flow for Microsoft Fabric using Entra Conditional Access policies.

1. **User Authentication Attempt**:  
   - The process begins with a user attempting to authenticate to Microsoft Fabric.

2. **Entra Conditional Access Policies**:  
   - The authentication request is evaluated against Entra Conditional Access policies.  
   - **Signal (Step 3)**: Signals such as user identity, group membership, and network location are analyzed.  
   - **Decision (Step 4)**: Based on the signals, a decision is made regarding access.  
   - **Enforcement (Step 5)**: The decision is enforced, allowing or denying access based on the policy configuration.

3. **Inbound Access to Microsoft Fabric**:  
   - If access is granted, the user is directed to Microsoft Fabric.  
   - Microsoft Fabric includes various components such as data engineering, data integration, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence tools.  
   - The diagram highlights **OneLake**, a unified data lake within Microsoft Fabric, as a central repository for data storage and management.

This flow ensures secure and conditional access to Microsoft Fabric resources, leveraging Entra's robust policy framework.

This image represents a technical architecture diagram illustrating a data processing and analytics workflow within the Microsoft Azure ecosystem, leveraging UMS Fabric and OneLake for data management and Power BI for visualization.

1. **Data Ingestion**:
   - Data originates from the **UMS Staging Area**, hosted on **Microsoft SQL Server 2017 – Data Warehouse**.
   - The ingestion process is managed by **Azure Data Factory**, which extracts and transfers data into the pipeline.

2. **Bronze Layer**:
   - The ingested raw data is stored in **XX_Bronze_LK Tables**. This layer serves as the initial landing zone for unprocessed data.

3. **Silver Layer**:
   - Data is transformed and refined in the **XX_Silver_LK Tables**. This layer represents cleaned and enriched data, ready for further processing.

4. **Data Science Integration**:
   - The refined data is processed further using **Data Factory** and **Data Science** tools. This step likely involves advanced analytics, machine learning, or predictive modeling.

5. **Gold Layer**:
   - The final, highly curated data is stored in **XX_Gold_LK Tables**. This layer is optimized for reporting and analytics.
   - **Identity_SM** is integrated at this stage, potentially for identity management, security, or metadata tagging.

6. **Data Storage and Connectivity**:
   - The entire workflow is integrated with **OneLake**, a unified data lake for centralized storage and management.
   - The processed data in the Gold Layer is connected to **Power BI** for visualization and reporting, enabling business intelligence insights.

This architecture demonstrates a structured, layered approach to data processing, ensuring data quality, scalability, and seamless integration with analytics tools.

This image is a technical architecture diagram.

The diagram illustrates a data flow between three components:

1. **UMS - Staging_Area - Microsoft SQL Server 2017 - Data Warehouse**:
   - Represents an on-premises SQL Server 2017 database used as a staging area or data warehouse.
   - It stores and processes data before it is transferred to other systems.

2. **On-Premise Data Gateway (OPDG)**:
   - Acts as a bridge between the on-premises SQL Server and cloud-based services.
   - Facilitates secure data transfer without requiring inbound ports to be opened in the on-premises network.
   - Establishes an outbound secure connection to the cloud, ensuring data security and compliance.

3. **UMS Fabric**:
   - Represents a cloud-based service or platform that consumes data from the on-premises SQL Server.
   - The connection to the UMS Fabric is established through the On-Premise Data Gateway.

**Data Flow**:
- Data flows from the SQL Server database to the On-Premise Data Gateway.
- The On-Premise Data Gateway securely transmits the data to the UMS Fabric using an outbound connection.
- The architecture ensures secure communication without exposing the on-premises environment to inbound traffic.

This diagram represents a technical architecture for a machine learning workflow integrated with data storage and visualization components. Below is a detailed description of the data flow and components:

1. **Data Source:**
   - **XX_Silver_LK Tables:** This is the initial data source stored in the UMS Fabric, likely representing pre-processed or intermediate data.

2. **Machine Learning Workflows:**
   - **AutoML Workflow:**
     - Data from the XX_Silver_LK Tables is fed into an **AutoML Experiment**.
     - The AutoML Experiment performs automated machine learning tasks, such as model selection and hyperparameter tuning.
     - The results are scored using a notebook, producing outputs for further processing.
   - **Manual Machine Learning Workflow:**
     - Alternatively, data from the XX_Silver_LK Tables is used for **Manual Machine Learning**.
     - This involves custom modeling using a notebook, allowing for more control over the machine learning process.

3. **Processed Data Storage:**
   - The outputs from both workflows are stored in **XX_Gold_LK Tables**, representing refined or final data ready for consumption.
   - The **Identity_SM** component is also connected to the XX_Gold_LK Tables, possibly for identity management or metadata tagging.

4. **Data Integration and Visualization:**
   - The XX_Gold_LK Tables are connected to **Power BI** via **OneLake**, enabling data visualization and reporting.
   - OneLake serves as a unified data storage layer, facilitating seamless integration between the data and Power BI.

5. **Infrastructure:**
   - The entire workflow operates within the **UMS Fabric**, which provides the underlying infrastructure for data storage, processing, and integration.

This architecture demonstrates a robust pipeline for machine learning and data analytics, leveraging both automated and manual approaches to model development, with seamless integration into a visualization platform for insights delivery.

slides.title: Azure Outbound Security Rules Consideration
slides.notes:
defines the outbound network rules required to allow the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) virtual machine (VM) to securely connect to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and related Microsoft cloud services. These rules ensure that the gateway can authenticate, communicate, and transfer data through HTTPS in compliance with Azure networking best practices.

Rule Name : Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services
Purpose: Enables HTTPS communication from the gateway VM to Microsoft Fabric and Power BI endpoints.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound TCP traffic on port 443 (HTTPS) from VM Private IP Addresses to essential Microsoft domains, including Fabric, Power BI, and Azure core services (e.g., *.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com). It ensures that the gateway can send and receive data securely using SSL/TLS encryption.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Authentication
Purpose: Enables authentication and identity verification with Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD).
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) to identity and login domains such as login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msftauth.net, and login.windows.net. It enables user sign-ins, token validation, and secure connection establishment with Microsoft cloud services.

Rule Name: Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test
Purpose: Supports network connectivity verification for the OPDG service.
Explanation: This rule allows outbound HTTP traffic on port 80 (TCP) to *.msftncsi.com, a Microsoft endpoint used for network connectivity status indication and troubleshooting. It ensures the VM can verify external internet access and service availability.

# UMS Power BI & Fabric Design Review # 2.0 Executive Summary This section provides an overview of the project objectives, the purpose of this Solution Design Document, and identifies the intended audience for review and sign-off. ## 2.1 Project Overview Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) has initiated a strategic project to modernize its data analytics and business intelligence capabilities by leveraging Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. The primary business objective is to design and implement a robust, scalable data workflow that supports the entire lifecycle from data ingestion, transformation, and advanced analytics to visualization. This initiative will enable UMS to derive actionable insights from diverse data sources, enhance data-driven decision-making, and support ongoing digital transformation efforts. The project scope includes: - Establishing a dedicated Microsoft Fabric workspace tailored to UMS’s operational and analytical needs. - Developing an end-to-end ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipeline to automate data integration and transformation processes. - Implementing machine learning processes for advanced analytics and predictive modeling. - Integrating Power BI to deliver interactive dashboards and reports aligned with UMS’s business requirements. - Aligning UMS’s cloud adoption strategy with Microsoft Fabric’s native capabilities, specifically utilizing Fabric Data Factory for orchestration, Fabric Synapse for big data analytics, and Fabric Data Science for machine learning applications. ## 2.2 Document Purpose The purpose of this Solution Design Document is to formally define the technical architecture, design decisions, and implementation approach for the UMS Power BI & Fabric project. The document serves as the authoritative reference for all stakeholders, ensuring a shared understanding of the solution scope, design rationale, and responsibilities. It will guide the implementation, facilitate effective communication, and support governance throughout the project lifecycle. ## 2.3 Document Audience This document is intended for the following audiences: - Enfrasys Solutions project team members and technical consultants. - Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) business stakeholders, project sponsors, and IT management. - UMS technical users, including data engineers, analysts, and system administrators. - Any other parties involved in the review, approval, and implementation of the solution. Both Enfrasys Solutions and Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) are responsible for the review and sign-off of this Solution Design Document to ensure mutual alignment and successful delivery of project objectives. # 3.0 Microsoft Fabric, Power BI & Entra ID Overview This section provides formal definitions and contextual roles for Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Microsoft Entra ID, as adopted in Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s (UMS) data and analytics architecture. ## 3.1 Microsoft Fabric ### Definition Microsoft Fabric is an integrated, end-to-end analytics platform designed to unify data movement, data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence within a single SaaS environment. Fabric consolidates core data workloads, enabling organizations to ingest, store, process, analyze, and visualize data securely and efficiently. The platform leverages a unified data lake (OneLake) and provides native integration with Microsoft security and governance frameworks. ### Core Components | ID | Description | Workload Type | |----|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | Data Factory: Data integration and orchestration for ingesting, transforming, and loading data from diverse sources. | Data Integration | | 2 | Synapse: Advanced analytics and big data processing engine, supporting batch and real-time workloads. | Data Analytics | | 3 | Lakehouse: Unified storage layer for structured and unstructured data, built on open standards (Delta Lake). | Data Storage | | 4 | OneLake: Organization-wide data lake enabling a single source of truth across all analytics workloads. | Data Lake | | 5 | Data Science: Tools and environments for developing, training, and deploying machine learning models. | Machine Learning | | 6 | Model Registry: Centralized repository for managing machine learning models and their lifecycle. | Model Management | Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for the initial deployment, configuration, and enablement of Microsoft Fabric for UMS. Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) shall assume operational ownership and day-to-day management post-handover. ## 3.2 Power BI ### Definition Power BI is Microsoft’s enterprise business intelligence (BI) platform that enables users to visualize, analyze, and share data-driven insights across the organization. Power BI provides interactive dashboards, robust reporting capabilities, and seamless integration with Microsoft Fabric, empowering business users to make informed decisions based on real-time analytics. ### Role in Business Intelligence and Fabric Integration Power BI serves as the primary visualization and analytics layer within the UMS data workflow. It connects natively to Microsoft Fabric workspaces, allowing users to consume curated datasets, build interactive reports, and collaborate securely. This integration ensures that data processed and stored in Fabric (via Data Factory, Synapse, and Lakehouse) is readily accessible for business-aligned analytics and decision-making. Enfrasys Solutions will oversee the integration of Power BI with Fabric, including workspace setup and security configuration. UMS will manage report creation, dataset governance, and user access within Power BI. ## 3.3 Microsoft Entra ID ### Definition Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is a comprehensive cloud-based identity and access management (IAM) solution. Entra ID provides secure authentication, single sign-on (SSO), conditional access policies, and identity governance across Microsoft cloud services, including Fabric and Power BI. ### Role in Authentication and Conditional Access Entra ID underpins secure access to all components of the UMS analytics platform. It authenticates users, enforces conditional access policies, and integrates with UMS’s existing identity governance framework. This ensures that only authorized users can access sensitive data workflows and analytics resources within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI. Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for configuring Entra ID integration and establishing foundational access controls. UMS shall maintain and operate ongoing identity governance, user provisioning, and policy management. --- This section establishes the foundational technologies and their respective operational responsibilities for Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s analytics modernization initiative. # 4.0 Network Design and Decision This section outlines the network architecture and connectivity decisions for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS), focusing on secure hybrid integration between on-premises SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric, including gateway deployment and network security controls. ## 4.1 Network Connectivity Overview Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) requires a robust hybrid network design to enable secure, high-performance connectivity between its on-premises SQL Server 2017 data warehouse and the Microsoft Fabric cloud platform. This design leverages the On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) to facilitate seamless data integration and analytics workflows. ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\python-backend\api_uploads\46645fc0-7de0-482c-a292-bc377db7c983\images\slide_011_img_004.png) *Figure 1: Hybrid connectivity architecture between UMS on-premises SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric via On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG). The OPDG establishes a secure, outbound-only connection, eliminating the need for inbound firewall changes and ensuring data security.* ### Design Considerations | ID | Description | Workload Type | |----|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------| | 1 | Secure, outbound-only connectivity from on-premises to Microsoft Fabric via OPDG | Data Integration | | 2 | No inbound ports required on UMS firewall, reducing attack surface | Security | | 3 | Support for bidirectional data flows between SQL Server and Fabric for analytics and reporting | Data Analytics | | 4 | Compliance with UMS network segmentation and security policies | Security | ### Design Decisions | No. | Design Decision | Decision | |-----|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------| | 1 | Use On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG) as the bridge between UMS SQL Server and Microsoft Fabric | Approved | | 2 | Deploy OPDG in a dedicated, hardened VM within UMS’s secure network segment | Approved | | 3 | Enforce outbound-only HTTPS traffic for all gateway communications | Approved | | 4 | Conditional Access enforced for all users accessing Microsoft Fabric resources | Approved | ### Task/Migration Rollout | Item | Activities | Action By | Status | |---------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------|----------| | Network Readiness | Ensure on-premises firewall and routing permit required outbound connections | UMS | Pending | | Gateway VM Provisioning | Deploy and configure VM for OPDG installation | UMS | Pending | | OPDG Installation & Configuration | Install and configure On-Premises Data Gateway software on provisioned VM | Enfrasys Solutions | Pending | | Connectivity Testing | Validate secure connectivity between SQL Server, OPDG, and Microsoft Fabric | Enfrasys Solutions | Pending | | User Access Policy Enforcement | Implement Conditional Access policies for Fabric access | UMS | Pending | ## 4.2 Microsoft Fabric Data Gateway (On-Premises Data Gateway - OPDG) ### Technology Definition **Microsoft On-Premises Data Gateway (OPDG)** is a secure, lightweight agent that enables organizations to bridge their on-premises data sources (such as SQL Server) with Microsoft cloud services, including Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and Azure Logic Apps. The gateway establishes secure outbound connections to the cloud, allowing data to flow securely without requiring inbound firewall changes, thus minimizing security risks and simplifying hybrid integration. ### OPDG VM Specifications Enfrasys Solutions recommends the following minimum specifications for the OPDG virtual machine, in accordance with Microsoft best practices and UMS workload requirements: | Component | Specification | |-------------------|------------------------------------------------| | Operating System | Windows 10 (64-bit) or Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) | | .NET Framework | Version 4.8 | | vCPU | 8 cores | | RAM | Minimum 8 GB | ### Network Security Group (NSG) Rules To ensure secure and reliable operation of the OPDG, the following outbound NSG rules must be applied to the gateway VM subnet. These rules are designed to allow only the necessary outbound traffic to Microsoft Fabric, Power BI, and authentication endpoints. | Name | Priority | Source | Source Ports | Destination | Destination Ports | Protocol | Access | |-----------------------------|----------|-----------------------|--------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------|----------|--------| | Allow_OPDG_HTTPS_Services | 130 | VM Private IP Addresses | * | *.servicebus.windows.net, *.download.microsoft.com, *.core.windows.net, *.frontend.clouddatahub.net, *.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com, *.datawarehouse.fabric.microsoft.com, *.powerbi.com, *.analysis.windows.net | 443 | TCP | Allow | | Allow_OPDG_Authentication | 140 | VM Private IP Addresses | * | *.login.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com, aadcdn.msauth.net, aadcdn.msftauth.net, *.microsoftonline-p.com, login.live.com | 443 | TCP | Allow | | Allow_OPDG_Connectivity_Test| 160 | VM Private IP Addresses | * | *.msftncsi.com | 80 | TCP | Allow | ### Responsibility Assignment - **Enfrasys Solutions** shall be responsible for the installation, configuration, and validation of the On-Premises Data Gateway on the designated VM. - **Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS)** shall be responsible for ensuring on-premises network readiness, including provisioning the VM, configuring outbound firewall rules, and ensuring network connectivity to Microsoft endpoints. ### Architecture Diagram ![](C:\Users\MuhammadIdzhansKhair\OneDrive - ENFRASYS\Documents\project-internal\design-document-converter\python-backend\api_uploads\46645fc0-7de0-482c-a292-bc377db7c983\images\slide_011_img_004.png) *Figure 2: Data flow between UMS on-premises SQL Server, OPDG, and Microsoft Fabric. The OPDG initiates outbound connections, supporting secure hybrid data integration.* --- This network design ensures that Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS) achieves secure, scalable, and compliant hybrid connectivity between its on-premises data warehouse and Microsoft Fabric, with clear division of responsibilities and adherence to Microsoft’s latest best practices. # 5.0 Roles in Microsoft Fabric & Power BI This section outlines the role-based access model for Microsoft Fabric and Power BI as designed for Universiti Malaysia Sabah (UMS). The following subsections define key administrative and workspace roles, their responsibilities, and the mapping of these roles to UMS personnel. ## 5.1 Microsoft Fabric Administrator Role Microsoft Fabric is an integrated analytics platform provided by Microsoft that unifies data engineering, data science, real-time analytics, and business intelligence (BI) under a single SaaS offering. It enables organizations to ingest, transform, analyze, and visualize data using a suite of collaborative tools. The Fabric Administrator is a critical role responsible for the governance, configuration, and security of the Fabric environment. Administrators oversee workspace creation, permissions management, data governance policies, and audit activities. They ensure compliance with Universiti Malaysia Sabah’s data security and operational requirements. Enfrasys Solutions recommends that UMS assign experienced personnel to the Fabric Administrator role to maintain platform integrity and operational continuity. The table below summarizes the core administrator capabilities within Microsoft Fabric and Power BI: | Capability | Description | |---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Workspace Management | Create, delete, and configure workspaces; manage workspace settings. | | User & Role Assignment | Assign and revoke workspace roles; manage user access and permissions. | | Data Governance | Implement and enforce data retention, privacy, and compliance policies. | | Audit & Monitoring | Review activity logs, monitor usage, and ensure compliance with UMS security standards. | | Resource Allocation | Manage and allocate capacity, storage, and compute resources across the Fabric environment. | ## 5.2 Fabric-native Roles Microsoft Fabric and Power BI employ a role-based access control (RBAC) model to assign permissions according to user responsibilities. The following are the primary Fabric-native roles: - **Contributor**: Users with this role can create, modify, and delete content within the workspace, such as datasets, reports, and dashboards. Contributors cannot manage workspace-level security or settings. - **Viewer**: Viewers have read-only access to content within the workspace. They can consume reports and dashboards but cannot make any modifications. - **Data Engineer**: Data Engineers are responsible for designing, building, and maintaining data pipelines, datasets, and transformations. They typically require Contributor or Admin permissions. - **Data Analyst**: Data Analysts focus on interpreting data, building reports, and providing insights. They generally require Contributor or Viewer permissions depending on their involvement in content creation. The following table details workspace-level role capabilities in Microsoft Fabric: | Permission | Admin | Contributor | Member | Viewer | |---------------------------|-------|-------------|--------|--------| | Manage Workspace Settings | Yes | No | No | No | | Add/Edit Content | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | | View Content | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Assign Roles | Yes | No | No | No | | Delete Workspace | Yes | No | No | No | ## 5.3 UMS Workspace Role and Access Enfrasys Solutions is responsible for mapping the required Microsoft Fabric and Power BI roles to the operational requirements of Universiti Malaysia Sabah. UMS is responsible for assigning the mapped roles to appropriate personnel. The table below presents the recommended workspace role mapping for UMS, including the suggested role and designated UMS Person-In-Charge (PIC): | No. | Workspace Role | Suggested Role | UMS PIC | |-----|----------------------|-----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | 1 | Workspace Admin | Fabric Admin / Data Engineer | Dr Suraya Binti Alias
